Emergency Name: Following Israeli strikes and evacuation orders in southern Lebanon and Beirut, over 30,000 people – mostly Syrians, but also some Lebanese – have crossed into Syria. More arrivals are likely as a ground invasion starts and Israeli attacks accelerate.

Heavy rain triggered floods across parts of N’Djamena (central-western Chad) on 20 August, resulting in fatalities and displacement. According to the latest report from the International Organisation for Migration (IOM), 3 people died and at least 1,502 have been displaced. About 230 shelters have been destroyed by flooding. The primary needs of those affected include food, shelter, non-food items.

This emergency has been ongoing. Due to insufficient data and limitations around classifying natural disasters with the current Classification System, this classification is delayed. Over 220 people have died and 5 million have been affected across 21 districts by the monsoon floods that began earlier this month. Floods and landslides have damaged roads and vital infrastructure leaving hundreds of thousands stranded and without power and electricity in particular in Gaibandha and Bandarban districts. It is estimated that 5,302,698 people are directly affected by the current flooding.

Heavy rains and flooding in most of Sudan have left a trail of destruction, including damages to homes, infrastructure, croplands and other livelihoods. Over 500 km2 of land appears to be flooded in Khartoum, Al Gezira and White Nile states alone, according to satellite images. Meanwhile, river levels continue to rise and more than 506,000 people have already been affected in 17 of the country’s 18 states, according to data from the Government’s Humanitarian Aid Commission (HAC). Nearly 100 people died due to floods and landslides, and another 46 people have been injured.
As of August 30, 281,407 people have been affected by consecutive flooding. Heavy rainfall has been recorded in seven regions of the country since July. More than 26,000 houses have collapsed.

On the night of Thursday, September 10, 2020, around 10 p.m. on the Djibo-Namsiguiya axis toward Mentao, an Unidentified Armed Group (GANI) attacked a military convoy. During this attack, 4 SDF were killed and 3 injured, the exchanges of fire continued until the morning of Friday September 11th in the area of Koubel Alpha. The attack forced around 1,200 people to flee fled  towards Djibo and Silgueye. The displacements were still ongoing as of September 18th.

Half of the population of the Nagorno-Karabakh region have been displaced since fighting erupted last week between Armenian separatists and Azerbaijan, rebel officials said on Wednesday. The two sides have been in conflict for decades over the ethnically Armenian region of Azerbaijan, which broke away from Baku in the 1990s. Nearly 290 people have been killed since the most recent clashes erupted, including 47 civilians.

Heavy rain triggered floods across parts of N’Djamena (central-western Chad) on 20 August, resulting in fatalities and displacement. According to the latest ACAPS report, around 10,000 shelters have been destroyed by flooding. The primary needs of those affected include food, shelter, non-food items. This emergency was previously classified at a Yellow 1.

On 1 November 2020, Super Typhoon Goni, the most powerful tropical cyclone in the world this year brought torrential rains, violent winds, mudslides, and storm surges to Luzon. The typhoon, locally known as Rolly, left extensive destruction and damage in its path, killing at least 25 people, injuring 399, and affecting 2 million people in 8 of the country’s 17 regions. Category-4 Typhoon Vamco (Ulysses) then swept through central Luzon on 11 and 12 November, flooding tens of thousands of homes and causing floods and landslides.

Tropical Cyclone Gati made landfall in Somalia on 22 November, primarily impacting 13 villages in Iskushuban district, Bari region, Puntland State. Reports indicate approximately 42,000 displaced and 8 deaths.

With the fighting between Ethiopian federal forces (EDF) and Tigray regional security forces,  reports of casualties are being heard through various media outlets. Telephone lines are still cut making information flow and corroboration of media reports very difficult for the humanitarian community, as well as to monitor population movement and additional humanitarian needs. Meanwhile, banks are reportedly closed, and transport is not allowed to and from Tigray as a result of which shortages of basic commodities are reportedly appearing, impacting the most vulnerable first and the most. Unofficial UN estimates shared in country indicate 900,000 displacements. Additionally, nearly 10,000 people have already crossed the border into Sudan

Heavy fighting in southern Afghanistan, near Lashkargah city in Hilmand province continues affecting thousands of people. Fighting that started on 11 October between the Afghan National Security Forces (ANSF) and a nonstate armed group (NSAG) has carried on for several days near Lashkargah city in Hilmand province.

Burkina Faso has been facing a humanitarian emergency due to ongoing escalation of violence. Over the past 3 months, more than 21,000 displacements have occurred due to violence throughout the Sahel including Djibo, Markoye, and Déou.

Since voluntary repatriation (VolRep) convoys have recommenced following the election period in July in Burundi, both Rwanda and DRCongo have sent VolRep convoys to Burundi, increasing the number of repatriated Burundians from one to two convoys a week to now four to five. These factors have required that more transit centers remain ready to host convoys while increasing transport, staff and supply needs to ensure continued protection and center management services ensured by the IRC as UNHCR’s implementing partner.

Hurricanes Eta and Iota both made landfall in Central America in November, causing major damage. Figures indicate 60 deaths and over 6,000 displaced in Guatemala due to the destruction of their homes.
Hurricanes Eta and Iota both made landfall in Central America in November, causing major damage. Figures indicate 98 deaths and over 11,000 displacements due to destroyed homes

Fighting between federal and local forces in northern Ethiopia is causing an influx of refugees crossing  into Sudan, as well as internal displacements. To date, over 48,000 people have crossed the border but this number is expected to rise as conflict continues inside Ethiopia.

Ongoing conflict between the Myanmar Armed Forces (MAF) and the Arakan Army (AA) continues unabated. In Rakhine State, Tatmadaw is conducting clearance operations and hundreds of houses were burnt down with thousands of  families displaced. Figures indicate approximately 18,600 displacements over the past 90 days.

Since mid-October 2020 the Government of Iraq has forced the closure and reclassification of a total of 15 IDP camps and informal sites in Iraq (11 formal camps closed, 2 informal sites closed, 2 formal camps reclassified to informal sites). 33,830 individuals have been forced to depart from these sites, verbal notice was given to residents only a few days in advance.

Fighting between federal and local forces in northern Ethiopia is causing an influx of refugees crossing into Sudan, as well as internal displacements. Planning figures suggest the total number of refugees could reach 100,000 in the coming months, though only half that number have arrived to date. This new crisis comes as Sudan is facing a major economic crisis that is driving widespread food insecurity with projections of almost 1.3 million at IPC 4.

Conflict in Cabo Delgado, drought in the south, disease outbreaks and COVID-19 are compounding already significant humanitarian needs in Mozambique. Since October 277,000 people from the northern province of Cabo Delgado have been displaced according to government estimates announced last week. After fleeing violence from ongoing attacks by armed groups and military actions from Mozambican forces, these displaced people now face serious health risks and inadequate living conditions.

Emergency (IPC Phase 4) outcomes are likely in 35 counties, with areas of greatest concern concentrated in Jonglei, Unity, Lakes, Warrap, and Upper Nile. Due to the impact of devastating floods, conflict, and worsening macroeconomic conditions, most households are still facing large food consumption gaps or using extreme livelihoods coping strategies to mitigate them. Catastrophe (IPC Phase 5) is likely among households in Jonglei and Greater Pibor Administrative Area, where recent conflict and two consecutive years of severe floods are exhausting coping capacity.

Over 1.7 million people are estimated to be in IPC Phase 4 (Emergency) in 35 counties, with areas of greatest concern concentrated in Jonglei, Unity, Lakes, Warrap, and Upper Nile. Due to the impact of devastating floods, conflict, and worsening macroeconomic conditions, most households are still facing large food consumption gaps or using extreme livelihoods coping strategies to mitigate them. It is estimated that 11,000 are in IPC Phase 5 (Famine) and is likely among households where recent conflict and two consecutive years of severe floods are exhausting coping capacity.

Due to attacks by ADF (Allied Democratic Force) forces in the villages of Lume, Nzenga, Mutwanga and Loselose, displacement of the population is estimated at around 3,406 households. Those displaced are starting to settle in schools and other public buildings (churches, etc.) in Beni, North Kivu.

Violence and insecurity surrounding the 27 December general election in the Central African Republic (CAR) has forced over 30,000 people to flee into neighboring Cameroon, Chad, the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), and the Republic of the Congo. 62,000 remain internally displaced since the start of the conflict.

Burkina Faso has been facing a humanitarian emergency due to ongoing escalation of violence. Over the past 3 months, more than 25,000 displacements have occurred due to violence throughout Burkina Faso’s Sahel region.

With the fighting between Ethiopian federal forces (EDF) and Tigray regional security forces,  reports of casualties are being heard through various media outlets. Telephone lines are still cut making information flow and corroboration of media reports very difficult for the humanitarian community, as well as to monitor population movement and additional humanitarian needs. Meanwhile, banks are reportedly closed, and transport is not allowed to and from Tigray as a result of which shortages of basic commodities are reportedly appearing, impacting the most vulnerable first and the most. Approximately 2,200,000 people have been displaced due to the conflict with over 50,000 fleeing to Sudan

Ongoing conflict between the Myanmar Armed Forces (MAF) and the Arakan Army (AA) continues unabated. In Rakhine State, Tatmadaw is conducting clearance operations and hundreds of houses were burnt down with thousands of  families displaced.

Due to the intensified armed clashes started from early December 2020, more than 4,700 people were displaced in Kyaukme and Namtu area. As of 14 January 2021, some IDPs in Namtu went back to their home even though their safety are still at high risk and more than 3000 people are still displaced in Kayukme. The armed clashes continue in Norther Shan and it is highly possible that more families will be displaced in the near future.

Since mid-October 2020 the Government of Iraq has forced the closure and reclassification of a total of IDP camps and informal sites. Residents have been forced to depart from these sites often with verbal notice given only a few days in advance. Over 32,000 have had to depart due to closures over the past 90 days. Many departing IDPs indicated to protection partners that they cannot return to areas of origin and do not have other options for safe, voluntary, and dignified resettlement.

Conflict in Cabo Delgado, drought in the south, disease outbreaks and COVID-19 are compounding already significant humanitarian needs in Mozambique. By now, over 565,000 people from the northern province of Cabo Delgado have been displaced. After fleeing violence from ongoing attacks by armed groups and military actions from Mozambican forces, these displaced people now face serious health risks and inadequate living conditions.

Tropical Cyclone Eloise made landfall on 23 January, preliminary reports indicate that nearly 7,000 people have been displaced and more than 5,000 houses have been destroyed, damaged or flooded, mainly in Buzi, Dondo, Nhamatanda and Beira City, according to the preliminary data from the Government. These numbers could rise in the days ahead as the full extent of the damage becomes known.

Fighting between federal and local forces in northern Ethiopia is causing an influx of refugees crossing  into Sudan, as well as internal displacements. Planning figures suggest the total number of refugees could reach 100,000 in the coming months, though only half that number have arrived to date. This new crisis comes as Sudan is facing a major economic crisis that is driving widespread food insecurity and renewed conflict in the Darfur region.

Over 1.7 million people are estimated to be in IPC Phase 4 (Emergency) in 35 counties, with areas of greatest concern concentrated in Jonglei, Unity, Lakes, Warrap, and Upper Nile. Due to the impact of devastating floods, conflict, and worsening macroeconomic conditions, most households are still facing large food consumption gaps or using extreme livelihoods coping strategies to mitigate them. It is estimated that 11,000 are in IPC Phase 5 (Famine) and is likely among households where recent conflict and two consecutive years of severe floods are exhausting coping capacity.

Violence and insecurity surrounding the 27 December general election in the Central African Republic (CAR) has forced people to flee into neighboring Cameroon, Chad, the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), and the Republic of the Congo. 117,000 remain internally displaced since the start of the conflict. Additionally, over 400,000 people face IPC 4 levels of food insecurity.

On 7 February Congolese authorities confirmed 1 death in Butembo, North Kivu of a women who was suffering from Ebola since Feb 3rd. She was a wife of a survivor of the previous Ebola outbreak in North Kivu. Investigation is ongoing with a list of 70 contacts.
A new Ebola outbreak has been declared in Guinea. Five people have now died and there are several more suspected and confirmed cases.

On January 15th, in one of the Krinding IDP camps in West Darfur, an incidence of violence triggered intercommunal clashes between the two communities and forced nearly 4,482 Sudanese people to cross the border into Chad. The Chadian authorities require all new arrivals spend one week quarantining in Adre transit center, in Ouddai province. After the quarantine period, they are relocated to Kouchaguine-Moura camp.

Conflict and food insecurity are now affecting several areas of Ethiopia. Almost 2.6 million people are at IPC 4 levels of food insecurity. While international attention is focused on Tigray, conflict is also affecting civilians in Benishangul-Gumuz and Oromia regions.

Due to the intensified armed clashes started from early December 2020 between RCSS and TNLA, more than 5400 have been displaced. Furthermore, armed clashes between KIA and Tatmadaw continue in the area.

Due to attacks by ADF (Allied Democratic Force) forces in the villages of Lume, Nzenga, Mutwanga and Loselose, displacement of the population estimated at around 11,000 households. Those displaced are starting to settle in schools and other public buildings (churches, etc.) to the town in Beni, in the North East of the province of North Kivu.

Persistent violence, particularly linked to criminal and communal armed groups, that has been growing in North West and North Central since 2011 has now displaced over 720,000 people. Over the past 90 days, DTM has reported over 400 injuries, 500 deaths, and 4,100 displaced.

Conflict in Cabo Delgado, drought in the south, disease outbreaks and COVID-19 are compounding already significant humanitarian needs in Mozambique. 150,000 people from the northern province of Cabo Delgado have been displaced in the past 90 days. After fleeing violence from ongoing attacks by armed groups and military actions from Mozambican forces, these displaced people now face serious health risks and inadequate living conditions.

Tropical Cyclone Eloise made landfall on 23 January. Over 43,000 people have been displaced and more than 5,000 houses have been destroyed, damaged or flooded, mainly in Buzi, Dondo, Nhamatanda and Beira City, according to the preliminary data from the Government.

Fighting between federal and local forces in northern Ethiopia is causing an influx of refugees crossing into Sudan, as well as internal displacements. Over 12,000 new refugee arrivals have been reported over the past 90 days, for a total of over 60,000 refugees.
Over 440,000 Venezuelans are now in Ecuador, of which 27,870 arrived in the last 90 days.

Clashes erupted on 16 January 2021 in the Krinding area of Ag Geneina town, West Darfur. Approximately 116,800 displacement, 407 injuries, and 152 deaths have been reported due to the inter-communal conflict between Masalit and Arab tribes. Based on a DTM assessment, the three main priority needs for the displaced  in Ag Geneina town are food, WASH and education.

Widespread protests due to a now shelved tax reform have led to demonstrations across the country. Over 800 injuries and 19 deaths have been reported.  People continue to protest due to the use of force, rising poverty, unemployment, and inequality which has worsened with the arrival of the coronavirus last year.

Violence and insecurity surrounding the 27 December general election in the Central African Republic (CAR) has forced people to flee into neighboring Cameroon, Chad, the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), and the Republic of the Congo. 177,000 remain displaced since the start of the conflict.  Additionally, over 400,000 people face IPC 4 levels of food insecurity.

A major fire broke out at Kutupalong Balukhali Extension camp on 22 March 2021. Around 88,000 people have been affected, including 45,000 who are displaced from their homes. Three health centers that served both Rohingya refugees and the local host community also burned down.

The ongoing political crisis has added an additional complexity to the multiple conflicts throughout the country. 7,120 people have been displaced by conflict and insecurity in Kayin State and Bago Region since late December 2020. In Myikyina Township, at least 150 people, including IDPs in camps, were displaced due to hostilities between the MAF and KIA on 15 March. 760 people from five villages in Injangyang Township were also displaced, fleeing mostly to the neighboring jungle. Additionally, over 2,570 people lost their shelters after a fire destroyed more than 660 houses on 17 March in the Tein Nyo in Rakhine State.

About 3,000 villagers from Myanmar’s southeastern Karen state fled to Thailand on Sunday following air attacks by the army on an area held by an ethnic armed group. Myanmar’s military launched air strikes on five areas in Mutraw district, near the border, including a displacement camp.

Since the start of 2021, armed groups in Niger have increased the scale and frequency of their attacks on civilians in Niger's border areas with Mali and Burkina Faso. Hundreds of people have been killed and nearly 13,000 displaced in a series of incidents that have triggered fears of retaliatory attacks along ethnic lines.

Since the start of 2021, armed groups in Niger have increased the scale and frequency of their attacks on civilians in Niger's border areas with Mali and Burkina Faso. Hundreds of people have been killed and nearly 13,000 displaced in a series of incidents that have triggered fears of retaliatory attacks along ethnic lines.

Water shortages are reported in 34 districts of Somalia with pockets of pre-drought conditions seen in some areas. The worst affected districts are in Somaliland and Puntland, as well as Galmudug, Hirshabelle and Jubaland.  Conflict-based displacements due to military offensive have also been reported, bringing the total to 112,000 internal displacements since January.

Armed groups overran the northern town of Palma in Cabo Delgado in March 2021 in the latest escalation in a conflict that had already displaced an estimated 670,000 people. Over 16,000 new IDP arrivals have been registered but even more people are reportedly sheltering near Palma, though the situation may become clearer in the coming weeks now that Mozambican security forces are back in control of the town.

Armed groups have attacked Damasak multiple times in the previous weeks, displacing the vast majority of the town's population. Reports indicate approximately 65,000 have been displaced.  Humanitarian offices and facilities including warehouses have been deliberately targeted during the attacks on the town.

Conflict and food insecurity are now affecting several areas of Ethiopia. Almost 2.6 million people are at IPC 4 levels of food insecurity while new deaths and displacements continue to be recorded. While international attention is focused on Tigray, conflict is also affecting civilians in Benishangul-Gumuz and Oromia regions.

The protracted armed conflict between government forces and non-state armed actors (Al Shabab) in Baadweyne town, northeast of Gaalkacyo resulted in civilian casualties and displacement since the beginning of this year, but the humanitarian situation worsened on 15 April 2021 when the Somalia National Army retreated from defense positions creating a power vacuum. The loss of this town has triggered approximately 6,891 displacements. Life-saving activities in this area, particularly Health, Nutrition, and Food Security interventions remain temporarily suspended since 15 April, while humanitarian staff fled the town due to security concerns.

Violence and insecurity surrounding the 27 December general election in the Central African Republic (CAR) have forced people to flee into neighboring Cameroon, Chad, the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), and the Republic of the Congo. 33,000 displacement have been recorded for the month of March. Additionally, over 632,000 people are estimated to be at IPC 4 levels of food insecurity.

The ongoing political crisis has added an additional complexity to the multiple conflicts throughout the country. Over 21000 displacements  have been recorded for the past three months in Northern Shan State, Kachin State, and South East Myanmar.

About 3,300 people from Myanmar’s southeastern state fled to Thailand following air attacks by the army on an area held by an ethnic armed group. Myanmar’s military launched air strikes on five areas in Mutraw district, near the border, including a displacement camp. Reports estimate 7,000 to 8,000 are still likely to cross into Thailand.

At least 119 people have been killed and hundreds more wounded since 10 May in the most intense conflict in Gaza since the 2014 war. Israeli airstrikes have also destroyed residences and infrastructure, displacing an estimated 1,750 people, though this number is likely to increase if military operations continue. We will continue to monitor the situation and update this classification as necessary.

150,000 people  from Geidam town and 30,000 people from Kanama town have been displaced and those in rural communities have been rendered vulnerable overnight following multiple armed attacks by suspected Non-State Armed Groups (NSAGs). The displaced and the host communities are in need of food, non-food items (NFI), shelter, WASH, health, nutrition and protection services.

The arrival of migrants at the Northern Border in Mexico continue to pose a challenge to NGOs with a marked increase in CBP enforcement actions since March as well as the increase in security risks on the Mexico side of the border.

At least 227 people have been killed and over 1,600 wounded since 10 May in the most intense conflict in Gaza since the 2014 war. Israeli airstrikes have also destroyed residences and infrastructure, displacing an estimated 75,000 people, though this number is likely to increase if military operations continue. We will continue to monitor the situation and update this classification as necessary.

Mount Nyiragono started to erupt on 22 May. Thousands of people fled their homes in nearby Goma, capital of North Kivu province, though the lava flows stopped just short of the city itself. This is a rapidly evolving situation and we will continue to track it closely. Please note that the severity score may be revised downwards if people are able to return to their homes.

Mount Nyiragono started to erupt on 22 May. Thousands of people fled their homes in nearby Goma, capital of North Kivu province, though the lava flows stopped just short of the city itself. Thousands have already been displaced, and evacuations are currently underway due to the risk of another eruption, but there is not yet sufficient data to factor in these latest displacements here. This is a rapidly evolving situation, and we will continue to track it closely and reclassify as data becomes available and clear.

Increased conflict in Mozambique's northern Cabo Delgado province since March 24, particularly in Palma district, has displaced over 67,000 people. The violence in Palma is part of a broader intensification in Cabo Delgado, exacerbating humanitarian needs in a region that has experienced a violent insurgency since July 2017.

Since the start of 2021, armed groups in Niger have increased the scale and frequency of their attacks on civilians in Niger's border areas with Mali and Burkina Faso. Hundreds of people have been killed and over 11,000 displaced over the past 90 days in a series of incidents that have triggered fears of retaliatory attacks along ethnic lines.

277,000 people have been forced to leave their homes since the start of April, most due to intensified conflict following a new military offensive. At the same time, Somalia declared a drought on 25 April, and loss of livelihoods has forced 19,000 people to leave their homes.

The ongoing political crisis has added an additional complexity to the multiple conflicts throughout the country. Over 80000 new displacements have been recorded for the past three months in Northern Shan State, Kachin State, Chin State and South East Myanmar with a large proportion occurring at the end of May.

Conflict in the southern Helmand and eastern Laghman provinces in Afghanistan has intensified since early May. The fighting has resulted in an estimated 19,500 displacements as well as numerous fatalities and injuries.

Western and central Afghanistan are experiencing a severe dry spell, leading to low water levels and reserves as well as early indications of agricultural drought. Nearly 11 million people in Afghanistan are experiencing high levels of food insecurity, which could be exacerbated by continued drought.

Owing to low water levels in the Euphrates River, millions of people in northern Syria are facing severe potential shortages of clean water. While dam closures will have an impact on electricity supply, the scarcity of clean water is already leading to health and other concerns in the region, including suspected outbreaks of acute watery diarrhea (AWD) affecting more than 12,000 people thus far.

Criminal banditry and armed violence are spreading in Nigeria's North West region. This, in combination with communal tensions, has resulted in numerous deaths, including 88 people in once incident in Kebbi state earlier this month. At least 7,000 people have also been displaced since May.

Following the initial displacements and evacuations after the Mount Nyiragongo eruption on 22 May and ensuing seismic activity, affected populations have begun returning to Goma and surrounding areas. However, many people remain displaced and humanitarian needs continue. Returns are occurring fast, and reclassifications will be issued as updated data becomes available.
On June 4 and 5, armed actors carried out attacks in Yagha province, killing 174 people and injuring several others. As a result, over 13,000 people have been displaced, many of them in Sebba.

The violence and insecurity that followed the 27 December general election in the Central African Republic (CAR) has displaced thousands of people, including more than 57,000 people since April. While the overall trend of new displacements is declining and returns are occurring, insecurity and protection issues persist. The food insecurity situation remains concerning, with almost 2.3 million people in IPC 3 and over 632,000 in IPC 4, though these figures remain below the classification threshold for major food insecurity.

Conflict and food insecurity are now affecting several areas of Ethiopia. More than 5.5 million people are at IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity in Tigray and neighboring areas (including over 350,000 people in IPC Phase 5). New deaths and displacements continue to be recorded across the country, with over 523,000 displacements recorded since late March. While international attention is focused on Tigray, conflict is also affecting civilians in other regions including Benishangul-Gumuz, Amhara and Oromia.

Madagascar is suffering its worst drought in 40 years along with other environmental factors, insecurity, and the impact of COVID-19. In the country's Grand Sud, nearly 1.4 million people are food insecure (with almost 14,000 at IPC 5 level). Needs are expected to increase as the lean season approaches, particularly as this year's harvest season is expected to be significantly reduced. IPC 3+ levels across the country remain below the threshold for "major food insecurity" in the classification system, and we continue to monitor the situation.

Haiti is experiencing a political crisis amid ongoing insecurity in the Port-au-Prince metropolitan area. Following protests and calls for his resignation, President Jovenel Moïse was assassinated by unknown actors on 7 July. At the same time, the security situation has deteriorated, with violence by criminal gangs and other armed actors displacing at least 14,700 people since June 1. While the impact of the events of 7 July remains unclear, both Moïse's assassination and continuing insecurity threaten to deepen the crisis in Haiti.

Western and central Afghanistan are experiencing a severe dry spell, leading to low water levels and reserves as well as early indications of agricultural drought. The government declared a national drought on 23 June, with 80% of the country now classified as being in either severe or serious drought status. Over 81,000 people have been displaced as a result of the drought, and more than 9 million people in Afghanistan are experiencing high levels of food insecurity, which could be exacerbated by continued drought.

The ongoing political crisis in Myanmar has added an additional complexity to the multiple conflicts throughout the country. Over 150,000 new displacements have been recorded since late April in Northern and Southern Shan States, Kachin State, Chin State and South East Myanmar.

The arrival of migrants at the Northern Border in Mexico continue to pose a challenge to NGOs with a marked increase in CBP enforcement actions since March as well as the increase in security risks on the Mexico side of the border. Please note that due to delays in reporting, this classification likely undercounts arrivals at the border.

Conflict in the Baghdis, Helmand, Herat, Khost, Kuner, Laghman, Nangarhar, and Paktya provinces in Afghanistan has intensified since early May as government forces, the Taliban, and armed actors battle for control of various districts. The fighting has resulted in almost 97,000 displacements as well as numerous fatalities and injuries.

Large-scale attacks by non-state armed actors are driving increased humanitarian needs in Burkina Faso's Sahel region, including a June 4 attack in Solhan (covered in the previous classification). Since then, more than 18,000 people have been displaced due to violence in the region.

Heavy rainfall since May has caused flooding, affecting over 22,000 people in Aweil South. 834 suspected cases of malaria and 24 suspected cases of acute watery diarrhoea (AWD) have also been reported. Due to violence, flooding, and the impacts of COVID-19, famine levels of food insecurity are present in Aweil South, while flooding has impacted multiple systems.

Owing to low water levels in the Euphrates River, millions of people in northern Syria are facing severe potential shortages of clean water. While dam closures will have an impact on electricity supply, the scarcity of clean water is already leading to health and other concerns in the region, including suspected outbreaks of acute watery diarrhea (AWD) and acute bloody diarrhea (ABD) affecting more than 17,000 people in the Northeast thus far.

The onset of the rainy season in Nigeria has caused flooding in the North East. Since mid-July, Adamawa, Borno and Yobe states in Nigeria have seen suspected outbreaks of acute watery diarrhoea (AWD) and cholera. Thus far, 45 cases of cholera have been confirmed, with another 50 cases of AWD and cholera suspected. Continued flooding, which has displaced 200 people and affected over 24,000 thus far, could create conditions for further spread of water-borne illness.

Heavy monsoon rains since late July in Bangladesh have caused flash floods and landslides in Cox's Bazar, and more heavy rainfall is expected. 21 people have been killed and 21,000 refugees have been displaced with almost 65,000 people affected in total thus far.

Since July, at least 15,000 migrants have been stranded in Necoclí as they wait for transit to continue their passage north through the Darién Gap and Panama toward the United States. Assessments have identified protection and WASH needs as key. COVID-19 restrictions in the US and irregular boat departures from Necoclí amid increased migrant flows have strained authorities' capacity to meet needs.

Madagascar is suffering its worst drought in 40 years along with other environmental factors, insecurity, and the impact of COVID-19. In the country's Grand Sud, nearly 1.4 million people are food insecure (with almost 14,000 at IPC 5 level). Impacts on health,  subsistence, and livelihood are already occurring, and needs are expected to increase as the lean season approaches, particularly as this year's harvest season is expected to be significantly reduced. IPC 3+ levels across the country remain below the threshold for "major food insecurity" in the classification system, and we continue to monitor the situation.

Conflict throughtout Afghanistan has intensified since early May as government forces, the Taliban, and armed actors battle for control of various districts. The fighting has resulted in over 260,000 displacements as well as over 21,000 civilian casualties since early May.

Conflict and food insecurity are now affecting several areas of Ethiopia. Almost 6 million people are at IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity in Tigray and neighboring areas (including over 400,000 people in IPC Phase 5). New deaths and displacements continue to be recorded across the country, with over 385,000 displacements recorded since mid-May. While international attention is focused on Tigray, conflict is also affecting civilians in other regions across Ethiopia.

Following weeks of growing tensions and anti-government protests in Dar’a al-Balad in southern Syria, hostilities between government forces and armed groups escalated at the end of July. Almost 140 people have been killed since May and 24,000 displaced since 28 July.

Amid an ongoing drought, conflict throughout Afghanistan has intensified since early May as government forces, the Taliban, and armed actors battle for control of various districts. Over 373,000 people have been displaced as a result of conflict and drought since early May, with an additional 320,000 returnees to Afghanistan in the same period. 80% of the country is classified as being in either severe or serious drought status, and more than 9 million people in Afghanistan are experiencing high levels of food insecurity, which could be exacerbated by continued conflict and drought.

A 7.2-magnitude earthquake struck Haiti on 14 August, killing at least 29 people. The impacts of the earthquake, stronger than the one in 2010, were felt in other parts of the region. Haiti is also currently in the path of Tropical Storm Grace, expected to hit the country in the coming days. Reports indicate significant damage to homes, roads, and infrastructure, though data on the scale of the impacts is limited at this time. Insecurity is also a concern amid ongoing political instability and food insecurity. We will continue to monitor the situation and update the classification as more data emerges.

A 7.2-magnitude earthquake struck Haiti on 14 August, killing at least 1,297 people. The impacts of the earthquake, stronger than the one in 2010, were felt in other parts of the region. Haiti is also currently in the path of Tropical Storm Grace, expected to hit the country in the coming days. Reports indicate significant damage to homes, roads, and infrastructure, though data on the scale of the impacts is limited at this time. Insecurity is also a concern amid ongoing political instability and food insecurity. We will continue to monitor the situation and update the classification as more data emerges.

A 7.2-magnitude earthquake struck Haiti on 14 August, killing almost 2,000 people and destroying 61,000 homes. The impacts of the earthquake, stronger than the one in 2010, were felt in other parts of the region. Haiti is also currently experiencing flooding as a result of Tropical Storm Grace. Insecurity is also a concern amid ongoing political instability and food insecurity. The IRC is supporting partners in responding to this emergency. We will continue to monitor the situation and update the classification as more data emerges.

Large-scale attacks by non-state armed actors are driving increased humanitarian needs in Burkina Faso's Sahel region, including an August 18 attack in Boukouma (Soum province) that killed at least 86 people and displaced 1,368. Since May, nearly 75,000 people have been displaced due to conflict in the region.

On August 14, members of al-Shabab attacked Adakibir village, displacing 3,100 people to neighboring areas in Adado district in the central Galmudug state of Somalia. Immediate needs identified are shelter, nutrition, and basic health care.

Inter-communal conflicts linked to the sharing of natural resources between fishermen and herders in north Cameroon starting August 8th triggered the displacement of over 10900 people to six villages in Chad, namely Danga, Gamar, Delbé, Mabloum, Oundouma and Bandouma. Existing needs include health, WASH, shelter, and food. Additional arrivals are expected in the coming days.

A 7.2-magnitude earthquake struck Haiti on 14 August, killing over 2,200 people, injuring over 12,200 and destroying almost 53,000 homes. The impacts of the earthquake, stronger than the one in 2010, were felt in other parts of the region. Haiti is also currently experiencing the impacts of Tropical Storm Grace. Insecurity is also a concern amid ongoing political instability and food insecurity. The IRC is supporting partners in responding to this emergency.

The ongoing political crisis in Myanmar has added an additional complexity to the multiple conflicts throughout the country. 14,000 new displacements have been recorded since June in Northern and Southern Shan States, Kachin State, Chin State and South East Myanmar.

The arrival of migrants at the Northern Border in Mexico continue to pose a challenge to NGOs with a marked increase in CBP enforcement actions since March as well as the increase in security risks on the Mexico side of the border. More than 200,000 people have been displaced at the border since June. Please note that due to delays in reporting, this classification likely undercounts arrivals at the border.

On August 14, members of al-Shabab attacked Adakibir village, displacing 11,100 people to neighboring areas in Adado district in the central Galmudug state of Somalia. So far, 42,000 people have been displaced since June due to the steadily deteriorating security situation in the state.

Madagascar is suffering its worst drought in 40 years along with other environmental factors, insecurity, and the impact of COVID-19. In the country's Grand Sud, nearly 1.4 million people are food insecure (with almost 14,000 at IPC 5 level). Impacts on health,  subsistence, and livelihood are already occurring, and needs are expected to increase as the lean season approaches. IPC 3+ levels across the country remain below the threshold for "major food insecurity" in the classification system, and we continue to monitor the situation.

Conflict, drought and food insecurity are now affecting several areas of Ethiopia. While international attention is focused on Tigray, conflict is also affecting civilians in other regions across Ethiopia. Oromia and Somali regions are also experiencing drought as a result of poor rains. This has impacted agricultural production, exacerbating food insecurity in these regions. Almost 6 million people are at IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity in Tigray and neighboring areas (including over 400,000 people in IPC Phase 5). Displacements due to conflict continue to be recorded across the country, with over 421,000 displacements recorded since mid-June.

Displacements continue amid the Taliban's takeover of the state, with over 450,000 people having been displaced since mid-June, with an additional 301,000 returnees to Afghanistan in the same period. Afghanistan is also experiencing drought, with 80% of the country is classified as being in either severe or serious drought status. More than 9 million people in Afghanistan are experiencing high levels of food insecurity, which could be exacerbated by continued conflict and drought.

South Sudan is experiencing multiple shocks related to flooding, conflict, ongoing food insecurity, and COVID-19, affecting multiple systems. Heavy rainfall in South Sudan has caused flooding, affecting over 381,000 people in Panyijar, Mayendit, Leer, Koch, Rubkona, Aweil South and Aweil East counties. Ongoing conflict has displaced over 102,000 people. Almost 1,500 suspected cases of acute watery diarrhoea (AWD) have been reported, as well as outbreaks of malaria, Hepatitis E, and polio.

The arrival of migrants at the Northern Border in Mexico continue to pose a challenge to NGOs with a marked increase in CBP enforcement actions since March as well as the increase in security risks on the Mexico side of the border. More than 215,000 people have been displaced at the border since August.  Please note that due to delays in reporting, this classification likely undercounts migrant arrivals.

Following the poor performance of two consecutive rainy seasons, the Government of Kenya declared the ongoing drought situation as a national disaster on 8 September. The drought has left almost 2.4 million people in IPC 3 or above levels of food insecurity, which is expected to worsen in early 2022 given the below average rains from October through December 2021.

Inter-communal conflicts linked to the sharing of natural resources between fishermen and herders in north Cameroon starting August 8th triggered displacements to six villages in Chad, namely Danga, Gamar, Delbé, Mabloum, Oundouma and Bandouma. 8,515 people remain displaced in Chad, with health, shelter, and WASH needs identified.

Amid ongoing insecurity in Borno state, Bama LGA is experiencing a high influx of displacements. More arrivals are expected as the government is returning IDPs from Maiduguri following the closure of the Farm Center Camp. As a result, the existing infrastructure is overstretched, with gaps identified including health and reproductive health, high malnutrition rates, shelter, WASH and disease outbreak.

Following a security operation in Las Anod, in the southeastern Sool region of Somaliland in Somalia, 1,750 IDPs have been forcibly displaced, with many people arriving in Galkayo, Harfo, Burtinle, and Garowe in Puntland. Those who have arrived need humanitarian assistance in terms of protections services, water, shelter, food, health and other basic services. Additional forced displacements are expected in the coming days.

On 8 October, the Ministry of Health announced a new laboratory confirmed case of Ebola in Butsili Health Area, Beni Health Zone in North Kivu Province. This follows a cluster of three deaths related to suspected Ebola in the same area last month. North Kivu was affected by an Ebola outbreak earlier this year in Butembo city that was declared over on 3 May. The emergence of sporadic cases is not unusual following an outbreak, and the MOH, WHO and partners are conducting follow-up assessments

On 8 October, the Ministry of Health announced a new laboratory confirmed case of Ebola in Butsili Health Area, Beni Health Zone in North Kivu Province. This follows a cluster of three deaths related to suspected Ebola in the same area last month. North Kivu was affected by an Ebola outbreak earlier this year in Butembo city that was declared over on 3 May. The emergence of sporadic cases is not unusual following an outbreak, and the MOH, WHO and partners are conducting follow-up assessments

On October 1, seven confirmed human cases of yellow fever were reported near Maturín Municipality in Monagas State in northeastern Venezuela. To date, no deaths have been reported among the confirmed cases.

A 7.2-magnitude earthquake struck Haiti on 14 August, killing almost 2,300 people, injuring over 12,700 and displacing almost 39,000 people. The impacts of the earthquake, stronger than the one in 2010, were felt in other parts of the region. Insecurity is also a concern amid ongoing political instability and food insecurity. The IRC is supporting partners in responding to this emergency.

Violence in Somalia's Galmudug state has displaced almost 119,000 people since August. Fighting between regional forces and armed actors since 23 October has displaced over 100,000 people from the town of Guri'el in the central Galguduud region. The displacements to nearby villages have put pressure on overstretched basic services, with urgent needs identified around food, shelter, NFIs, WASH, health, nutrition, and protection.

Since July, migrants have been attempting to cross Belarusian borders with Poland, Lithuania and Latvia into the EU. At least 16,000 migrants have been stopped at the border with Poland since August, and 8 migrants have died.

On 2 October, a fire broke out in Guanaja, an island located near Honduras. While the cause of the fire remains unclear, 4,000 people have been affected, 450 people have been displaced, and 90 homes have been destroyed. Immediate needs include shelter, WASH, and food.

Inter-communal conflicts linked to the sharing of natural resources between fishermen and herders in north Cameroon starting August 8th triggered displacements to six villages in Chad, namely Danga, Gamar, Delbé, Mabloum, Oundouma and Bandouma. 8,860 people remain displaced in Chad as planned repatriations have not gone ahead. Health, shelter, and WASH needs remain high.

Widespread drought in Afghanistan is affecting at least 80% of the population, worsening food insecurity for the nearly 23 million people in IPC 3+ (Crisis or above) levels. Conflict and displacements also continue, with more than 460,000 people displaced since mid-August.

Conflict, drought and food insecurity are now affecting several areas of Ethiopia. While international attention is focused on Tigray, conflict is also affecting civilians in other regions across Ethiopia. Oromia and Somali regions are also experiencing drought as a result of poor rains. This has impacted agricultural production, exacerbating food insecurity in these regions. Almost 6 million people are at IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity in Tigray and neighboring areas (including over 400,000 people in IPC Phase 5). Displacements due to conflict continue to be recorded across the country, with over 1.1 million displacements reported by local authorities since early August.
At least 99 people have been killed after a fuel tanker exploded following a collision in the capital Freetown on 6 November 2021. Several nearby shops and houses were also set on fire.

Following the dissolution of the transitional government and military declaration of a state of emergency on 25 October, protests and civil unrest have broken out across Sudan. At least 14 people have been killed and 300 injured since the protests broke out. Access to essential services has been disrupted by suspension of internet and telecommunications services, as well as restricted movement of people and goods.

Fighting between armed groups and government forces in North Kivu's Rutshuru territory in eastern DRC has forced at least 11,000 people to flee across the border into Uganda since 7 November. Most of these refugees have since returned, with 960 remaining in Uganda. The majority of arrivals are women and children. Immediate needs include water, shelter, WASH, food, health and protection.

South Sudan is experiencing multiple shocks related to flooding, conflict, ongoing food insecurity, and COVID-19, affecting multiple systems. Heavy rainfall in South Sudan has caused flooding, affecting over 429,000 people in Jonglei, Unity, Upper Nile, Northern Bahl el Ghazal, Lakes, Warrap, Western Bahr el Ghazal and Central Equatoria states and displacing almost 34,000. Ongoing conflict has displaced over 22,000 people since mid-August. Almost 300 suspected cases of Hepatitis E have been reported in Bentiu camp, as well as outbreaks of malaria, cholera, and polio.

Following armed clashes in the northwest Ouham-Pendé prefecture of the Central African Republic since 28 October, more than 12,000 people have been displaced in the sub-prefecture of Bocaranga, specifically in the villages of Ndokombali, Ngaoukala, Assana and Gbengbara.

Over 3.5 million Somali people are experiencing high levels of acute food insecurity as drought intensifies, particularly in southern and central Somalia due to delayed, poor Deyr rainfall. At least 21,000 people have been displaced since mid-August as a result. The current drought conditions are expected to deteriorate given the below average rainy season forecast and a La Niña event lasting until April 2022.

Following the 8 October announcement by the Ministry of Health of a lab confirmed case of Ebola in Butsili Health Area, Beni Health Zone in North Kivu Province, additional cases have been confirmed, bringing the total to 11 confirmed or probable cases and 9 deaths. Cases have also spread to Ngilinga and Bundji health areas. While no new cases have been reported for 22 days, the health reponse is ongoing as further spread remains a risk.

On October 1, seven confirmed human cases of yellow fever were reported near Maturín Municipality in Monagas State in northeastern Venezuela. To date, no deaths have been reported among the confirmed cases and no new cases have been reported, but the risk of further outbreak remains.

Since 17 November, intercommunal clashes in several villages in the Jabel Moon locality in West Darfur, Sudan have killed at least 13 people and forced 14,000 to flee to Birak town in eastern Chad. A possible increase of Sudanese refugees is expected in the coming weeks, and a rapid assessment will be conducted to assess needs.

Armed attacks in Djugu territory in DRC's Ituri province have killed at least 70 people and displaced 50,000 people to nearby towns, primarily Roe, since 19 November. Humanitarian access has reduced in recent weeks in Djugu due to security incidents, and some humanitarian actors have suspended their activities as a result.

The arrival of migrants at the Northern Border in Mexico continue to pose a challenge to NGOs with a marked increase in CBP enforcement actions since March as well as the increase in security risks on the Mexico side of the border. Almost 160,000 people have been displaced at the border since September. Please note that due to delays in reporting, this classification likely undercounts migrant arrivals.

A new outbreak of intercommunal violence on 5 December in the Logone-Birni province of Cameroon has triggered between 27,800-38,700 displacements to Chad, with many hosted in and around N'djamena. 8,860 Cameroonian refugees remain displaced in Chad as planned repatriations have not gone ahead following intercommunal conflict in August. Food, health, shelter, and protection needs remain urgent.

Since March 2021, Burkinabe refugees fleeing violence have arrived in the Bounkani and Tchologo regions of Northern Côte d'Ivoire. IRC assessments in these regions identified 1,971 arrivals since early September. Primary needs include food, WASH, protection and health.

Super Typhoon Rai (Odette), the strongest typhoon to hit the Philippines in 2021, made landfall on 16 December, affecting the southern and central regions of the country. At least 12 people have been killed, and more than 140,000 people have been severely affected, with at least 18,000 displaced. Initial reports indicate severe flooding, road closures, and power and communications disruptions.  Immediate needs of affected families include food, WASH, medicine and medical personnel, personal protective equipment (PPE), and shelter. We are monitoring the situation as details are still emerging about the extent of the emergency.

Super Typhoon Rai (Odette), the strongest typhoon to hit the Philippines in 2021, made landfall on 16 December, affecting the southern and central regions of the country. More than 430 people have been killed or are missing, and more than 1.8 million people have been severely affected, with at least 631,000 displaced. Initial reports indicate severe flooding, road closures, and power and communications disruptions. Immediate needs of affected families include food, WASH, medicine and medical personnel, personal protective equipment (PPE), and shelter. We are monitoring the situation as details are still emerging about the extent of the emergency.

Ongoing conflict in Myanmar has forced some 3,900 people to flee across the border to Thailand since 16 December. They are currently being hosted in several locations in Mae Sot district. It is also reported that some individuals who fled Myanmar in the past several days have since returned, while an additional 2,400 are currently gathered on the Myanmar side of the border.

Heavy rains since 17 December have caused flooding affecting 8 states in Malaysia: Perak, Selangor, Kuala Lumpur, Negeri Sembilan, Melaka, Kelantan, Terengganu, and Pahang. Almost 63,000 people have been displaced.

Following the dissolution of the transitional government and military declaration of a state of emergency on 25 October, protests and civil unrest have broken out across Sudan. At least 44 people have been killed and 300 injured since the protests broke out. Access to essential services has been disrupted by suspension of internet and telecommunications services, as well as restricted movement of people and goods.

Following  intercommunal clashes in several villages in the Jabel Moon locality in West Darfur, Sudan since 17 November 2021, 4,579 refugees remain in Birak town in eastern Chad. The refugees are being relocated to Mile and Kounougou camps. Needs identified include shelter, food assistance, health and nutrition, WASH,  vaccination, education, protection, and legal assistance.

Widespread drought in Afghanistan is affecting at least 80% of the population, worsening food insecurity for the nearly 23 million people in IPC 3+ (Crisis or above) levels. Conflict and displacements also continue, with more than 292,000 people displaced since the end of September.

Nationwide conflict in Myanmar has continued since 1 February 2021, displacing at least 81,000 and killing almost 4,000 people since October. Fighting that started on 15 December around Myawaddy Township in Myanmar's Kayin State, near the border with Thailand is ongoing and has displaced nearly 16,000 people, including 4,788 refugees in Thailand. The main needs of IDPs are food, clean water, shelter, WASH facilities, and psychosocial support.

Since 31 December 2021, heavy rainfall on the Indonesian island of Sumatra has caused widespread flooding, particularly in Jambi and Aceh provinces. Nearly 33,000 people have been displaced with more rain forecasted in the coming days.

The 26 December 2021 collapse of a pair of dams in the eastern state of Bahía, coupled with weeks of intermittent rains and flooding, has affected more than 815,000 people in Bahia and Minas Gerais states. 23 people have been killed and more than 73,000 displaced. Humanitarian needs are urgent for populations in shelters. Prioirty needs include food, shelter, WASH, and protection.

Protests that began in western Kazakhstan on 2 January over an increase in fuel prices spread across the country, leading to widespread unrest and crackdowns. At least 164 people have been killed and 2,200 injured.

Ongoing insecurity in Nigeria's northwestern Zamfara state has displaced almost 17,000 people since the end of October. Armed attacks took place from 4 January in villages in Anka and Bukkuyum LGAs, leaving at least 200 people dead and over 10,000 displaced.

Nationwide conflict in Myanmar has continued, displacing more than 191,000 and killing over 5,000 people since late October 2021. The security situation in the South-East, particularly in Kayin and Kayah states since mid-December has displaced over 46,000 people, while 1,200 people remain displaced in Thailand. Main concerns include protection, shelter, food, medicine and WASH.

South Sudan is experiencing multiple shocks related to flooding, conflict, ongoing food insecurity, and COVID-19, affecting multiple systems. Heavy rainfall in South Sudan since May 2021 has caused flooding, affecting over 55,000 people in Jonglei, Unity, Upper Nile, Northern Bahr el Ghazal, Lakes, Warrap, Western Bahr el Ghazal and Central Equatoria states and displacing almost 25,000 since November. Outbreaks of malaria, cholera, and polio have also been reported.

A new outbreak of intercommunal violence on 5 December in the Logone-Birni province of Cameroon triggered nearly 36,000 displacements to Chad, with many hosted in and around N'djamena. These refugees will be relocated to two sites in Guilmey and Kalambari by the end of January. Shelter, WASH, and health needs remain urgent.

Since March 2021, Burkinabe refugees fleeing violence have arrived in the Bounkani and Tchologo regions of Northern Côte d'Ivoire. IRC assessments in these regions identified over 3,500 arrivals since November 2021. Primary needs include food, WASH, protection and health.

Following the dissolution of the transitional government and military declaration of a state of emergency on 25 October 2021, protests and civil unrest have broken out across Sudan. At least 59 people have been killed and 1,500 injured since the protests began.
Continued conflict in South Sudan, Ethiopia, and other countries is driving increased arrivals refugees fleeing to Sudan. Over 11,255 refugees fled to Sudan in November and December 2021.

On 17 January, 5.3 magnitude and 4.9 magnitude earthquakes struck in Qadis District, Badghis Province in the Western Region of Afghanistan. On 20 January, another earthquake struck in the area. At least 26 people have been killed, and up to 1,000 houses were damaged or destroyed by the earthquake. Shelter, food, and water needs remain high. Details on the impacts of the earthquakes are still emerging, and we are monitoring for updates.

Armed attacks in DRC's Ituri province have displaced nearly 126,000 people since November. Nearly 3 million people, more than half the population of Ituri, are also facing IPC 3+ food insecurity. Humanitarian access has reduced in recent weeks due to security incidents, and some humanitarian actors have suspended their activities as a result.

On 15 January, the Hunga Tonga-Hunga Ha'apai undersea volcano erupted and prompted a tsunami that killed three people in Tonga and have displaced 62 people. Data on the extent of the crisis continues to emerge given challenges in communications due to damaged infrastructure. Water and food supplies and health are urgent needs.

Over 3.5 million Somali people are experiencing high levels of acute food insecurity as drought intensifies, particuarly in southern and central Somalia due to delayed, poor Deyr rainfall. 3.2 million people are affected by ongoing drought and at least 171,000 people have been displaced since November as a result. The current drought conditions are expected to deteriorate given the below average rainy season forecast and a La Niña event lasting until April 2022.

Fighting that began on 20 January in Al Hasakeh governorate in northeast Syria has displaced around 45,000 people, while the number of civilian casualties remains unconfirmed due to the full lockdown in Hasakeh city.

Ongoing conflict in Myanmar has forced some 2,600 people to flee across the border to Thailand since 16 December. They are currently being hosted in several locations in Mae Sot and Meung districts. It is also reported that some individuals who fled Myanmar in the past several weeks have since returned.

Conflict in Yemen has escalated in recent weeks. UAE-backed forces advanced in Marib and Shabwah governorates in late 2021. Ansar Allah carried out a drone strike on Abu Dhabi on 17 January 2022, after which the Saudi-led coalition conducted airstrikes in Ansar Allah's strongholds in Sanaa, Hodeidah, and Jawf. Nearly 58,000 people have been displaced and more than 10,000 killed due to conflict since November, primarily in Hodeidah, Jawf, Marib and Shabwah governorates.

Armed attacks in DRC's Ituri province have displaced over 160,000 people since November, including a 1 February attack on an IDP camp in Djugu territory that displaced 35,000 and killed 58 people. Nearly 3 million people, more than half the population of Ituri, are also facing IPC 3+ food insecurity. Humanitarian access has reduced in recent weeks due to security incidents, and some humanitarian actors have suspended their activities as a result.

Heavy rains in Ecuador caused a hillside in the capital Quito to collapse, killing at least 23 people and destroying at least 8 homes. The landslide caused damage to public infrastructure, including electricity services.

Following the poor performance of two consecutive rainy seasons, the Government of Kenya declared the ongoing drought situation as a national disaster on 8 September 2021. This has left almost 2.4 million people in IPC 3 or above levels of food insecurity, which is expected to worsen in early 2022 given the below average rains from October - December 2021.

Super Typhoon Rai (Odette), the strongest typhoon to hit the Philippines in 2021, made landfall on 16 December, affecting the southern and central regions of the country. More than 9.9 million people have been affected, with at least 406 people killed and 144,000 displaced. Needs of affected families include food, WASH, medicine and medical personnel, personal protective equipment (PPE), and shelter.

Chad continues to experience conflict, with recent violence in Fouli department displacing nearly 10,000 people. Nearly 1 million people nationwide are experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity, which is expected to worsen throughout the first half of 2022, particularly in conflict-affected regions.

Conflict continues throughout Niger, having displaced almost 40,000 people in Diffa, Tillabery and Tahoua regions since November. More than 2.5 million people nationwide are experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity, which is expected to worsen throughout the first half of 2022, particularly in conflict-affected regions.

Following intercommunal violence in West Darfur on 20 January, more than 6,700 refugees have fled to Adre and Mamata villages along Chad's eastern border with Sudan. The main needs of the affected population are food, shelter, medicine, and WASH.

Armed attacks in the Nord and Sahel regions of Burkina Faso have intensified in the past two months. Nearly 93,000 have been displaced as result of these attacks. Almost 700,000 people are also experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity in Yatenga, Loroum, Oudalan, Seno, Soum, and Yagha provinces. Primary needs include WASH, protection and food.

Widespread drought in Afghanistan is affecting at least 80% of the population, worsening food insecurity for the nearly 23 million people in IPC 3+ (Crisis or above) levels. Conflict and displacements also continue, with nearly 170,000 people displaced since the mid-November.

Ethiopia is experiencing a prolonged drought after three consecutive failed rainy seasons since late 2020 affecting 6.8 million people living in Oromia, SNNP, Southwest and Somali regions. This has impacted livelihoods and food insecurity in these regions. Almost 4.4 million people are at IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity in Tigray and neighboring areas (including over 400,000 people in IPC Phase 5). Conflict is also affecting several areas of Ethiopia, and displacements continue to be recorded across the country, with over 502,000 displacements reported since mid-November.

Mali continues to experience armed attacks as a result of ongoing conflict. Since late November, more than 19,000 people have been displaced in Ménaka, Gao, Mopti and Koulikoro regions. Nearly 1.2 million people in Mali are also facing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity.

After weeks of tensions, conflict between Ukraine and Russia began on 24 February. At least 100 people have reportedly been killed and 100,000 displaced, though initial reports are difficult to verify. Additional cross-border displacements have been reported and will be classified separately as data becomes available. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Heavy rains in eastern Brazil have led to significant flooding, particularly in Bahia, Minas Gerais, and Rio de Janeiro states. More than 152,000 people have been displaced, with nearly 250 killed and 720 injured. Humanitarian needs are urgent for populations in shelters. Priority needs include food, shelter, WASH, and protection.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced thousands of people so far into neighboring countries, with nearly 16,000 arrivals in Moldova since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Pakistani populations displaced to Afghanistan in 2013 following security operations have begun returning to the Tirah Valley of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Province in Pakistan due to the improved security situation in Pakistan and the deteriorating context in Afghanistan. 5,000 families have returned so far, with additional returns planned in the coming months. Those returning are staying with host families and immediate assistance with shelter, food, NFIs, and WASH.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced thousands of people so far into neighboring countries, with more than 50,000 arrivals in Poland since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced thousands of people so far into neighboring countries, with nearly 7,300 arrivals in Romania since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced thousands of people so far into neighboring countries, with more than 84,000 arrivals in Hungary since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced thousands of people so far into neighboring countries, with more than 36,000 arrivals in Moldova since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced thousands of people so far into neighboring countries, with more than 281,000 arrivals in Poland since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced thousands of people so far into neighboring countries, with more than 32,000 arrivals in Romania since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced thousands of people so far into neighboring countries, with more than 30,000 arrivals in Slovakia since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

South Sudan is experiencing multiple shocks related to flooding, conflict, and COVID-19, affecting multiple systems. Ongoing conflict across the country has displaced at least 132,000 people since late 2021. At least 7.2 million people nationwide are also facing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity.

After weeks of tensions, conflict between Ukraine and Russia began on 24 February. More than 350 people have reportedly been killed and 160,000 internally displaced, though initial reports are difficult to verify. Additional cross-border displacements have been reported and will be classified separately as data becomes available. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing insecurity in Nigeria's northwestern  Zamfara state has displaced over 17,000 people since December. Armed attacks took place from 4 January in  villages in Anka and Bukkuyum LGAs, leaving at least 200 people dead and over 10,000 displaced. Over 801,000 people in Zamfara are also facing IPC 3+ food insecurity.

Nationwide conflict in Myanmar has continued, displacing more than 135,000 and killing over 4,700 people since December 2021. The security situation in the southeast, particularly in Kayin and Kayah states, has displaced 36,000 people in 2022 alone, while armed clashes also intensified in the northwestern part of the country in January. Main concerns include protection, shelter, food, health, education and WASH.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced thousands of people so far into neighboring countries, with nearly 550,000 arrivals in Poland since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing drought in Somalia is affecting 4.5 million people, including 4.2 million people facing IPC 3+ food insecurity, and has also displaced over 458,000 people since December. Drought conditions could persist depending on the outcomes of the March-May rainy season, which is currently projected to bring average levels of rainfall in the country.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced nearly 3 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 1.8 million arrivals to Poland since the conflict began on 24 February. We are aware that not all of these people will have remained in Poland and so we are monitoring closely for data that would enable us to reflect these onwards movements. We will update the classification as we obtain updated and more accurate data.

After weeks of tensions, conflict between Ukraine and Russia began on 24 February. 1.85 million people have been internally displaced and hundreds killed. Local authorities claim 2,500 people have been killed in the southern city of Mariupol alone. Additional cross-border displacements have been reported and are classified separately.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displacedover 3 million people into neighboring countries, with over 350,000 arrivals to Moldova since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some people are moving onwards within Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced 3 million people so far into neighboring countries, with nearly 500,000  arrivals to Romania since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some refugees are moving onwards within Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced 3 million people so far into neighboring countries, with more than 220,000 arrivals to Slovakia since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving situation and reality that some people are moving onwards within Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Super Typhoon Rai (Odette), the strongest typhoon to hit the Philippines in 2021, made landfall on 16 December, affecting the southern and central regions of the country. More than 11.8 million people have been affected, with at  405 people killed and 8000 people still displaced outside shelters.Needs of affected families include food, WASH, and shelter.

After weeks of tensions, conflict between Ukraine and Russia began on 24 February. 6.48 million people have been internally displaced and hundreds killed. Local authorities claim 20,000 civilian casualties  in the southern city of Mariupol alone. Additional cross-border displacements have been reported and are classified separately.

The Government of Kenya declared the ongoing drought situation as a national disaster on 8 September 2021, which is likely to worsen as the country experienced its third consecutive below-average rainy season in late 2021. This has left 3.1 million people food insecure, a figure that might grow depending on the outcomes of the March-May long rains.

Armed attacks in DRC's Ituri province have displaced over 51,000 people since January, including a 1 February attack on an IDP camp in Djugu territory that displaced 35,000 and killed 58 people, as well as an attack on a displaced persons camp on 19 March. Nearly 3 million people, more than half the population of Ituri, are also facing IPC 3+ food insecurity. Humanitarian access has reduced in recent weeks due to security incidents, and some humanitarian actors have suspended their activities as a result.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has forced nearly 3.5 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 207,000 arrivals to Germany since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some people are moving onwards through Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has forced nearly 3.5 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 10,000 arrivals to France since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some people are moving onwards through Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has forced nearly 3.5 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 330,000 arrivals to Hungary since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some people are moving onwards through Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has forced nearly 3.5 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 53,000 arrivals to Italy since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some people are moving onwards through Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced nearly 3.5 million people so far into neighboring countries, with more than 563,000 arrivals to Romania since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some refugees are moving onwards within Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced 3 million people so far into neighboring countries, with more than 260,000 arrivals to Slovakia since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving situation and reality that some refugees are moving onwards within Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Nearly 40,000 refugees, most fleeing conflict in eastern DRC, have arrived in Kisoro district in Western Uganda since January. More than 7,000 refugees have also arrived from South Sudan.  Urgent needs include food, health, and WASH.
Following intercommunal violence in West Darfur on 20 January, nearly 9,000 refugees have fled to Chad. The main needs of the affected population are food, shelter, health, and WASH.

Since the start of January, a cholera outbreak has led to over 2000 suspected cases as well as 32 deaths in the SW region. Additionally, over 2.8 million people are experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity throughout Cameroon.

Chad continues to experience conflict, with recent violence in Fouli department displacing more than 11,000 people. Nearly 1 million people nationwide are experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity, which is expected to worsen throughout the first half of 2022, particularly in conflict-affected regions.

Ethiopia is experiencing a prolonged drought after three consecutive failed rainy seasons since late 2020 affecting over 6.8 million people living in Oromia, SNNP, Southwest and Somali regions. This has impacted livelihoods and food insecurity in these regions and has displaced nearly 150,000 people. Almost 4.4 million people are at IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity in Tigray and neighboring areas (including over 400,000 people in IPC Phase 5). Conflict also continues across the country, with over 300,000 displacements reported since January in Tigray, Benishangul Gumuz, Oromia, Amhara, Afar, and SNNP regions.
