Armed attacks in Burkina Faso continue, with the Nord and Sahel regions most affected. Nearly 239,000 have been displaced as result of these attacks since the beginning of 2022. 1.65 million people are also experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity throughout Burkina Faso.

Conflict continues throughout Niger, having displaced more than 140,000 people in since the beginning of 2022. More than 2.5 million people nationwide are experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity, which is expected to worsen throughout the first half of 2022, particularly in conflict-affected regions.

Flooding has affected Bubanza, Rumonge and Cibitoke provinces in northeastern Burundi since the end of February, displacing almost 3,000 people in late March and early April. WASH and shelter remain the primary needs, while the the impacts of COVID-19 and rising food prices are compounding the pressures on the population.

Mali continues to experience armed attacks as a result of ongoing conflict, including a massacre of at least 300 civilians in the village of Moura in late March. Since January, nearly 25,000 people have been displaced in Ménaka, Gao, Mopti, Koulikoro and Ségou regions. Nearly 1.2 million people in Mali are also facing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity.

The effects of protracted conflict and economic crisis are fueling food insecurity across Yemen. 17.4 million people, 54% of the population, are experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity, with 5.6 million people in IPC 4 and 31,000 in IPC 5. The recent ceasefire comes as more than 52,000 people have been displaced and 4,000 people killed by conflict across the country since January.

Nationwide conflict in Myanmar has continued, displacing more than 185,000 and killing over 4,800 people since January. Armed clashes in Chin state and Magway and Sagaing regions in particular displaced more than 151,000 people, while clashes continue in the south east.

Intercommunal violence across South Kordofan and North, South, and West Darfur since late January has led to the displacement of over 21,000 people. Additionally, crop failure and the underlying economic crisis place additional challenges on this response as nearly 1.5 million people in the affected areas are facing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity.

Ongoing insecurity in Nigeria's northwestern Zamfara state has displaced over 6,600 people since late January. Ongoing insecurity has disrupted access to affected populations, particularly in locations with high numbers of displaced people. Over 710,000 people in Zamfara are also facing IPC 3+ food insecurity.

South Sudan is experiencing compounding shocks related to flooding, drought, conflict, and economic downturn, affecting multiple systems. Ongoing conflict across the country has displaced at least 66,000 people since late January. At least 7.2 million people nationwide are also facing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity.

Armed attacks are continuing across Afghanistan. The ongoing economic crisis in the country is also worsening the impacts of widespread drought that has affected at least 80% of the population. Nearly 23 million people are facing IPC 3+ (Crisis or above) levels of food insecurity across the country.

The DRC Ministry of Health declared an Ebola outbreak after three cases were confirmed in Mbandaka, a city in DRC's northwestern Equateur province. One of the patients died on 21 April after experiencing symptoms starting on 5 April. This is the third outbreak in the province since 2018. The MOH, WHO and partners are investigating further. We will continue to update the classification as more data becomes available.

Ongoing drought in Somalia is affecting 6.1 million people, including over 6 million people facing IPC 3+ food insecurity, including 81,000 facing IPC 5. Drought has also displaced 451,000 people since February. While some parts of Somalia have experienced light rains, the subsequent dry periods have not alleviated the impact of the drought.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced nearly 5.4 million people into neighboring countries, with almost 440,000 arrivals to Moldova since the conflict began on 24 February. The IRC is continuing to respond in Moldova. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some people are moving onwards within Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced nearly 5.4 million people into neighboring countries, with almost 3 million arrivals to Poland since the conflict began on 24 February. Partners indicate that 20-25% of arrivals have moved onto other countries, leaving about 1.6 million refugees in Poland as of 20 April. The IRC is continuing to respond in Poland. We will update the classification as we obtain updated and more accurate data on the number of refugees remaining in the country.

Armed attacks in DRC's northeastern Ituri province continue. Between 11 and 15 April, armed attacks in Irumu province caused the displacement of more than 165,000 people. Nearly 3 million people, more than half the population of Ituri, are also facing IPC 3+ food insecurity. Humanitarian access is affected due to security incidents.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has forced more than 5.5 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 390,000 arrivals to Germany since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some people are moving onwards through Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has forced more than 5.5 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 45,000 arrivals to France since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some people are moving onwards through Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has forced more than 5.5 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 530,000 arrivals to Hungary since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some people are moving onwards through Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has forced more than 5.5 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 86,000 arrivals to Italy since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some people are moving onwards through Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced over 5.5 million people into neighboring countries, with 98,000 refugees currently remaining in Moldova, where the IRC is continuing to respond. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some people are moving onwards within Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced more than 5.5 million people so far into neighboring countries, with at least 80,000 refugees remaining in Romania. Given the rapidly evolving context on the ground and reality that some refugees are moving onwards within Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced more than 5.5 million people so far into neighboring countries, with almost 380,000 arrivals to Slovakia since the conflict began on 24 February. Given the rapidly evolving situation and reality that some refugees are moving onwards within Europe, we will continue to monitor the situation and issue reclassifications as we receive updated data.

The conflict in Ukraine continues as Russian forces have shifted their focus primarily to the eastern Donbas region of Ukraine. 7.7 million people have been internally displaced across the country. Nearly 3,000 people have been killed and 3,200 injured, though actual civilian casualty figures are likely higher. More than 5.5 million people have fled Ukraine to neighboring countries; these additional cross-border displacements are classified separately.

On 14 April, the South Sudan Ministry of Health reported a confirmed case of cholera in Bentiu IDP camp in Unity State. It is the first cholera case reported in the country since the 2017 outbreak. As of 7 May, 31 probable or confirmed cases have been reported in Bentiu camp and Rubkona town, with one death.

Since the beginning of 2022, South Sudan's Northern Bahr el Ghazal state has been experiencing a suspected meningitis outbreak, with Aweil East and Aweil West counties most affected. Reports identify at least 66 probable cases and 8 deaths since mid-February.

Drought is continuing to affect 4.2 million in Kenya's arid and semi-arid lands (ASAL), which are counties where the demand for water is higher than supply. These areas, the most affected which are in the northeastern and northwestern part of Kenya, make up 89% of the country and 38% of the population and are particularly vulnerable to climate-induced shocks. The drought is likely to worsen as the country experienced its third consecutive below-average rainy season in late 2021 and the delayed onset of the March-May long rains. This has left 3.5 million people facing IPC 3+ food insecurity.

Ethiopia is experiencing a prolonged drought after three consecutive failed rainy seasons since late 2020 affecting over 6.8 million people living in Oromia, SNNP, Southwest and Somali regions. This has impacted livelihoods and food insecurity in these regions, and has displaced nearly 40,000 people. Almost 4.4 million people are at IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity in Tigray and neighboring areas (including over 400,000 people in IPC Phase 5). Conflict also continues across the country, with over 100,000 displacements reported since March.

Intercommunal conflict in the Abyei Administrative Area (AAA) since February has displaced 76,000 people within Abyei. The AAA is a disputed area on the border between Sudan and South Sudan that was granted a special administrative status in the 2005 Comprehensive Peace Agreement (CPA) that ended Sudan's civil war. Under the CPA, local governments on both sides of the border will administer the AAA jointly, with support from a UN peacekeeping force, until a referendum can be held on the area's permanent status. The referendum was due to be held in 2011 but has been delayed indefinitely.

Chad continues to experience conflict, with recent violence in the western Lac province displacing more than 6,100 people since February. Nearly 1 million people nationwide are experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity, which is expected to worsen throughout the first half of 2022, particularly in conflict-affected regions.

The ongoing cholera outbreak in Cameroon has led to over 5,9000 suspected cases as well as 50 deaths across the country, with the Southwest region most affected. This outbreak is further complicated by conflicts in the Southwest, Northwest and Far North regions. Additionally, over 2.8 million people are experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity throughout Cameroon.

Conflict continues throughout Niger, having displaced more than 23,000 people since March. More than 2.5 million people nationwide are experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity, which is expected to worsen throughout the first half of 2022, particularly in conflict-affected regions.

Armed attacks in Burkina Faso continue, with the Centre-Est, Centre-Nord, Est, Nord, and Sahel regions most affected. Nearly 40,000 have been displaced as result of these attacks since March. 1.65 million people are also experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity throughout Burkina Faso.

Nationwide conflict in Myanmar has continued, displacing more than 154,000 and killing over 5,000 people since the end of February. Armed clashes in Chin state and Magway and Sagaing regions in particular displaced more than 141,000 people, while clashes continue in the southeast.

Increased conflict between armed groups and the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) in eastern DRC's Ituri province has displaced more than 173,000 people since mid-March. Nearly 3 million people in Ituri are also facing IPC 3+ food insecurity. Humanitarian access is affected due to security incidents.

Armed attacks in eastern DRC's North Kivu province continue  due to the re-emergence of the M23 armed group and increased clashes with the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) since March. More than 142,000 people have been displaced, including at least 72,000 people following recent attacks in May near the provincial capital, Goma. More than 2 million people in North Kivu are also facing IPC 3+ food insecurity. Humanitarian access is affected due to security incidents.

Mali continues to experience armed attacks as a result of ongoing conflict, which has displaced more than 7,550 people since February. Ménaka, Gao, Mopti, Koulikoro and Ségou regions are most affected. Nearly 1.2 million people in Mali are also facing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity.

Armed attacks are continuing across Afghanistan, with fighting between government forces and armed actors displacing 1,600 families in Laghman province in late May. The ongoing economic crisis in Afghanistan is also worsening the impacts of widespread drought that has affected at least 80% of the population. Nearly 19 million people are facing IPC 3+ (Crisis or above) levels of food insecurity across the country, a minimal improvement due to harvest, but one that is unlikely to persist given socioeconomic pressures on the population.

Since September 2021, Afghanistan has been experiencing a potential cholera outbreak. Cases of acute watery diarrhoea (AWD) have been increasing, with outbreaks in Kabul, Kandahar, Kapisa, Laghman, and Zabul provinces. 91 cases have been confirmed since late March, but poor surveillance across the country likely means that actual case numbers are higher. These provinces are also experiencing high levels of food insecurity, with nearly 8.2 million people facing IPC 3+.

High intensity heatwaves since March have affected the Cholistan region of the eastern Punjab region in Pakistan. These heatwaves have resulted in the onset of a drought due to depleted water reservoirs. 26,000 people have been displaced as a result, while grazing areas for livestock have also reduced.

Ongoing violence in northwest Nigeria, particularly in Katsina, Sokoto, and Zamfara states, continues to cause displacement, with more than 18,000 people displaced since mid-March. Nearly 4 million people across these states are facing IPC 3 or worse levels of food insecurity. Additionally, the combination of conflict, disrupted access to farming and livelihoods, widespread displacement, rising food prices and disease outbreaks are driving a growing health and nutrition crisis - including high rates of childhood malnutrition - in the region, where humanitarian access remains limited.

At least 11 people were killed and 54 injured following heavy rains and windstorms in Damaturu, the capital of Nigeria's northeastern Yobe state. Nearly 2,000 households are affected, with immediate needs including food items, WASH, shelter, and NFIs.

Flooding has affected Bubanza, Rumonge and Cibitoke provinces in northeastern Burundi in late March and early April, displacing almost 1,800 people. WASH and shelter remain the primary needs, while the impacts of COVID-19 and rising food prices are compounding the pressures on the population.

More than 4.8 million people from Ukraine are currently displaced in Europe since the conflict in Ukraine began on 24 February, with the total number of refugees from Ukraine currently in Poland estimated at 1.5-2 million people. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers the 1.02 million new refugee arrivals within the past 90 days. The IRC is continuing to respond in Poland.

Mali continues to experience armed attacks as a result of ongoing conflict, which has displaced more than 7,550 people since February. Ménaka, Gao, Mopti, Koulikoro and Ségou regions are most affected. Nearly 1.2 million people in Mali are also facing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity.

Intercommunal violence across North, South, and West Darfur and South and West Kordofan since mid-March has led to the displacement of nearly 20,000 people. Additionally, crop failure and the underlying economic crisis place additional challenges on this response as the population faces higher-than-usual food assistance needs.

South Sudan is experiencing multiple shocks related to flooding, conflict, and COVID-19, affecting multiple systems. Ongoing conflict continues to affect civilians, including in Leer County, where armed violence since 4 April has displaced at least 44,000 people. At least 7.2 million people nationwide are also facing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity. We have also separately classified meningitis and cholera outbreaks in South Sudan.

The effects of protracted conflict and economic crisis are fueling food insecurity across Yemen. 17.4 million people, 54% of the population, are experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity, with 5.6 million people in IPC 4 and 31,000 in IPC 5. The ceasefire announced on 2 April has largely held, but localized conflict and the economic impacts of years of war continue to fuel displacements, with more than 10,000 people displaced since late February.
Ongoing drought in ASAL counties

Fighting between armed groups and government forces in the eastern DRC provinces of Ituri and North Kivu has forced nearly 38,000 people to flee across the border into Uganda since mid-March. Immediate needs include protection, food, and WASH.

Armed attacks in Burkina Faso continue, with the Centre-Est, Centre-Nord, Est, Nord, and Sahel regions most affected. More than 27,000 people have been displaced as result of these attacks since mid-March, with nearly 15,000 displaced after a 11 June attack in Seytenga (Sahel region). Nearly 2.5 million people are also experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity throughout these regions.

Communal violence in Adamawa state in northwestern Nigeria continues, with disputes predominantly occurring over land. Over 10,000 people were displaced and at least 30 killed by the latest clashes from 6 to 9 June in Guyuk and Lamurde LGAs. Needs for affected populations include WASH, shelter, food, NFIs, and health.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced more than 4.9 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 572,000 refugees arriving in Germany since mid-March. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers new refugee arrivals within the past 90 days.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced more than 4.9 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 33,000 refugees arriving in France since mid-March. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers new refugee arrivals within the past 90 days.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced more than 4.9 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 72,000 refugees arriving in Italy since mid-March. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers new refugee arrivals within the past 90 days.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced more than 4.9 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 28,000 refugees arriving in Romania since mid-March. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers people who are newly displaced within the last 90 days and remain in Romania. Thus, the severity score and response stance for this classification has fallen though the overall number of displacements remains high. The IRC is continuing to respond in Romania.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced more than 4.9 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 47,000 refugees arriving in Slovakia since mid-March. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers people who are newly displaced within the last 90 days and remain in Slovakia. Thus, the severity score and response stance for this classification has fallen though the overall number of displacements remains high. The IRC is continuing to respond in Slovakia.

The conflict in Ukraine continues as Russian forces have shifted their focus primarily to the eastern Donbas region of Ukraine. More than 7.1 million people are internally displaced, though this classification only covers the 654,000 people that have been newly internally displaced in Ukraine within the last 90 days. More than 3,600 people have been killled and more than 4,100 injured in that same period, though actual civilian casualty figures are likely higher. More than 4.9 million people have also fled Ukraine to neighboring countries; these additional cross-border displacements are classified separately.

On 14 April, the South Sudan Ministry of Health reported a confirmed case of cholera in Bentiu IDP camp in Unity State. It is the first cholera case reported in the country since the 2017 outbreak. As of 26 June, 233 probable or confirmed cases have been reported in Bentiu camp and Rubkona town, with one death. The current transmission remains sporadic, but the risk of transmission is very high due to population movements, presence of IDPs, persistent flooding, and low access to safe water and sanitation.

A 5.9 magnitude earthquake struck the Central Region of Afghanistan on 22 June, impacting Paktika and Khost provinces. The earthquake was also reportedly felt in neighbouring provinces including Kabul, as well as in Islamabad, Pakistan and India. As of the latest reporting, more than 1,000 people have been killed and 1,600 injured. At least 2,000 homes have also been destroyed. Telecommunications are poor or nonexistent, making it hard to get a full accounting of the casualties.

Increased violence in Mozambique's northern Cabo Delgado province, particularly in Ancuabe and Chiure districts, has displaced over 36,000 people in June. The recent surge in violence comes as nearly 48,000 people have been displaced by fighting in Cabo Delgado since April, exacerbating humanitarian needs in a region that has experienced conflict since July 2017. More than 932,000 people in Cabo Delgado are also facing Crisis (IPC 3) or worse levels of food insecurity.

Ongoing drought in Somalia is affecting 7.7 million people, with nearly 7.1 million people facing IPC 3+ food insecurity, including 213,180 facing IPC 5. Drought has also displaced over 102,000 people since mid-March. While some parts of Somalia have experienced light rains, the subsequent dry periods have not alleviated the impact of the drought.

Armed attacks in Burkina Faso continue, with the Centre-Est, Centre-Nord, Est, Nord, and Sahel regions most affected. Nearly 50,000 people have been displaced as result of these attacks since mid-March, with more than 34,000 displaced after a 11 June attack in Seytenga (Sahel region). Nearly 2.5 million people are also experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity throughout these regions.

Ongoing conflict in Myanmar has forced over 1,800 people to flee across the border to Thailand since 21 June, with more refugees expected to arrive in the coming days. They are currently being hosted in several locations in Tak province.

Conflict continues to affect Niger, having displaced nearly 53,000 people in since April. More than 4.4 million people nationwide are experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity, particularly in conflict-affected regions.

Nationwide conflict in Myanmar has continued, displacing more than 208,000 and killing over 5,500 people since the beginning of April. Armed clashes in Chin state and Magway and Sagaing regions in particular displaced more than 196,000 people, while clashes continue in the south east.

Heavy rainfall has been affecting southern and north-western Pakistan over the past few days, causing floods, and flash floods that resulted in casualties and damage. In Balochistan province, 2,800 people have been displaced and 77 killed. Additional rains are expected as the monsoon season continues. The IRC is already responding to the emergency with food packages and dignity kits for affected communities.

Chad continues to experience conflict, with recent violence between gold miners in Tibesti province (near the border with Libya) displacing 3,300 people. The Chadian government declared a food and nutrition emergency in the country on 1 June. More than 2 million people nationwide are experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity, particularly in conflict-affected regions.

The effects of a protracted civil war since 2014 and economic crisis are fueling acute food insecurity and malnutrition across Yemen. Russia’s invasion of Ukraine has further exacerbated food insecurity in Yemen due to disruptions in food supplies. Over 19 million people, 60% of the population, are projected to be experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity between June and December 2022. Meanwhlie, the current ceasefire agreement, which has largely reduced conflict levels is set to expire on 2 August. Still, over 25,000 people have been displaced as a result of conflict since late April.

Ethiopia is experiencing a prolonged drought after now four consecutive failed rainy seasons across the eastern Horn of Africa since late 2020, affecting over 8.1 million people living in Oromia, SNNP, Southwest and Somali regions. This has impacted livelihoods and food insecurity in these regions, and has displaced more than 17,000 people since April. Drought is likely to worsen as the upcoming October to December rains are expected to be below average. 7.4 million people are facing severe food insecurity across the country. Conflict also continues across the country, with over 111,000 displacements reported since April.

A measles outbreak is spreading in South Kordofan in Sudan, affecting children in particularly remote enclaves of the state. Since May, at least 320 cases have been suspected, with 13 deaths, though actual case numbers are likely higher due to ongoing conflict and lack of access to health care.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced more than 5.8 million people into neighboring countries, with more than 573,000 refugees arriving in Germany since mid-April. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers new refugee arrivals within the past 90 days.

Continuous armed attacks in Mali have led to displacement of more than 90,000 people since May. A projected 1.8 million people between June and August 2022, 8% of the population, are now facing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity, while 1.2 million children are acutely malnourished.

Nearly 2,700 people have been killed and more than 3,600 injured during the past three months of the Ukraine conflict, though actual civilian casualty figures are likely higher. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers new civilian causalities within the past 90 days. We have not included displacement figures in this classification since more Ukrainians have returned to their habitual residence than have been displaced within the past 90 days. Therefore, CRRD leaders have increased the response stance from the system-recommended Yellow to Orange because the classification does not account for the large numbers of people unable to displace from conflict-affected areas.

Floods and flash floods were reported in Yobe state in north-eastern Nigeria, resulting in 7,000 shelters destroyed and 21 people killed. Flooding has affected 11 LGAs, with Damaturu, Gujba and Gulani being the hardest hit. Loss of food and livestock have been reported, while bridges and road networks have also been destroyed.

Intercommunal violence broke out on 11 July in Sudan's Blue Nile state due to land disputes that played out along ethnic lines. The clashes have led to more than 100 deaths, while at least 31,000 people have been displaced as a result. Nearly 180,000 people are facing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity in Blue Nile.

A magnitude 7.0 earthquake struck the northern part of the Philippines on 27 July, with the epicenter of the quake affecting Abra province. 7,331 people have been displaced, more than 79,260 affected, and 1,583 houses damaged across Ilocos region and Cordillera administrative region, while schools have also been damaged. Vital infrastructure, including electricity and roads, were also affected.

Since September 2021, Afghanistan has been experiencing a potential cholera outbreak. 8,901 cases of acute watery diarrhea (AWD) since May have been confirmed in Kabul, Kandahar, Kapisa, Ghazni, Jawzjan, Paktika, Zabul, Helmand, Laghman and Samangan provinces. Food insecurity is an exacerbating factor for this outbreak, with nearly 8 million people in these provinces facing IPC 3+.

A 5.9 magnitude earthquake struck the Central Region of Afghanistan on 22 June, impacting Paktika and Khost provinces. This was followed by a 4.2 magnitude aftershock on 24 June in Paktika and another 5.1 magnitude earthquake on 18 July in Khost and Paktika provinces. As of the latest reporting, more than 1,000 people have been killed and 6,000 injured. At least 4,540 homes have also been destroyed or partially damaged.

Ethiopia is experiencing a prolonged drought after now four consecutive failed rainy seasons across East Africa since late 2020, affecting over 8.1 million people living in Oromia, SNNP, Southwest and Somali regions. This has impacted livelihoods and food insecurity in these regions and has displaced more than 15,000 people since May. Drought is likely to worsen as the upcoming October to December rains are expected to be below average. 7.4 million people are facing severe food insecurity across the country. Conflict also continues across the country, with over 130,000 displacements reported since the start of May.

Drought conditions have persisted in Kenya's arid and semi-arid lands (ASAL) counties, with more than 1.1 million people facing IPC 4+ levels of acute food insecurity. These counties are in the northern and northeastern parts of Kenya and make up over 80% of the country. They are particularly vulnerable to climate-induced shocks and are often marginalized. The country experienced delayed onset and poor distribution of the March-May long rains. There is also a forecasted fifth consecutive below-average rainy season during the October-December short rains, that is likely to exacerbate the already fragile situation.

Heavy rainfall has been affecting southern and north-western Pakistan since July, causing floods that have resulted in casualties and heavy damage. In Baluchistan province, 21,000 people have been displaced and nearly 130 killed. Additional rains are expected as the monsoon season continues. Additionally, damage to infrastructure and crops has been reported, along with livestock deaths. The IRC is already responding to the emergency with food packages and dignity kits for affected communities.

Ongoing drought in Somalia is affecting 7.7 million people, with nearly 2.3 million people facing IPC 4+ food insecurity, including 213,180 facing IPC 5. Drought has also displaced over 145,000 people since May. While some parts of Somalia have experienced light rains, the subsequent dry periods have not alleviated the impact of the drought.

Between 21 and 23 July, flooding has affected CAR, predominantly in the western and central prefectures of the country (Bangui, Ombella M'Poko, Lobaye, Nana-Gribizi, Ouham-Pendé and Ouham-Fafa). More than 21,700 people have lost their homes and a dozen bridges have been destroyed. While the rains have stopped for now, humanitarian needs are likely to increase as damaged houses continue to collapse and more rainfall is expected in the country.

The ongoing economic crisis in Afghanistan is worsening the impacts of widespread drought that has affected at least 80% of the population. Nearly 19 million people are facing IPC 3+ (Crisis or above) levels of food insecurity across the country, including nearly 6 million at IPC 4. The increase in severity score and stance since this emergency was last classified primarily reflects the change since 1 August in how we factor food insecurity into classifications, rather than a dramatic shift in the situation on the ground.

Intercommunal violence across North, South, and West Darfur and South and West Kordofan since May has led to the displacement of more than 63,000 people. Additionally, more than 1.04 million pepole in these regions are facing IPC 4+ food insecurity. Crop failure and the underlying economic crisis place additional challenges on this response as the population faces higher-than-usual food assistance needs. Please note that this classification does not cover the recent clashes in Blue Nile, which are classified separately.

Increased conflict between armed groups and the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) in eastern DRC's Ituri province has displaced nearly 84,000 people since May. More than 706,000 people in Ituri are also facing IPC 4+ food insecurity. Humanitarian access is affected due to security incidents.

Armed attacks in eastern DRC's North Kivu province continue  due to the re-emergence of the M23 armed group and increased clashes with the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) since March. Nearly 120,000 people have been displaced since May, while nearly 550,000 people are facing IPC 4+ food insecurity in the region. Humanitarian access is affected due to security incidents.

South Sudan is experiencing multiple shocks related to flooding, conflict, and COVID-19, affecting multiple systems. Ongoing conflict in northern South Sudan continues to affect civilians, displacing more than 7,300 people in Warrap in June. Nearly 450,000 people in Warrap are also facing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity. We have also separately classified an ongoing cholera outbreak in Bentiu camp, as well as conflict in the Equatorias.

South Sudan is experiencing multiple shocks related to flooding, conflict, and COVID-19, affecting multiple systems. Ongoing conflict continues to affect civilians, displacing 14,000 people since May in Central and Eastern Equatoria. More than 300,000 people in these regions are also facing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity. We have also separately classified an ongoing cholera outbreak in Bentiu camp, as well as conflict in northern South Sudan.

Armed attacks in Burkina Faso continue, with the Centre-Est, Centre-Nord, Est, Nord, and Sahel regions most affected. More than 34,000 people remain displaced after a 11 June attack in Seytenga (Sahel region). Over 583,000  people in these regions are also experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity.

Cameroon is experiencing an ongoing cholera epidemic, with over 1,500 suspected cases recorded since May, with the Southwest region most affected. This outbreak is is further complicated by conflicts in the  Southwest, Northwest and Far North regions, with at least 10,000 people displaced in the Far North since May. Additionally, over 185,000 people are experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity in these regions.

Nationwide conflict in Myanmar has continued, displacing more than 297,000 and killing over 4,700 people since mid-May. The ongoing conflict continues to impact IRC activities. Additionally, fuel shortages and price increases have been reported from various locations across the country, most recently in Kayah and Kachin States.
Flooding across Chad due to heavy rains

Floods and flash floods were reported in Yobe state in north-eastern Nigeria, resulting in 458 households requiring immediate shelter and affecting 22,000 people overall. Flooding has affected 11 LGAs, with Damaturu, Gujba and Gulani being the hardest hit. Loss of food and livestock have been reported, while bridges and road networks have also been destroyed. At the same time, Yobe state is also experiencing a cholera outbreak as a result of the flooding, with the first case confirmed on 29 July. 123 cases have been reported across 6 LGAs, with 6 deaths.

Conflict continues to affect various areas of Niger, having displaced more than 28,000 people since mid-May. More than 16,000 people in the Torodi and Makalondi communes of Tillaberi region have been displaced since May due to ongoing violence. 316,000 people in the conflict-affected regions of Diffa, Maradi, Tahoua and Tillaberi are also experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity.

On 14 April, the South Sudan Ministry of Health reported a confirmed case of cholera in Bentiu IDP camp in Unity State. It is the first cholera case reported in the country since the 2017 outbreak. 240 new probable or confirmed cases have been reported in Bentiu camp and Rubkona town since mid-May, with one death. The current transmission remains sporadic, but the risk of transmission is very high due to population movements, presence of IDPs, persistent flooding, and low access to safe water and sanitation.

Chad continues to experience conflict, with Lac province particularly affected. The Chadian government declared a food and nutrition emergency in the country on 1 June. More than 2 million people nationwide are experiencing acute food insecurity. Heavy rains have also caused flooding in eight of Chad's 23 provinces; please note that this emergency has been classified separately.

Health authorities in the Democratic Republic of the Congo declared a resurgence of Ebola on 22 August, following the death of a 46-year-old woman  on 15 August in Beni. The WHO and other actors are monitoring and responding to the outbreak. Additionally, the Beni area is facing a high level of insecurity with significant humanitarian needs linked to the displacement of the population.

Heavy rainfall has been affecting southern and north-western Pakistan since July, causing floods that have resulted in casualties and heavy damage. Sindh province - which is 90% submerged - is one of the worst affected areas, where more than 855,000 people have been displaced and  over 260 killed. Additional rains through August are expected as the monsoon season continues. Additionally, damage to infrastructure, including roads, bridges and telecommunications networks has been reported, while crop destruction and livestock deaths have also occurred.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced nearly 6.9 million people across Europe, with more than 244,000 refugees arriving in Germany since mid-April. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers new refugee arrivals within the past 90 days.

Continuous armed attacks in Mali have led to displacement of more than 10,000 people across Ménaka, Gao, Mopti and Segou regions since June. Nearly 150,000 people in these regions are now facing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity, while 145,000 children are acutely malnourished.

Heavy rainfall has been affecting Pakistan since mid-June, causing floods that have affected 33 million people. Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Sindh and Baluchistan provinces are the most affected areas. Nearly 1.69 million homes have been affected, with at least 560,000 homes completely destroyed. More than 1,300 people have been killed and 12,000 injured due to the flooding.  Additionally, damage to infrastructure, including roads, bridges and telecommunications networks has been reported, while crop destruction and livestock deaths have also occurred. Access continues to be a major obstacle to the delivery of aid and the ability of people to flee to safer locations.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has newly displaced nearly 2 million people within the past three months. After a slight decline in the number of people newly displaced within Ukraine, displacements are again on the rise, primarily in the south and east of the country where active conflict continues. Additionally, nearly 1,500 people have been killed and more than 3,000 injured during that same time, though actual civilian casualty figures are likely higher. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers new population movements and civilian casualties within the past 90 days.

Since September 2021, Afghanistan has been experiencing a potential cholera outbreak. More than 135,000 suspected cases of acute watery diarrhoea (AWD) since early June have been reported across Afghanistan, with 42 deaths. 55% of cases are among children younger than 5 years. Ongoing flooding is likely to increase the risks of water-borne illness and increase AWD cases in the country.

A 5.9 magnitude earthquake struck the Central Region of Afghanistan on 22 June, impacting Paktika and Khost provinces. This was followed by a 4.2 magnitude aftershock on 24 June in Paktika and another 5.1 magnitude earthquake on 18 July in Khost and Paktika provinces. More than 1,000 people have been killed and 6,000 injured, with at least 53% of homes completely destroyed.

The effects of a protracted civil war since 2014 and economic crisis are fueling acute food insecurity and malnutrition across Yemen. Russia’s invasion of Ukraine has further exacerbated food insecurity in Yemen due to disruptions in food supplies. Over 7.3 million people are experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity, with 161,000 facing the worst levels (IPC 5). Meanwhile, the UN-backed ceasefire was renewed for another two months on 2 August, significantly reducing conflict-related casualties. Still, over 11,000 people have been displaced as a result of conflict since early June, and conflict activity has renewed in Shabwah and Abyan provinces in recent weeks.

Ethiopia is experiencing a prolonged drought after now four consecutive failed rainy seasons across the eastern Horn of Africa since late 2020, affecting over 24 million people living in Afar, Oromia, SNNP, Southwest and Somali regions. This has impacted livelihoods and food insecurity in these regions. Drought is likely to worsen as the upcoming October to December rains are expected to be below average. 9.9 million people are facing severe food insecurity across the country. Conflict also continues across the country, displacing more than 360,000 peopel since mid-June, with more than 260,000 displaced in Tigray and Amhara after the resumption of fighting between the federal government and the TPLF on 24 August.

Drought conditions have persisted in Kenya's arid and semi-arid lands (ASAL) counties, with more than 785,000 people facing IPC 4+ levels of acute food insecurity. These counties are in the northern and northeastern parts of Kenya and make up over 80% of the country. They are particularly vulnerable to climate-induced shocks and are often marginalized. The country experienced delayed onset and poor distribution of the March-May long rains. There is also a forecasted fifth consecutive below-average rainy season during the October-December short rains that is likely to worsen food insecurity in these regions.

Ongoing drought in Somalia is affecting 7.8 million people, with nearly 1.3 million people facing IPC 4+ food insecurity, including 121,180 facing IPC 5, the worst level of food insecurity. Famine is projected in Baidoa and Burhakaba districts. Drought has also displaced over 183,000 people since mid-June. While some parts of Somalia have experienced light rains, the subsequent dry periods have not alleviated the impact of the drought. Moreover, the October-December rainy season is projected to fail, likely worsening the situation.

The Syrian Ministry of Health declared a cholera outbreak on 10 September. At least 43 cases of cholera have been reported across Syria and nearly 1,000 more are suspected The outbreak is likely tied to people drinking and using unsafe water from the Euphrates River, so it reflects the severe water shortages affecting Syria.

In Liberia, this year’s rainy season caused flooding across 15 counties but has severely affected Montserrado county - which includes the capital Monrovia - in the northwest of Liberia. Flooding between August and September  in Tweh Farm, Jallah’s Town, the Bushrod Island Block A, Bushrod Island Christopolis, and Battery Factory Communities have displaced nearly 6,000 people. Immediate needs for those affected include food and NFIs.

On 2 October, Haiti reported its first cholera outbreak in three years, with at least 2 confirmed cases and 60 additional suspected cases. Moreover, since late July, Haiti has experienced civil unrest and protests due to fuel price hikes and escalating gang violence, particularly around the capital Port-au-Prince. At least 419 people have been killed amid the violence. The security and economic situation have resulted in hospital closures and other impediments to the humanitarian response.

Intercommunal violence across North, South, and West Darfur and South and West Kordofan since May has led to the displacement of more than 22,000 people. Additionally, more than 1.04 million pepole in these regions are facing IPC 4+ food insecurity. Crop failure and the underlying economic crisis place additional challenges on this response as the population faces higher-than-usual food assistance needs. Please note that this classification does not cover the recent clashes in Blue Nile, which are classified separately.

Nationwide conflict in Myanmar has continued, displacing nearly 250,000 and killing over 4,500 people since July. Fighting has escalated in Rahkine and southern Chin, displacing more than 17,000 people since August. The ongoing conflict continues to impact IRC activities. Additionally, fuel shortages and price increases have been reported from various locations across the country, affecting the ability of the population to afford basic commodities and the ability of farmers to produce food - adding to concerns about food affordability and availabity in the upcoming lean season.

Health authorities in Uganda declared an outbreak of Ebola after confirming a case - who later died - in Mubende district in the central part of the country. There are currently 8 suspected cases who are receiving treatment. This is the first recorded outbreak of Ebola in Uganda since 2019.

The crisis in the northwest and southwest regions of Cameroon continues, with insecurity since August displacing nearly 9,000 people. Additionally, nearly 108,000 people are experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity in these regions. The country has not experienced any new cholera cases in over a month, therefore it is no longer included in this classification. Repeated lockdowns imposed by armed groups continue to disrupt humanitarian activities.

The ongoing economic crisis in Afghanistan is worsening the impacts of widespread drought that has affected at least 80% of the population. Nearly 19 million people are facing IPC 3+ (Crisis or above) levels of food insecurity across the country, including nearly 6 million at IPC 4.

Several states in Nigeria have experienced periods of heavy rain and widespread flooding, increasing the risk of water-borne illnesses spreading. Borno state in the northeast of the country declared a cholera outbreak on 14 September, with 1,700 people affected and 7 deaths thus far.

Since July 2024, Chad has been facing unprecedented flooding due to heavy rainfall and flooding of rivers. Torrential rains have destroyed more than 220,000 homes, affected more than 350 health facilities, flooded nearly 355,600 hectares of crops, and caused the loss of more than 66,570 animals. These floods have impacted 1,700,000 people and killed 503 people. These floods have coincided with an increase in the number of Sudanese refugees entering the eastern part of Chad, which is also affected by flooding.

Increased conflict between armed groups and the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) in eastern DRC's Ituri province has displaced more than 124,000 people since July, predominantly in Djugu and Mambasa territories. More than 833,000 people in Ituri are also facing IPC 4+ food insecurity. Humanitarian access is affected due to security incidents.

On 23 September, a boat departing Lebanon  capsized off the coast of Syria, killing at least 104  of the estimated 150 people on board, who were a mix of Syrians, Palestinians and Lebanese nationals. Only 20 people were rescued, so it is likely the fatality figure is higher than reported. This incident, and the growing number of people trying to leave Lebanon more broadly, reflects the ever-deteriorating economic crisis in the country, which has pushed 80% of the population below the poverty line. Please note that the Crisis Analysis team is working closely with colleagues in the MENA region and Lebanon program to monitor the economic crisis in Lebanon so that we can issue updated emergency classifications as soon as new data is available about its humanitarian impacts.

Super Typhoon Noru made landfall in Luzon in northern Philippines on 25 September. Nearly 975,000 people are affected, with almost 10,000 homes totally destroyed. Nearly 150,000 hectares of farmlands have been damaged - affecting livelihoods. Immediate needs include food, drinking water, shelter, NFIs, medicine, and hygiene kits.

Intercommunal violence broke out on 11 July in Sudan's Blue Nile state due to land disputes that played out along ethnic lines. The clashes have led to nearly 150 deaths and 400 injured, while almost 65,000 people have been displaced as a result. While the situation is currently relatively calm, some renewed clashes were reported in Ar Rusayris and Wad Al Mahi localities in early September. Also, IPC projects 179,508 people in phase 4 emergency level of food insecurity in Blue Nile, and IDPs and affected populations require food assistance, access to water and sanitation, health services, shelter, NFIs and protection.

On 12 October, the US announced new measures to control the entry of Venezuelan migrants into the country, impacting the flow of people moving north through the Darien Gap between Colombia and Panama. At least 10,000 Venezuelan migrants are stranded in the municipalities of Medellin, Turbo and Necocli (in the Uraba Antioqueño region in northwestern and western Colombia) since the policy was announced. Those stranded do not have access to housing and have settled in informal camps or on the streets. They also lack access to basic services, including clean water and sanitation and hygiene services.

Amid the ongoing rainy season that has caused damages and casualties across Central America, Hurricane Julia moved through the Caribbean, affecting El Salvador, Guatemala and Honduras between 8-10 October. More details are emerging about the impacts of Julia, but as of latest reporting, over 851,581 people are affected and more than 7,300 displaced in Guatemala, with the most affected departments including Izabal, Alta Verapaz, Huehuetenango. Local authorities and communities are requesting IRC for response mainly for hygiene kits, psychosocial kits and activities, case management and multi-purpose cash assistance.

Amid the ongoing rainy season that has caused damages and casualties across Central America, Hurricane Julia moved through the Caribbean, affecting El Salvador, Guatemala and Honduras between 8-10 October. More details are emerging about the impacts of Julia, but as of latest reporting, more than 103,000 people are affected and 20,000 displaced in Honduras, with 13,000 displaced. The most affected areas include Yoro, Cortés and Francisco Morazán. Local authorities and communities are requesting IRC for response mainly for hygiene kits, psychosocial kits and activities, case management and multi-purpose cash assistance.

Amid the ongoing rainy season that has caused damages and casualties across Central America, Hurricane Julia moved through the Caribbean, affecting El Salvador, Guatemala and Honduras between 8-10 October. More details are emerging about the impacts of Julia, but as of latest reporting, more than 1,700 people are displaced in El Salvador, with the most affected departments including La Unión, San Miguel and La Libertad. Local authorities and communities are requesting IRC for response mainly for hygiene kits, psychosocial kits and activities, case management and multi-purpose cash assistance.

South Sudan is experiencing multiple shocks related to flooding, conflict, and COVID-19, affecting multiple systems. Clashes between armed groups in Upper Nile displaced 27,000 people in August, where 410,000 people are also facing IPC 4 levels of food insecurity. We have also separately classified an ongoing cholera outbreak in Bentiu camp, as well as conflict in the Equatorias.

On 14 April, the South Sudan Ministry of Health reported a confirmed case of cholera in Bentiu IDP camp in Unity State. It is the first cholera outbreak reported in the country since the 2017 outbreak. 67 new probable or confirmed cases have been reported in Bentiu camp and Rubkona town since July. The current transmission remains sporadic, but the risk of transmission is very high due to population movements, presence of IDPs, persistent flooding, and low access to safe water and sanitation.

South Sudan is experiencing multiple shocks related to flooding, conflict, and COVID-19, affecting multiple systems. Ongoing conflict continues to affect civilians in Central and Eastern Equatoria states, with intercommunal violence in Eastern Equatoria displacing more than 3,000 people in August. Nearly 330,000 people in Central and Eastern Equatoria are also facing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity. Please note that we have also separately classified an ongoing cholera outbreak in Bentiu camp, as well as conflict in northern South Sudan.

Armed attacks in Burkina Faso's northern Sahel region continue, particuarly around the town of Djibo in Soum province. The town has effectively been under blockade since February 2022, impeding humanitarian assistance from reaching the population. This comes as nearly 275,000 across Sahel region are also experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity.

Nigeria has experienced heavy rainfall since June, causing the worst floods in the country in at least a decade. In Kogi and Adamawa states in the northeast of the country, more than 307,000 people are affected, with at least 5,200 people displaced in Adamawa alone. Nigeria has experienced a spike in cholera cases, and flooding is likely to increase the risk of cholera spreading to affected areas. Please note that Yobe state is also affected but we will classify the flooding and cholera outbreak there separately.

The effects of a protracted civil war since 2014 and economic crisis are fueling acute food insecurity and malnutrition across Yemen. Russia’s invasion of Ukraine has further exacerbated food insecurity in Yemen due to disruptions in food supplies. Over 7.3 million people are experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity, with 161,000 facing the worst levels (IPC 5). Meanwhile, the UN-backed ceasfire expired on 2 October, increasing the likelihood of a return to wider conflict. Still, lower level conflit has displaced over 5,400 people since mid-July.

Heavy rains across Chad have led to intense flooding affecting 18 of 23 provinces in the country, namely the capital Ndjamena, Mayo Kebbi East, Logone Occidental, Tandjilé, Mandoul, Sila, Moyen Chari, Guera, and Lake provinces. More than 1 million people have been affected by the floods, with nearly 16,000 displaced, 22 deaths reported and 229 injured. Needs for household items, food, drinking water, shelter and access to health have been identified as priorities. Construction of latrines in the disaster area is deemed as critical to avoid worsening WASH conditions and disease outbreaks.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced nearly 7.7 million people across Europe, with nearly 103,000 refugees arriving in Germany since mid-July. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers new refugee arrivals within the past 90 days.

Heavy rainfall across Pakistan since mid-June caused floods that have affected 33 million people. Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Sindh and Balochistan provinces are the most affected areas. Since mid-July, nearly 2.3 million homes have been affected, with over 888,000 homes completely destroyed. More than 1,600 people have been killed and 12,500 injured due to the flooding.  Additionally, damage to infrastructure, including roads, bridges and telecommunications networks has been reported, while crop destruction and livestock deaths have also occurred. Increases in water-borne illnesses have also been reported.

Lebanon has reported a cholera outbreak - its first after nearly 30 years - after confirming a case on 6 October. Thus far, 168 cases have been confirmed with 5 deaths. Cholera cases have been reported in rural areas bordering Syria, specifically Akkar and Aarsal, likely as both Syria itself is experiencing a widespread cholera outbreak and the economic crisis in Lebanon has led to crumbling infrastructure - including health and WASH systems - that leaves the population at high risk for continued spread.

The Syrian Ministry of Health declared a cholera outbreak on 10 September. More than 5,800 cases of cholera have been reported across Syria with 43 deaths. The outbreak is likely tied to people drinking and using unsafe water from the Euphrates River, so it reflects the severe water shortages affecting Syria following nearly a decade of conflict.

On 12 October, the US announced it would deport Venezuelan migrants to Mexico while they wait for their asylum applications to be processed. Since then, at least 36,000 Venezuelans have been pushed back to northern border cities or stranded at the northern border and in San Pedro Tepanatepec in southern Mexico. As a result, shelters are over capacity. Moreover, migrants, particuarly those stuck at the northern border, face numerous protection risks.

Armed attacks in eastern DRC's North Kivu province continue  due to the re-emergence of the M23 armed group and increased clashes with the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) since March. 46,000 people have been displaced since late July, while nearly 865,000 people are facing IPC 4+ food insecurity in the region. Insecurity is constraining humanitarian access.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine continues as Ukraine has gone on the counteroffensive in the northeast and southeast of the country, making significant territorial gains. More than 220,000 people have been internally displaced since. Additionally, nearly 1,000 people have been killed and more than 2,300 injured due to conflict within the past 90 days, though actual civilian casualty figures are likely higher. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers new population movements and civilian casualties within the past 90 days.

Cyclone Sitrang made landfall in Bangladesh on 24 October - the first cyclone to hit the country since 2017. 960,000 people are affected, with at least 94,000 displaced and 15 killed. In addition, around 10,000 homes were damaged or destroyed. Drinking water sources are contaminated, while santiation facilities have also been damaged.

Ethiopia is experiencing a prolonged drought after now four consecutive failed rainy seasons across the eastern Horn of Africa since late 2020, affecting over 24 million people living in Afar, Oromia, SNNP, Southwest and Somali regions. This has impacted livelihoods and food insecurity in these regions. Drought is likely to worsen as the current October to December rains are expected to be below average. Additionally, a cholera outbreak has been reported in the drought-affected Oromia and Somali regions, with 273 cases and 9 deaths reported. On 2 November, the Ethiopian government and the TPLF signed a peace deal to end the conflict in Tigray, but uncertainties about implementation and improved access in the region remain. Still, conflict also continues across the country, displacing more than 316,000 people since August.

Drought conditions have persisted in Kenya's arid and semi-arid lands (ASAL) counties, with more than 1,211,000 people facing IPC 4+ levels of acute food insecurity. These counties are in the northern and northeastern parts of Kenya and make up over 80% of the country. They are particularly vulnerable to climate-induced shocks and are often marginalized. The country experienced delayed onset and poor distribution of the March-May long rains, and there is a high likelihood of below-average rainfall during the October to December short rains.

Ongoing drought in Somalia is affecting 7.8 million people, with nearly 2.5 million people facing IPC 4+ food insecurity, including 301,000 facing IPC 5, the worst level of food insecurity. Famine is projected in Baidoa and Burhakaba districts in the southern Bay region. Drought has also displaced over 176,000 people since August. The October-December rainy season is projected to fail, likely worsening the situation amid high food prices and ongoing conflict, which has also displaced more than 172,000 people since August.

Health authorities in Uganda declared an outbreak of Ebola after confirming a case - who later died - in Mubende district in the central part of the country on September 20th. There are currently 130 confirmed cases, including 43 deaths, amongst 7 districts, including the capital, Kampala. This is the first recorded outbreak of Ebola in Uganda since 2019.

Tropical Storm Paeng made landfall over Virac, Catanduanes in the northern Philippines on 29 October. Over 2,000 homes have been totally destroyed, with at least 150 people dead and 120 injured. About half of the affected population is unable to access water. Immediate needs include drinking water, sanitation, and hygiene.

Lebanon has reported a cholera outbreak - its first after nearly 30 years - after confirming a case on 6 October. Thus far, 390 cases have been confirmed with 17 deaths. Overall, more than 1,700 cases have been reported throughout the country, including in rural areas bordering Syria, specifically Akkar and Aarsal. This is likely as both Syria itself is experiencing a widespread cholera outbreak and the economic crisis in Lebanon has led to crumbling infrastructure - including health and WASH systems - that leaves the population at high risk for continued spread.

Floods and flash floods were reported in Nigeria's northeastern Yobe state in July, resulting in 458 households requiring immediate shelter and affecting 160,000 people overall. Flooding has affected 11 LGAs, with Damaturu, Gujba and Gulani being the hardest hit. Loss of food and livestock have been reported, while bridges and road networks have also been destroyed. At the same time, Yobe state is also experiencing a cholera outbreak as a result of the flooding, with the first case confirmed on 29 July. 1,618 cases have been reported, with 181 confirmed, and 39 deaths.

Armed attacks in Burkina Faso continue, with the Centre-Est, Centre-Nord, Est, and Nord regions among the most affected. More than 22,000 people were displaced in the Centre-Nord region in early October after armed group attacks. Over 308,000 people in these northern regions are also experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity. Please note that we have classified the conflict and food insecurity in the Sahel region separately.

Since October 12, flooding in the Far North region of Cameroon has affected over 150,000 people and displaced over 10,000. Lack of access to safe drinking water, shelter, and latrines are immediate concerns, increasing protection and disease risks. Additionally, over 100 people have been killed due to violence from armed groups, while over 70,000 people are experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity in the Far North.

The ongoing economic crisis in Afghanistan is worsening the impacts of widespread drought that has affected at least 80% of the population. Nearly 19 million people are facing IPC 3+ (Crisis or above) levels of food insecurity across the country, including nearly 6 million at IPC 4.

Since September 2021, Afghanistan has been experiencing a potential cholera outbreak. Since August 2022, more than 185,000 suspected cases of acute watery diarrhea (AWD) have been reported across Afghanistan, with 42 deaths. 57% of cases and 76% of deaths are among children younger than five years old. Cases are reported in all 34 provinces, including Kabul.

On 28 September, flooding affected 4 provinces in central and western CAR: Paoua, Kouango, Bambari, and the capital, Bangui. Over 20,000 people have been displaced, bridges have been destroyed, and schools have been destroyed or are being used to shelter displaced people. This recent flooding follows flooding in late July and August that affected western and central CAR, including Bangui.

Nationwide conflict in Myanmar has continued, displacing nearly 210,000 and killing over 3,700 people since August. The ongoing conflict continues to impact IRC activities. Additionally, fuel shortages and price increases have been reported from various locations across the country, affecting the ability of the population to afford basic commodities, including food.

Continuous armed attacks starting in June have led to displacement of more than 30,000 people in the Menaka region of eastern Mali.  There are over 100,000 people facing IPC4+ in the surrounding area and high rates of severe and global acute malnutrition in Menaka.

Increased conflict between armed groups and the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) in eastern DRC's Ituri province has displaced more than 30,000 people since September, predominantly in Kyamata and Mambasa territories. More than 200,000  people in Ituri are also facing IPC 4+ food insecurity. Humanitarian access is affected due to security incidents.

Intercommunal violence in Sudan's Blue Nile State has been ongoing since July and a recent spike of violence in October has displaced over 30,000 people and killed over 300. The most recent clashes mainly affected Dam Town 6 in Wad Al Mahi locality. Additionally, IPC projects about 100,000 people in Phase 4 emergency level of food insecurity in Blue Nile, and IDPs and affected populations require food assistance, access to water and sanitation, health services, shelter, NFIs and protection.

On 21 November, a 5.6 magnitude earthquake hit West Java province in Indonesia, killing more than 260 people and injuring over 1,000. Information about the extent of the impacts is still emerging, but urgent needs include shelter, medicine, food and clean water.

On 14 April, the South Sudan Ministry of Health reported a confirmed case of cholera in Bentiu IDP camp in Unity State. It is the first cholera case reported in the country since the 2017 outbreak. 108 new probable or confirmed cases have been reported in Bentiu camp and Rubkona town since August. Women account for 62% of all cases, while children between 0-4 account for 33% of all cases.

Over 188,000 people have been displaced since recent clashes began on 20 October between the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) and the M23 armed group in North Kivu, primarily around the Rutshuru territory and Nyiragongo territories, outside the provincial capital Goma. Armed attacks and clashes in North Kivu have increased since the re-emergence of M23 in March 2022, while fighting between the FARDC and Allied Democratic Forces (ADF) continues in Beni territory. Additionally, nearly 865,000 people in North Kivu are facing IPC 4+ food insecurity in the region.

Lebanon has reported a cholera outbreak - its first after nearly 30 years - after confirming a case on 6 October. Thus far, 586 cases have been confirmed with 20 deaths. Overall, more than 4,000 cases have been reported, including in rural areas bordering Syria, specifically Akkar and Aarsal. This is likely as both Syria itself is experiencing a widespread cholera outbreak and the economic crisis in Lebanon has led to crumbling infrastructure - including health and WASH systems - that leaves the population at high risk for continued spread. Additionally, over 300,000 people across Lebanon are experiencing IPC4+ levels of food insecurity.

In the Menaka region of eastern Mali, there have been continious armed attacks that started in June. Since September, the insecurity has displaced over 30,000 people in Menaka.  Additionally, there are currently over 14,000 people facing IPC4+ levels of food insecurity in Menaka, with projections to increase in 2023, and high rates of severe and global acute malnutrition.

Clashes between armed groups in South Sudan's Upper Nile have continued since 14 August, displacing over 30,000 people in August and September across Upper Nile, Unity and Jonglei states. Additionally, over 200,000 people in Upper Nile are facing IPC4+. We have also separately classified an ongoing cholera outbreak in Bentiu camp, as well as conflict in the Equatorias.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine continues, with Ukraine experiencing large-scale missile strikes that have destroyed significant infrastructure as winter approaches. More than 450,000 people have been internally displaced since September, with more than 1,000 people killed and 2,300 injured due to conflict, though actual civilian casualty figures are likely higher. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers new population movements and civilian casualties within the past 90 days.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine has displaced more than 7.8 million people across Europe, with more than 50,000 refugees arriving in Germany since the end of August. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers new refugee arrivals within the past 90 days.

On 2 October, Haiti reported its first cholera outbreak in three years, with at least 938 confirmed cases and 202 deaths among more than 11,000 suspected cases. Moreover, since late July, Haiti has experienced civil unrest and protests due to fuel price hikes and escalating gang violence, particularly around the capital Port-au-Prince. Nearly 4,000 people have been displaced and at least 180 killed amid the violence in Port-au-Prince and Cité Soleil. These areas are also experiencing high levels of food insecurity, with nearly 345,000 people in IPC 4+, including more than 19,000 in IPC 5. The security and economic situation have resulted in hospital closures and other impediments to the humanitarian response.

Over 170,000 people in South and West Kordofan, in the southern part of Sudan, are facing IPC Phase 4 levels of food insecurity. Intercommunal conflict in the region has continued, with recent violence in West Kordofan's Al Lagowa locality displacing over 36,000 people since late October.

The Syrian Ministry of Health declared a cholera outbreak on 10 September. More than 35,000 cases of cholera have been reported across Syria with 92 deaths. 25% of all reported cases are among children under 5 years old. The outbreak is likely tied to people drinking and using unsafe water from the Euphrates River, so it reflects the severe water shortages affecting Syria following nearly a decade of conflict. The most affected governorates are Deir-ez-Zor, Raqqa, Aleppo, Idlib and Al-Hasakheh.

Health authorities in Uganda declared an outbreak of Ebola after confirming a case - who later died - in Mubende district in the central part of the country on 20 September. There are currently 141 confirmed cases, including 55 deaths, among 9 districts, including the capital, Kampala. This is the first recorded outbreak of Ebola in Uganda since 2019.

Pakistan experienced unprecedented levels of rainfall during the 2022 monsoon season, leading to extreme flooding across the country. While the rainy season has ended and much of the floodwaters have since receded, the population continues to be impacted by the flooding. Between October and December 2022, nearly 130,000 homes were fully destroyed as a result of the floods.  Additionally, damage to infrastructure, including roads, bridges and telecommunications networks persist, while crop destruction and livestock deaths have also occurred as Pakistan is expected to experience a sharp economic downturn. Increases in water-borne illnesses have also been reported.

On October 19th, 2022, the Kenyan Ministry of Health declared a cholera outbreak. There have been over 500 reported cases in 9 counties, including in Nairobi and Garissa, where Dadaab refugee complex is located. This is the second cholera outbreak declared in Kenya this year. Additionally, there is currently an outbreak of measles reported in Garissa.

Drought conditions have persisted in Kenya's arid and semi-arid lands (ASAL) counties, with more than 1,211,000 people facing IPC 4+ levels of acute food insecurity. These counties are in the northern and northeastern parts of Kenya and make up over 80% of the country. They are particularly vulnerable to climate-induced shocks and are often marginalized. The country experienced delayed onset and poor distribution of the March-May rains as well as the October-December short rains, likely prolonging the drought.

The ongoing economic crisis in Afghanistan is worsening the impacts of widespread drought that has affected at least 80% of the population. Nearly 19 million people are facing IPC 3+ (Crisis or above) levels of food insecurity across the country, including nearly 6 million at IPC 4, which is likely to be exacerbated by the below-average harvest and upcoming winter. Afghanistan is additionally experiencing a nation-wide Cholera outbreak that is classified separately.

Since September 2021, Afghanistan has been experiencing a potential cholera outbreak. More than 65,000 suspected cases of acute watery diarrhea  (AWD) have been reported across Afghanistan, with 42 deaths since September 2022. 55% of cases and 77% of deaths are among children younger than five years old. Cases are reported in all 34 provinces, including Kabul

Armed attacks in Burkina Faso continue, with the Centre-Est, Centre-Nord, Est, and Nord regions among the most affected. More than 22,000 people were displaced in the Centre-Nord region in early October after armed group attacks. Over 160,000  people in these northern regions are also experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity. Please note that we have  classified the conflict and food insecurity in the Sahel region separately.

Armed attacks in Burkina Faso's northern Sahel region continue, particuarly around the town of Djibo in Soum province. The town has effectively been under blockade since February 2022, impeding humanitarian assistance from reaching the population. This comes as more than 160,000 people across Sahel region are also experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity .

Flooding, due to heavy rains and overflow of two major rivers, has affected 18 of Chad's 23 provinces. Over 180,000 people have been displaced from the capital of N'Djamena, with over 400,000 affected in Lake Chad province and in the south and central parts of the country.

The crisis in the northwest and southwest regions of Cameroon continues, with insecurity since September displacing nearly 8,000 people. Additionally, over 116,000 people are experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity in these regions. Repeated lockdowns imposed by armed groups continue to disrupt humanitarian activities, and armed groups continue to conduct attacks on health care facilities, workers, and teachers. Note that conflict, flooding, and food insecurity in the Far North has been classified separately.

Since October 12, flooding in the Far North region of Cameroon has affected over 300,000 people and displaced over 100,000. Lack of access to safe drinking water, shelter, and latrines are immediate concerns, increasing protection and disease risks. Additionally, violence from armed groups and intercommunal tensions have displaced over 23,500 people and killed over 50. Finally, almost 82,000 people are experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity in the Far North.

Over 3.5 million Somali people are experiencing high levels of acute food insecurity as drought intensifies, particuarly in southern and central Somalia due to delayed, poor Deyr rainfall. 3.2 million people are affected by ongoing drought and at least 55,000 people have been displaced since mid-September as a result. The current drought conditions are expected to deteriorate given the below average rainy season forecast and a La Niña event lasting until April 2022.

Heavy rains beginning on 12 December in DRC's capital Kinshasa caused landslides, flashfloods, muflows, and river overflow. More than 160 people were killed, while  others are still reported missing. The storm also destroyed more than 300 homes, displacing hundreds, with more than 38,000 people affected in total. Authorities have identified food, medical care, and financial and logistical support as urgent needs.

On 28 September, flooding affected 12 out of 17 prefectures in central and western CAR, most severely affecting Vakaga and Haute-Kotto, as well as the capital  Bangui. Over 20,000 people have been displaced, bridges have been destroyed, and schools have been destroyed or are being used to shelter displaced people. This recent flooding follows flooding in late July and August that affected western and central CAR, including Bangui.
At least 151 people have been killed after a fuel tanker exploded following a collision in the capital Freetown on 5 November 2021. Several nearby shops and houses were also set on fire.

Nationwide conflict in Myanmar has continued, displacing over 160,000 and killing over 3,500 people since September. The ongoing conflict continues to impact IRC activities. Additionally, fuel shortages and price increases have been reported from various locations across the country, affecting the ability of the population to afford basic commodities, including food.

Over 260,000 people have been displaced since October between the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) and the M23 armed group in North Kivu, primarily around the Rutshuru and Nyiragongo territories outside the provincial capital Goma. Additionally, there has been a spike in cholera cases reported in Nyiragongo territory since the end of November, affecting over 900 people. Moreover, nearly 865,000 people in North Kivu are facing IPC 4+ food insecurity in the region.

Ethiopia is experiencing a prolonged drought after now five consecutive failed rainy seasons across the eastern Horn of Africa since late 2020, affecting over 24 million people living in Afar, Oromia, SNNP, Southwest and Somali regions. This has impacted livelihoods and food insecurity in these regions. Drought is likely to worsen as the March to May 2023 rains are expected to underperform.  Additionally, a cholera outbreak has been reported in the drought-affected Oromia and Somali regions, with almost 500 cases and 24 deaths reported. Conflict also continues across the country, displacing more than 200,000 people since September. On 2 November, the Ethiopian government and the TPLF signed a peace deal to end the conflict in Tigray, however, there are still large access constraints and UN and other agencies are unable to provide significant humanitarian aid.
