Intercommunal violence in Sudan's Blue Nile State has been ongoing since July and a recent spike of violence in October has displaced over 49,000 people and killed over 260 during the September - December period. The clashes in October mainly affected Dam Town 6 in Wad Al Mahi locality. Additionally, IPC projects about 100,000 people in Phase 4 emergency level of food insecurity in Blue Nile, and IDPs and affected populations require food assistance, access to water and sanitation, health services, shelter, NFIs and protection.

Conflict, drought and food insecurity are now affecting several areas of Ethiopia. While international attention is focused on Tigray, conflict is also affecting civilians in other regions across Ethiopia. Displacements due to conflict continue to be recorded across the country, with almost 1.8 million displacements reported since the end of September. Oromia and Somali regions are also experiencing drought as a result of poor rains. This has impacted agricultural production, exacerbating food insecurity in these regions. Almost 6 million people are at IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity in Tigray and neighboring areas (including over 400,000 people in IPC Phase 5).

Ongoing drought in Somalia is affecting almost 8 million people, with more than 1.7 million people facing IPC 4+ food insecurity, including over 200,000 facing IPC 5, the worst level of food insecurity. Famine is projected in parts of Baidoa and Burhakaba districts in the southern Bay region and food insecurity is projected to worsen throughout the country. Drought has also displaced over 213,000 people and conflict has displaced over 180,000 people since mid-September.

Conflict continues throughout Niger, having displaced almost 16,000 people in Diffa and Tillabery regions since October. More than 2.5 million people nationwide are experiencing IPC 3+ levels of food insecurity, which is expected to worsen throughout the first half of 2022, particularly in conflict-affected regions.

Increased conflict between armed groups and the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) and attacks against civilians in eastern DRC's Ituri province has displaced more than 43,000 people since September 20, predominantly in Kyamata and Djugu territories. More than 200,000 people in Ituri are also facing IPC 4+ food insecurity. Humanitarian access is affected due to security incidents.

Clashes between armed groups in South Sudan's Upper Nile state have displaced over 8,000 people since early October. Additionally, over 230,000 people in Upper Nile are facing IPC 4 levels of food insecurity. We have  separately classified an ongoing cholera outbreak in Bentiu camp.

On 12 October 2022, the US announced it would expel Venezuelan migrants under Title 42 back to Mexico. Since then, at least 11,000 Venezuelans have been pushed back to northern border cities in Mexico, where they face numerous protection risks. Moreover, shelters are at capacity, straining the ability of existing services to meet the needs of those stranded in Mexico.

The Syrian Ministry of Health declared a cholera outbreak on 10 September. Since October, nearly 52,000 cases of cholera have been reported, and just over 1,000 have been confirmed, across Syria with 32 deaths. The outbreak is likely tied to people drinking and using unsafe water from the Euphrates River, so it reflects the severe water shortages affecting Syria following nearly a decade of conflict. The most affected governorates are Deir-ez-Zor, Raqqa, Aleppo, Idlib and Al-Hasakheh.

Lebanon has reported a cholera outbreak - its first after nearly 30 years - after confirming a case on 6 October. Thus far, 669 cases have been confirmed with 20 deaths. Overall, more than 5,700 cases have been reported, including in rural areas bordering Syria, specifically Akkar and Aarsal. This is likely as both Syria itself is experiencing a widespread cholera outbreak and the economic crisis in Lebanon has led to crumbling infrastructure - including health and WASH systems - that leaves the population at high risk for continued spread. Additionally, over 350,000 people across Lebanon are experiencing IPC4+ levels of food insecurity amid the economic crisis.

Ongoing conflict in Ukraine continues, with Ukraine experiencing large-scale missile strikes that have destroyed significant infrastructure amid winter. Nearly 1 million people have been internally displaced due to the war since October 2022, with more than 600 people killed and 1,800 injured due to conflict, though actual civilian casualty figures are likely higher. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers new population movements and civilian casualties within the past 90 days.

In the Menaka region of eastern Mali, there have been continuous armed attacks that started in June. Since October 2022, the insecurity has displaced over 2,000 people in Menaka. Additionally, there are currently over 14,000 people facing IPC4+ levels of food insecurity in Menaka, with numbers projected to increase in the second half of 2023, and high rates of severe and global acute malnutrition.

Drought conditions have persisted in Kenya's arid and semi-arid lands (ASAL) counties, with more than 1,211,000 people facing IPC 4+ levels of acute food insecurity. These counties are in the northern and northeastern parts of Kenya and make up over 80% of the country. They are particularly vulnerable to climate-induced shocks and are often marginalized. The country experienced delayed onset and poor distribution of the 2022 March-May rains and October to December short rains, prolonging the drought.

On 1 January, Burundi's Ministry of Public Health declared a cholera outbreak initially localized to the capital Bujumbura and its surrounding areas. More than 30 cases have been reported in the country, as well as 2 deaths. The risk of spread remains high as the rains, flooding and high winds during the rainy season have caused latrines, septic tanks and sewers to overflow - as well as the lack of access to clean water and sanitation in the country.

On 2 October 2022, Haiti reported its first cholera outbreak in three years, with more than 1,500 confirmed cases and 440 deaths among more than 22,000 suspected cases in the past 90 days. Moreover,  Haiti has experienced civil unrest and protests due to fuel price hikes and escalating gang violence, particularly around the capital Port-au-Prince. Nearly 7,000 people have been displaced and at least 170 killed amid the violence in Port-au-Prince and Cite Soleil. These areas area also experiencing high levels of food insecurity, with nearly 345,000 people in IPC 4+, including more than 19,000 in IPC 5.  The security and economic situation have resulted in hospital closures and other impediments to the humanitarian response.

Armed attacks in Burkina Faso continue, with the Centre-Est, Centre-Nord, Est, and Nord regions among the most affected. More than 17,000 people were displaced in the Centre-Nord and Nord region in  November after armed group attacks. Over 160,000  people in these  regions are also experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity. Please note that we have  classified the conflict and food insecurity in the Sahel region separately.

Malawi is facing the worst cholera outbreak it has seen in over two decades. The outbreak began on March 3, 2022, after two tropical storms caused flooding, displacement, and lack of access to safe water. Since October, there have been over 15,000 confirmed cases, and 500 deaths. Cases have been reported in all 29 districts, although the northern districts are most affected. Although the outbreak began in March, it is being classified now due to a rapid increase in cases and deaths in the past month.

Mexico has seen a surge in Haitian migrants seeking asylum as the humanitarian crisis in Haiti worsens, forcing Haitians to go on the move. The US policy announcement on 5 January that it would expel Venezuelan migrants under Title 42 back to Mexico has meant a surge in asylum seekers remaining in Mexico, meaning that existing services and shelters are overstretched.

Over 170,000 people in South and West Kordofan, in the southern part of Sudan, are facing IPC Phase 4 levels of food insecurity. Intercommunal conflict in the region has continued, with recent violence in West Kordofan displacing nearly 50,000 people since late October.

Nationwide conflict in Myanmar has continued, displacing over 130,000 and killing nearly 3,500 people since late Octber. The ongoing conflict continues to impact IRC activities. On top of this, the rapid depreciation of the Myanmar Kyat, inflation, movement restrictions and active fighting are causing a reduction in food production and are pushing the price of food beyond the reach of many people in need.

Flooding, due to heavy rains and overflow of two major rivers that began in July and has continued throughout December, has affected 19 of Chad's 23 provinces. Since November, over 155,000 people have been displaced from the capital of N'Djamena, with over 800,000 affected in the south and central parts of the country.

Armed attacks in Burkina Faso's northern Sahel region continue, particuarly around the town of Djibo in Soum province. The town has effectively been under blockade since February 2022, impeding humanitarian assistance from reaching the population. This comes as more than 160,000 people across Sahel region are also experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity, including over 1,800 people experiencing IPC5.

The crisis in the northwest and southwest regions of Cameroon continues, with insecurity since November displacing nearly 8,000 people. Additionally, over 116,000 people are experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity in these regions. Repeated lockdowns imposed by armed groups continue to disrupt humanitarian activities, as well as attacks on health care facilitaties, workers, and teachers. Note that conflict, flooding, and food insecurity in the Far North has been classified separately.

Flooding, that began in October of 2022, has displaced over 20,000 people, and affected over 100,000 since November in the Far North region of Cameroon. Lack of access to safe drinking water, shelter, and latrines are immediate concerns, increasing protection and disease risks. Additionally, violence from armed groups and intercommunal tensions have displaced over 24,000 people and killed over 60. Finally, almost 82,000 people are experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity in the Far North.

The ongoing economic crisis in Afghanistan is worsening the impacts of widespread drought that has affected at least 80% of the population. Nearly 19 million people are facing IPC 3+ (Crisis or above) levels of food insecurity across the country, including nearly 6 million at IPC 4, which is likely to be exacerbated by the below-average harvest and harsh winter. Afghanistan is additionally experiencing insecurity amid increased armed group activity. A nation-wide cholera outbreak has also been classified separately. Meanwhile, a recent ban by the government on female aid workers has led to disruptions to aid delivery as many NGOs have temporarily suspended programming.

Since June 2022, over 16,000 Cameroonian refugees have been displaced to Adamawa state in northeastern Nigeria due to attacks by armed groups. In the past month, Adamawa state has seen an influx of Cameroonian refugees, with over 6,000 arriving since mid-December. Protection, shelter, and food security are major concerns.

Ongoing drought in Somalia is affecting almost 8 million people, with more than 1.9 million people facing IPC 4+ food insecurity, including over 320,000 facing IPC 5, the worst level of food insecurity. Famine is projected in parts of Baidoa and Burhakaba districts in the southern Bay region and food insecurity is projected to worsen throughout the country. Drought has also displaced over 200,000 people and conflict has displaced over 250,000 people since late October

In Zamfara State in northwestern Nigeria, attacks by armed bandits over the past month have led to over 15,000 displacements since January 1, 2023. Banditry in Zamfara has additionally resulted in restricted mobility, disrupted livelihood activities, and limited humanitarian access.

Increased conflict between armed groups and the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) and attacks against civilians in eastern DRC's Ituri province has displaced more than 165,000 people since November, predominantly in Mahagi and Djugu territories. More than 200,000  people in Ituri are also facing IPC 4+ food insecurity. Humanitarian access is affected due to security incidents.

Intercommunal violence in Sudan's Blue Nile State has been ongoing since July but has decreased since late October, with around 18,800 people newly displaced in November. Occasional clashes over the months of November, December and January led to 14 deaths, amid reports of an agreement reached between the fighting groups. The food insecurity levels remain dire with over 100,000 in IPC Phase 4 emergency level of food insecurity in Blue Nile, and IDPs and affected populations require food assistance, access to water and sanitation, health services, shelter, NFIs and protection.

On 2o January 2023, Nigerian health authorities declared an outbreak of diphtheria in four states - Kano, Yobe, Osun, and Lagos, with Kano being the most affected. In the past month, over 130 cases were reported, with 9 confirmed deaths. Over 90% of all cases are in children ages 2-14.

On 6 February, a 7.8-magnitude earthquake struck Gaziantep region in southern Turkey and northern Syria. In northern Syria, primarly in governorates of Idlib, Aleppo, Latakia, and Hama, more than 800 peopele have died so far, and more than 1,000 injured, but these figurse are likely to increase as recue efforts continue. Note we are classifying the impacts of th eearthquake on Turkey separately and we will update both as more data emerges about the impact of the earthquake.

On 6 February, a 7.8-magnitude earthquake struck Gaziantep region in southern Turkey and northern Syria. In Turkey, at more than 1,000 people have died so far and 7,000 injured, but these figures are likely to increase as rescue efforts continue. The earthquake, which is the strongest to hit Turkey in nearly 100 years, has led to the severe damage and destruction of thousands of buildings. Note we are classifying the impacts of the earthquake on Syria separately, and we will update both as more data emerges about the impacts of the earthquake.

As the humanitarian crisis in Haiti worsens, forcing Haitians to go on the move, the US continues to experience an influx of asylum seekers arriving from Haiti. Since November 2022, at least 11,000 Haitians have arrived in the US. The US policy announcement on 5 January that it would expel Haitian migrants crossing the border back to Mexico under Title 42 has meant a surge in asylum seekers remaining in Mexico as well, which we have classified separately.

In Unity State in the northern South Sudan, over 252,000 people are facing IPC 4 levels of food insecurity. This is further compounded by a measles outbreak in the country, with 51 cases confirmed in Unity State since 17 November. Among the confirmed cases, most are children. Moreover, vaccination coverage rates for measles have remained low.

Since late December, conflict between local militials and Somaliland forces in and around the city of Laascaanood in northeastern Somalia displaced over 180,000 people, killed over 170, and injured over 500. The majority of people displaced are women, children, the elderly  and persons with disabilities. In addition to the 180,000 people displaced, over 50,000 are trapped in the town since the fighting began in December.

On 13 February, Equatorial Guinea declared its first ever Marburg virus outbreak. There are at least 16 suspected cases, and 10 deaths, all in the northern district of Kie Ntem bordering Cameroon. Over 4,000 people in Kie Ntem are in quarnatine, and movement restrictions have been enforced on the entire district. Most refugees are women, children, and older people, and primary needs include food, water, and shelter. Additionally, over 180,000 people have been internally displaced in Somalia due to the violence in Laascaanood, which is classified separately.

Since  February 21, over 6,000 refugees fleeing fighting in southeastern Myanmar have arrived in Thailand. As fighting in southeastern Myanmar is likely to continue, more refugees are expected to arrive in Thailand. Please note, insecurity in Myanmar is classified separately.

On 6 February, a 7.8-magnitude  earthquake struck Gaziantep region in southern Turkey and northern Syria, displacing over affecting 9.1 million people and displacing over 1.6 million in Turkey. In Turkey, more than 44,000 people have died so far with more than 108,000 injured, but these figures are likely to increase as aftershocks continue to cause additional impacts. The earthquake, which was the strongest to hit Turkey in nearly 100 years, has led to the severe damage and destruction of thousands of buildings. Note we are classifying the impacts of the earthquake on Syria separately, and we will update both as more data emerges about the impacts of the earthquake.

Sub-Saharan African migrants as well as Black Tunisians have faced a wave of violence, following remarks from Tunisian President Kais Saied on 21 February in which he ordered a crackdown on sub-Saharan African migrants. Over 1,000 migrants from sub-Saharan Africa have been evicted and left homeless, with hundreds of others leaving the country. There are numerous reports of violent attacks against migrants, resulting in injuries and one death.

On 05 March, a fire broke out in Cox's Bazar, a camp serving refugees from Myanmar, in Bangladesh. The fire burnt down health facilities, water networks, and learning centers, as well as over 2,000 homes, leaving over 5,000 people displaced and over 15,000 people affected.

Armed attacks in Burkina Faso's northern Sahel region continue, particuarly around the town of Djibo in Soum province. The town has effectively been under blockade since February 2022, impeding humanitarian assistance from reaching the population. This comes as more than 160,000 people across Sahel region are also experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity, including over 1,800 people experiencing IPC5.

Since late February, 52 cases of menigitis have been reported, with 16 deaths, in two locations in Yobe state, including areas in which IRC has offices, located in Northeast Nigeria. Meningitis can be fatal when untreated and is typically spread through close contact with a personal already infected.

Mexico has seen a surge in Haitian migrants seeking asylum as the humanitarian crisis in Haiti worsens, forcing Haitians to go on the move. The US policy announcement on 5 January that it would expel Venezuelan migrants under Title 42 back to Mexico has meant a surge in Haitian asylum seekers remaining in Mexico. Since January, over 15,000 Haitians have applied for asylum in Mexico. Due to th increase in Haitian asylum seekers, services and shelters are overstretched.

Heavy rainfall since the beginning of March has casued flooding and landslides in the western departments of Peru, notably, in Cajamarca, La Libertad, Lambayeque, and Piura. Over 24,000 people are affected, with over 4,000 displaced. Moderate to heavy rain is anticipated to continue, increasing the risk of flood and impact of the population.

Nationwide conflict in Myanmar has continued, displacing over 290,000, particularly in Southeast Myanmar, and killing over 3,000 people since late December. The ongoing conflict continues to impact IRC activities. On top of this, the  depreciation of the Myanmar Kyat, inflation, movement restrictions and active fighting are causing a reduction in food production and are pushing the price of food beyond the reach of many people in need.

ce 8 March, over 3,000 refugees have fled conflict in eastern DRC, arriving in Kigoma in western Tanzania. Additional refugees are likely to arrive in the coming days. The main needs identified are health and protection support for those arriving.

Since September 2021, Afghanistan has been experiencing a potential cholera outbreak. More than 21,000 suspected cases of acute watery diarrhea (AWD) - with 7 deaths - have been reported across Afghanistan since December. Cases are reported in all 34 provinces, including Kabul and over half of all cases reported are in children under 5 years old.

The conflict in Ukraine continues, with fighting concentrated in eastern Ukraine and missile strikes continuing across the country. At least 375,000 people have been internally displaced due to the war within the past 90 days, with more than 1,000 people killed and nearly 3,000 injured due to conflict, though actual displacement and civilian casualty figures are likely higher. Since the conflict has been ongoing for more than three months, this classification only covers new population movements and civilian casualties within the past 90 days.

As the humanitarian crisis in Haiti worsens, forcing Haitians to go on the move, the US continues to experience an increase of asylum seekers arriving from Haiti. Since January 2023, at least 19,000 Haitians have arrived in the US. The US policy announcement on 5 January that it would expel Haitian migrants crossing the border back to Mexico under Title 42 has meant a surge in asylum seekers remaining in Mexico as well, which we have classified separately.

Heavy rainfall since the beginning of March has casued flooding and landslides in the western departments of Peru, notably in Cajamarca, La Libertad, Lambayeque, and Piura. Over 56,000 people are affected, with over 13,000 displaced. Moderate to heavy rain is anticipated to continue, increasing the risk of flood and impact on the population.

On 2 October 2022, Haiti reported its first cholera outbreak in three years, with more than 1,000 confirmed cases and 200 deaths among more than 15,000 suspected cases since early November 2022. Moreover, Haiti has experienced civil unrest and protests due to fuel price hikes and escalating gang violence, particularly around the capital Port-au-Prince. Since January, more than 5,000 people have been displaced and at least 500 killed amid the violence in Port-au-Prince and Cite Soleil. These areas area also experiencing high levels of food insecurity, with nearly 300,000 people in IPC 4+, including more than 19,000 in IPC 5.  The security and economic situation have resulted in hospital closures and other impediments to the humanitarian response.

The Syrian Ministry of Health declared a cholera outbreak on 10 September 2022. Since December, nearly 22,500 cases of cholera have been reported across the country. The most affected governorates are Deir-ez-Zor, Raqqa, Aleppo, Idlib and Al-Hasakheh. The outbreak is likely tied to people drinking and using unsafe water from the Euphrates River, so it reflects the severe water shortages affecting Syria following nearly a decade of conflict. Moreover, the 6 February earthquake that impacted northern Syria is likely to further strain the capacity of existing health systems and exacerbate needs.

On 6 February, a 7.8-magnitude earthquake struck Gaziantep region in southern Turkey and northern Syria, affecting over 4.5 million people and displacing over 550,000  in Syria. In northern Syria, primarily in governorates of Idlib, Aleppo, Latakia, and Hama, more than 6,500 people have died. Humanitarian access in NW Syria remains a challenge, limiting agencies ability to provide services. Note we are classifying the impacts of the earthquake on Turkey separately and we will update both as more data emerges about the impact of the earthquake.

Increased conflict between armed groups and the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) and attacks against civilians in eastern DRC's Ituri province has displaced more than 93,000 people since December, predominantly in Djugu territory. More than 200,000  people in Ituri are also facing IPC 4+ food insecurity, while over 400 cases of measles have been reported since January. Humanitarian access is affected due to security incidents.

On March 21, the Ministry of Health in Tanzania declared the first ever Marburg Virus Disease outbreak reported in the country. As of March 22, 8 cases have been reported, with five deaths, inlcuding two health workers. All cases have been reported in Bukoba district, in north-western Tanzania, near the borders of Uganda, Rwanda, and Burundi.

Lebanon has reported a cholera outbreak - its first after nearly 30 years - after confirming a case on 6 October 2022. Since December, more than 1,300 cases have been reported, including in rural areas bordering Syria, specifically Akkar and Aarsal. This is likely as both Syria itself is experiencing a widespread cholera outbreak and the economic crisis in Lebanon has led to crumbling infrastructure - including health and WASH systems - that leaves the population at high risk for continued spread. Additionally, over 350,000 people across Lebanon are experiencing IPC4+ levels of food insecurity amid the economic crisis.

In early March, Vanuatu was hit by two back-to-back category 4 cyclones, affecting over 251,000 people, 80% of the population. Since the cyclones, the country has seen a surge in waterborne diseaes, epecially amongst children, that is overwhelming the damaged health system. Additionally, the cyclones destroyed betweeen 90 and 100% of agricultural land on the most impacted islands in the southern part of the country, Efate and Tafae, increasing food insecurity as most of the country relies on small-scale farming for subsistence and livlihoods.

Over 500,000 people have been displaced since January due to clashes between the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) and the M23 armed group in North Kivu, primarily around the Rutshuru, Masisi, and Nyiragongo territories outside the provincial capital Goma. Additionally, there has been a spike in cholera cases reported in  Nyiragongo and Karisimbi health zones since Jnauary, affecting  nearly 4,000  people. Moreover, over 631,000 people in North Kivu are facing IPC 4+ food insecurity in the region.

Ethiopia is experiencing a prolonged drought after now five consecutive failed rainy seasons across the eastern Horn of Africa since late 2020, affecting over 24 million people living in Afar, Oromia, SNNP, Southwest and Somali regions. This has impacted livelihoods and food insecurity in these regions. Drought is likely to worsen as the March to May 2023 rains are expected to underperform.  Additionally, a cholera outbreak has been reported in the drought-affected Oromia and Somali regions, with over 1,500 cases and 22 deaths reported since January. Conflict also continues across the country, displacing more than 400,000 people since January, most from North Shewa and Oromia Special zones in Amhara.

On 13 February, Equatorial Guinea declared its first ever Marburg virus disease outbreak. There are at least 35 suspected cases, including 9 confirmed, and 27 deaths, amongst three provinces: Centro Sur, Littoral, and Kie-Ntem, which borders Cameroon. Marburg virus is a highly infectious disease that cases hemorrhagic fever, similar to Ebola. There is currently no existing treatment or vaccine.

Heavy rainfall since the beginning of March has casued flooding and landslides in the western departments of Peru, notably in Cajamarca, La Libertad, Lambayeque, and Piura. Over 500,000 people are affected, with over 42,000 displaced. Moderate to heavy rain is anticipated to continue, increasing the risk of flood and impact on the population.

On 6 February, a 7.8-magnitude earthquake struck Gaziantep region in southern Türkiye and northern Syria, displacing over affecting 9.1 million people and displacing over 3 million in Türkiye. In Türkiye, more than 50,000 people have died so far with more than 108,000 injured. The earthquake, which was the strongest to hit Türkiye in nearly 100 years, has led to the severe damage and destruction of thousands of buildings. Note we are classifying the impacts of the earthquake on Syria separately.

Armed attacks in Burkina Faso's northern Sahel region continue, particuarly around the town of Djibo in Soum province. The town has effectively been under blockade since February 2022, impeding humanitarian assistance from reaching the population. This comes as more than 200,000 people across Sahel region are also experiencing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity, including over 22,000 people experiencing IPC5.

Since February 21, over 12,000 refugees fleeing fighting in Myawaddy province in southeastern Myanmar have arrived in the Tak province of Thailand.  As fighting in southeastern Myanmar is likely to continue, more refugees are expected to arrive in Thailand. Please note, insecurity in Myanmar is classified separately.

Following months of tensions over a transition to a civilian government, Sudan's military (SAF) and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF) clashed in the capital Khartoum. The clashes in Khartoum have also triggered fighting elsewhere in Sudan, including Darfur and North Kordofan. Thus far, at least 413 people have been killed amid the fighting, with more than 3,500 injured. Critical services, including health, water and electricity have been disrupted, while many humanitarian organizations have paused their activities due to the fighting. Please note that we currently do not have reliable figures for displacements in Khartoum or civilian casualty figures by region, but we will continue to update this classification as we get more data about the humanitarian impacts of the fighting.

Nationwide conflict in Myanmar has continued, displacing over 270,000, particularly in Southeast Myanmar, and killing over 3,750 people since January. The ongoing conflict continues to impact IRC activities. On top of this, the  depreciation of the Myanmar Kyat, inflation, movement restrictions and active fighting are causing a reduction in food production and are pushing the price of food beyond the reach of many people in need.

As clashes in Khartoum and throughout Sudan persist, population movements continue into Chad. Since the conflict in Sudan began on 15 April, an estimated 10,000-20,000 people have sought refuge in villages along the border in Chad. More refugees are likely to arrive in the coming days as the situation in Sudan continues. Please note we are classifying the situation in Sudan separately.

On 2 October 2022, Haiti reported its first cholera outbreak in three years, with more than 600 confirmed cases and 100 deaths among more than 12,600 suspected cases since February. Moreover, Haiti has experienced escalating gang violence, particularly around the capital Port-au-Prince. Since January, more than 22,000 people have been displaced and at least 270 killed amid the violence in Port-au-Prince and Cite Soleil. These areas are also experiencing high levels of food insecurity, with more than 325,000 people in IPC 4.  The security and economic situation have resulted in hospital closures and other impediments to the humanitarian response.

In the Gao and Menaka regions of eastern Mali, armed attacks continue. Since January,  insecurity has displaced over 60,000 people. Additionally, there are nearly 35,000 people facing IPC4+ levels of food insecurity in the regions, with numbers projected  to increase in the second half of  2023, along with high rates of severe and global acute malnutrition among the population.

Since March, at least 7,000 migrants have been expelled from Algeria and are stranded in the Agadez region of northern Niger. Most migrants are from West African countries, and are in need of shelter, water, food, and protection services.

Since the beginning of April, 3,500 people have been displaced by flooding caused by heavy rainfall in the Gatumba area of Northern Burundi. At least 400 homes have been destroyed, as well as agricultural fields and stocks, and latrines.

As the clashes in Khartoum and other areas of Sudan continue, at least 9,500 people have sought refuge in South Sudan, primarly in Renk County, on the border of Sudan.  More refugees are likely to arrive in the coming days as the situation in Sudan continues. Please note we are classifying the situation in Sudan, as well as Sudanese refugee arrivals in Chad,  separately.

On 21 March, the Ministry of Health in Tanzania declared the first ever Marburg Virus Disease outbreak reported in the country. As of 23 April, 9 cases have been reported, with 6 deaths, including two health workers. All cases have been reported in Bukoba district, in north-western Tanzania, near the borders of Uganda, Rwanda, and Burundi.

As the humanitarian crisis in Haiti worsens, forcing Haitians to go on the move, the US continues to experience an increase of asylum seekers arriving from Haiti. Since February, nearly 12,000 Haitians have arrived in the US through the southern border. The US policy announcement on 5 January that it would expel Haitian migrants crossing the border back to Mexico under Title 42 has meant a surge in asylum seekers remaining in Mexico as well, which we have classified separately.

Following months of tensions over a transition to a civilian government, Sudan's military (SAF) and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF) clashed in the capital Khartoum on 15 April. Thus far, at least 459 people have been killed amid the fighting, with more than 4,00 injured, although actual figures may be higher. Critical services, including health, water and electricity have been disrupted, while many humanitarian organizations have paused their activities due to the fighting. The clashes in Khartoum have also triggered fighting elsewhere in Sudan, particularly in Darfur and North Kordofan. Nationwide, at least 75,000 people have been displaced by the recent fighting. We will continue to update this classification as we get more data about the humanitarian impacts of the fighting.

Since 8 March, over 9,000 Congolese refugees have arrived in Kigoma in western Tanzania, having fled conflict in eastern DRC. Additional refugees are likely to arrive in the coming days. The main needs identified are health and protection support for those arriving.

As the clashes in Khartoum and other areas of Sudan continue, at least 16,000 people have sought refuge in Egypt, largely through the Argeen land port on the border of Sudan and Egypt.  More refugees are likely to arrive in the coming days as the situation in Sudan continues. Please note we are classifying the situation in Sudan, as well as Sudanese refugee arrivals in Chad and South Sudan, separately.

Mexico has seen a surge in Haitian migrants seeking asylum, with over 9,000 people seeking asylum since early February, as the humanitarian crisis in Haiti worsens, forcing Haitians to go on the move. The US policy announcement on 5 January that it would expel Venezuelan migrants under Title 42 back to Mexico has meant a surge in asylum seekers remaining in Mexico, meaning that existing services and shelters are overstretched

Heavy rainfall since the beginning of March has casued flooding and landslides in the western departments of Peru, notably in Cajamarca, La Libertad, Lambayeque, and Piura. Over 377,000 people are affected, with more than 65,000 displaced. Despite the humanitarian response, aid has not reached all affected populations. Urgent needs persist for food, safe water, health services, hygiene, education and protection.

As the conflict in Sudan continues, more than 100,000 people have fled Sudan. Nearly 10,000 Sudanese refugees have arrived in CAR since the conflict began, and more refugees are likely to arrive as the situation in Sudan continues.

As the conflict in Sudan continues, more than 100,000 people have fled Sudan. Nearly 9,000 Sudanese refugees have arrived in Ethiopia since the conflict began, and more refugees are likely to arrive as the situation in Sudan continues.

As the conflict in Sudan continues, more than 100,000 people have fled Sudan. At least 42,000 Sudanese refugees have arrived in Egypt since the conflict began, largely through the Argeen land port on the border of Sudan and Egypt. More refugees are likely to arrive as the situation in Sudan continues.

Following months of tensions over a transition to a civilian government, Sudan's military (SAF) and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF) continue to clash in the capital Khartoum. While the conflict in Khartoum continues as repeated ceasefires have failed, conflict has also spread to the Darfur and North Kordofan regions. Overall, nearly 335,000 people have been displaced by the conflict in these three areas, and  at least 528 people have been killed amid the fighting, with nearly 4,600 injured, although actual figures may be higher. Critical services, including health, water and electricity have been disrupted, while conflict and access challenges persist. Over 100,000 people have also fled Sudan amid the fighting, and we have classified refugee arrivals by country separately.

As the conflict in Sudan continues, more than 100,000 people have fled Sudan. At least 30,000 Sudanese refugees have arrived in South Sudan since the fighting began, and more refugees are likely to arrive as the situation in Sudan continues.

Afghanistan is experiencing a complex humanitarian crisis driven by poor economic and security conditions, as well as the impacts of drought. The country is facing the highest risk of famine in 25 years, with nearly 6.1 million people facing IPC 4+ levels of food insecurity and more than 875,000 children facing severe acute malnutrition. Meanwhile, ongoing bans by the government on female NGO workers have led to disruptions to aid delivery amid funding shortages.

Since 4 May, parts of South Kivu province in eastern DRC have been affected by heavy rainfall, which las led to flooding and landslides in Bushushu and Nyamukubi villages of Kalehe Territory. At least 400 people have been killed, while a further 3,000 families have been left homeless. Several roads in the region have also been damaged by the severe weather, including sections of the main road connecting Bukavu and Goma.

As the conflict in Sudan continues, more than 177,000 people have fled Sudan. Nearly 1,000 Sudanese refugees have arrived in Libya since the conflict began. More refugees are likely to arrive as the situation in Sudan continues.

Cyclone Mocha hit the coastal areas along the Bangladesh-Myanmar border on 14 May, bringing heavy rainfall and strong winds and causing landslides in some areas. Over 429,000 people have been affected in Bangladesh, with 60,000 displaced. Assessments are ongoing to identify urgent needs for affected populations. Please note that we have classified the cyclone impacts in Myanmar separately.

As the conflict in Sudan continues, more than 177,000 people have fled Sudan. Nearly 16,000 Sudanese refugees have arrived in Ethiopia since the conflict began. More refugees are likely to arrive as the situation in Sudan continues.

Cyclone Mocha hit the coastal areas along the Bangladesh-Myanmar border on 14 May, bringing heavy rainfall and strong winds, and causing landslides in some areas. In Myanmar, 5.4 million people affected by the storm, with western Rakhine state was worst hit. Assessments are ongoing to identify urgent needs for affected populations, and the classification will be updated as more information about the humanitarian impacts emerge. Please note that we have classified the cyclone impacts in Bangladesh separately.

Following months of tensions over a transition to a civilian government, Sudan's military (SAF) and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF) clashed in the capital Khartoum on 15 April. While the conflict in Khartoum continues as repeated ceasefires have failed, conflict has also spread to the Darfur and North Kordofan regions. Overall, more than 736,000 people have been displaced by the conflict in these three areas, and at least 600 people have been killed amid the fighting, with more than 5,100 injured, although actual figures may be higher. Critical services, including health, water and electricity have been disrupted, while conflict and access challenges persist. Over 177,000 people have also fled Sudan amid the fighting, and we have classified refugee arrivals by country separately.

On 27 April, a measles outbreak was reported in Blue Nile state in southeastern Sudan, with Ihskap and Madit localities in Kurmuk district most affected. The first suspected case was reported on 15 March. So far, at least 458 cases have been reported, with 185 confirmed cases and 95 deaths. Children between 5-10 years old are most affected. Kurmuk, located in southern Blue Nile, is an area where non-state armed groups are active and have varying levels of control over territory in the area.

The Gu seasonal (April-June) rains in Somalia have caused flooding in several parts of Somalia, particularly Hirshabelle, Jubaland and South West states. Initial estimates indicate that at least 328,000 people have been affected, of whom more than 164,000 have been displaced. The floods have destroyed shelters, latrines, farmland, washed away livestock, temporarily closed schools and damaged health facilities.

As the conflict in Sudan continues, more than 177,000 people have fled Sudan. At least 60,000 Sudanese refugees have arrived in Chad – including at least 30,000 in the past few days. More refugees are likely to arrive as the situation in Sudan continues.

Following months of tensions over a transition to a civilian government, Sudan's military (SAF) and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF) clashed in the capital Khartoum on 15 April. Conflict between Sudan's military (SAF) and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF), which began in Khartoum on 15 April, has continued - displacing over 843,000 people within Sudan. An additional 64,000 people have been displaced to SPLM-N areas of South Kordofan that are only partially accessible from the rest of Sudan. We have classified internal displacements in Sudan and refugee movements to neighboring countries separately.

In Zamfara State in northwestern Nigeria, attacks by armed bandits over the past month have led to over 7,000 displacements since March, 2023. Banditry in Zamfara has additionally resulted in restricted mobility, disrupted livelihood activities, and limited humanitarian access.

Since late February, over 13,000 refugees fleeing violence in Burkina Faso arrived in the regions of Tchologo and Boukani in northern and northeastern Côte d'Ivoire. Immediate needs include water, education, medical care, and protection.

Cyclone Mocha  hit the coastal areas along the Bangladesh-Myanmar border on 14 May, bringing heavy rainfall, strong winds, and causing landslides in some areas, and hitting western Rakhine state worst in Myanmar. At least 20,000 people have been displaced with 7.9 million people affected by the cyclone. Assessments are ongoing to identify urgent needs for affected populations, and the classification will be updated as more information about the humanitarian impacts emerge. Please note that we have classified the cyclone impacts in Bangladesh separately.

Armed attacks in Burkina Faso's northern Sahel region continue, particuarly around the town of Djibo in Soum province. Since March, over 3,000 migrants have been expelled from Algeria and are stranded in the Agadez region of northern Niger. Most migrants are from West African countries, and are in need of shelter, water, food, and protection services.

Violent clashes between tribal minorities and the non-tribal majority group in India's northeastern state of Manipur have resulted in at least 75 deaths and 45,000 people displaced. The clashes began after the Kuki tribal group held an organized protest on 03 May.

On 03 June, heavy rain caused severe flooding and landslides, displacing over 19,000 people. The flooding affected 5 of Haiti's 10 departments, with particularly severe damage around the capital city, Port-au-Prince.The torrential rains and flooding are exacerbating the humanitarian situation in Haiti, where nearly one half of the population is in need of assistance.

As the conflict in Sudan continues, at least 99,000 refugees have fled to South Sudan, with more expected to arrive as the situation progresses. Please note we are classifying the situation in Sudan and refugee movement to other countries separately.

Since the beginning of Apirl, 13,000 people have been displaced by flooding caused by heavy rainfall in the Gatumba area of Northern Burundi. Homes, buildings, agricultural fields, and latrines hav been destroyed by the floods.

As the conflict in Sudan continues,more than 350,000 people have fled Sudan. At least 169,000 refugees from Sudan have arrived in Egypt since the conflict began, largely through the Argeen land port on the border of Sudan and Egypt. More refugees are likely to arrive as the situation in Sudan continues.

Since April 2023, clashes in Plateau State in north-central Nigeria have resulted in over 20,000 displacements and 100 deaths. The clashes and attacks have been concentrated in Mangu Local Government Area in Plateau State.

On 06 June, the Nova Kakhovka dam in the Kherson region of southern Ukraine collapsed, flooding the surrounding areas. The floods have displaced at least 16,000 people while over 180,000 people are affected. Over 25,000 people in the Russian-controlled left bank of the Dnipro River cannot access humanitarian services.
