In the past three months alone, nearly 4,000 people have been killed and more than 11,000 injured, with more affected since the start of the conflict, though these figures are likely higher. At least 100,000 people have also been displaced within the past 90 days. Moreover, nearly 350,000 people are facing Catastrophe (IPC 5) levels of food insecurity, with an additional 876,000 facing emergency (IPC 4), while humanitarian access remains severely constrained.

Nonstate armed groups launched their largest offensive in several years on 27 November, targeting government-held territory in the northwest of the country. Government forces collapsed, allowing armed groups to take control of nearly all of the second city Aleppo without major fighting, although Russian and government forces have responded with airstrikes. Nearly 50,000 people have already been displaced. The situation is evolving rapidly and so we will reclassify this emergency as more data becomes available.

Nonstate armed groups launched their largest offensive in several years on 27 November, quickly taking control of government-held territory.  After government forces collapsed, allowing armed groups to take control of Aleppo, they have moved south, taking the 4th largest city, Hama, and are heading towards the strategic city of Homs. At least 370,000 people have already been displaced. The situation is evolving rapidly and so we will reclassify this emergency as more data becomes available.

On 15 December, Cyclone Chido made landfall in northern Mozambique, displacing nearly 150,000 people. Cabo Delgado, Mozambique's northern-most province, was most affected, and also has significant access constraints due to armed group activity.

Since late September, nearly 7,000 people hae been displaced and over 300 killed due to conflict with armed groups in Borno State, in northeast Nigeria. In addition, over 6,000 people were affected by flooding in late November after water was released from a dam in Cameroon.

The conflict in Sudan between Sudan's military (SAF) and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF) has continued and intensified in parts of the country, particularly North Darfur, Khartoum, and Al Jazirah states. In the past three months, conflict throughout the country has displaced over 640,000 people and left more than 8 million people in emergency or IPC4 levels of food insecurity, with over 630,000 people facing catastrophic or IPC5 food insecurity. Nearly half of the population is facing IPC 3 or higher food insecurity

Since early October, nearly 260,000 people have arrived in eastern Chad, fleeing the conflict in Sudan. Intense fighting in North Darfur state in Sudan, in particular, has sparked further displacement to eastern Chad.

The escalation in violence in Haiti has displaced over 103,000 people since October and killed nearly 1,000 others. Non-state armed group activity has also constrained food systems throughout the country, leaving over 2 million people facing IPC4 levels of food insecurity, with nearly 6,000 people facing catastrophic (IPC5) levels of food insecurity. As armed groups have announced their intent to escalate the situation, further displacement is expected in the coming months. In additional, over 90,000 Haitians have been forcibly displaced from the Dominican Republic in this time period
Since October 2024, more than 113,000 refugees from Sudan have fled to Libya as conditions in Sudan worsen. Most refugees are in Alkufra in southeastern Libya, which borders Sudan.

Since late October, over 9,000 cholera cases have been reported in South Sudan from 7 states throughout the country, mostly in the north as well as Juba county in the south. The outbreak has been exacerbated by  recent flooding as well as over-crowded conditions for refugees and returnees from Sudan in areas near the border.

Since October 2023, Israel has launched airstrikes and ground invasions in Gaza, killing over 45,000 and displacing nearly 2 million people. In the past three months alone, over 3,500 people have been killed and nearly 11,000 injured, with more affected since the start of the conflict, though these figures are likely higher. At least 195,000 people have also been displaced within the past 90 days. Moreover, nearly 350,000 people are facing Catastrophe (IPC 5) levels of food insecurity, with an additional 876,000 facing emergency (IPC 4), while humanitarian access remains severely constrained.

Since late October, a surge in attacks by armed groups have displaced over 735,000 people and killed at least 300 in North Kivu in the eastern DRC. Furthermore, over 900,000 people are experiencing IPC4 levels of food insecurity within North Kivu.

Since late December, the Afar and Oromia regions in northern Ethiopia have been experiencing a series of earthquakes - with at least 50 ranging from 4.2 to 5.8 magnitude - in early January alone. The earthquakes have displaced at least 60,000 people and affected over 80,000.

On 10 January, Sierra Leone confirmed its first Mpox case since the WHO declared it a public health emergency. Since 10 January, at least 82 suspected cases have been reported with 8 confirmed. Confirmed cases are spread throughout 6 districts, most in the western part of the country.

Following the rainy season to late december 2024, heavy rainfall has caused flooding  across most of Burundi. The provinces of Rutana, Ruyigi, Bujumbura rural and Bujumbura Mairie are most affected. nearly 7,000 people have been displaced with over 24,000 affected.

Following the shutdown of the CBP One app, which allowed people on the move to schedule appointments in the United States, over 300,000 asylum seekers, including Haitians, Venezuelans, Cubans, and others, are now stuck on Mexico's northern border. Unable to return to their country of origin largely due to unsafe conditions, most are now staying in informal settlements or otherwise sleeping outside, facing freezing temperatures and high levels of protection risks.

Clashes between armed groups in Catatumbo region of northern Colombia, on the border with Venezuela, have displaced over 32,000 and resulted in at least 100 deaths. The main armed groups involved are the National Liberation Army (ELN) and Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC), which are vying for control over the border with Venezuela.

Following the fall of Bashir al-Assad in Syria in December, over 87,000 people fled to Baalbek, Lebanon from Syria for safety. Most are experiencing protection risks and in need of humanitarian assistance.

Since late October, a surge in attacks by armed groups have displaced nearly 1.8 million people and killed at least 475 in North and South Kivu in the eastern DRC. Furthermore, over 1 million people are experiencing IPC4 levels of food insecurity within North and South Kivu.

On 20 January, Tanzania has declared a Marburg Virus Disease (MVD) Outbreak, with at least two confirmed and 29 suspected cases and 8 deaths. All cases are in the Kagera region of Northwestern Tanzania, near the borders of Rwanda, Burundi, and Uganda, increasing the risk of its spread across borders. MVD is a highly contagious disease that causes haemorrhagic fever, similar to Ebola virus. There are no approved treatments or vaccines.

In late November, nonstate armed groups launched an offensive, quickly taking control of government land and eventually toppling the Assad regime in December. As a result, at least 627,000 people were newly displaced during the offensive and remain displaced still. Over 40% of those displaced are in Idleb governorate in northwest Syria.

On 30 January, the Uganda department of health declared an Ebola outbreak. Since then, at least 9 people have tested positive and at least one - a healthcare worker - has died.  High risk areas include Kampala, Wakiso, and Mbale districts.

The conflict in Sudan between Sudan's military (SAF) and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF) has continued and intensified in parts of the country, particularly North Darfur, Khartoum, and Al Jazirah states. In the past three months throughout the country has displaced over 226,000 people and left more than 8 million people in emergency or IPC4 levels of food insecurity, with over 630,000 people facing catastrophic or IPC5 food insecurity. Nearly half of the population is facing IPC 3 or higher food insecurity.

Since October 2023, Israel has launched airstrikes and ground invasions in Gaza, killing over 40,000 and displacing nearly 2 million people. In the past three months alone, nearly over 4,500 people have been killed and more than 8,500 injured. At least 100,000 people have also been displaced within the past 90 days. Despite the ceasefire, needs remain high with, nearly 350,000 people facing Catastrophe (IPC 5) levels of food insecurity, and 876,000 facing emergency (IPC 4).

Over 7,900 Nigerians who had been previously displaced to Chad, are returning to norrthern Nigeria. They will stay in Gubio IDP camp in Maiduguri, where they are in need of protection, health, nutrition, and other services.

Since the beginning of 2025, at least 44,000 people have fled from the DRC to Burundi. Nearly 33,000 of those have fled South Kivu in the past week alone.  Most have arrived to Rugombo, a city in northwestern Burundi near the border with the DRC. Please note that the situation in the DRC is being classified separately.

Since 10 January, there have been at least 147 cases of Mpox reported in Sierra Leone. Cases were first reported in the Western Areas but have spread to Moyamba, Bo, Tonkolili, and Bombali districts. Nearly 75% of all cases are in children 15 years and younger.

Since late November, over 26,000 cholera cases and nearly 450 deaths have been reported in South Sudan across 7 states, with over half of cases reported in Rubkona county. The outbreak has been exacerbated by recent flooding as well as over-crowded conditions for refugees and returnees from Sudan in areas near the border

Since 21 January, Israeli forces have escalated operations in the West Bank, killing at least 51 people and displacing 40,000 Palestinian refugees from multiple camps including Jenin, Tulkarm, Nur Shams,  and El Far'a. The Israeli military says residents of the camp will not be permitted to return.

Clashes between armed groups in Catatumbo region of northern Colombia, on the border with Venezuela, have displaced at least 53,000 people and left tens of thousands of others without access to humanitarian aid. The main armed groups involved are the National Liberation Army (ELN) and Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC), which are vying for control over the border with Venezuela.

Escalation in the eastern DRC has displaced at least 2.3 million people and killed over 7,000 as Rwandan-backed armed group, M23, advanced on and captured key cities in North and South Kivu.  Hundreds of thousands of others remained trapped in M23 controlled areas, without access to humanitarian aid. Furthermore, over 1 million people are experiencing IPC4 levels of food insecurity within North and South Kivu

On 20 January, Tanzania has declared a Marburg Virus Disease (MVD) Outbreak. There have been two confirmed and eight probable cases, all of which resulted in death. All cases are in the Kagera region of Northwestern Tanzania, near the borders of Rwanda, Burundi, and Uganda, increasing the risk of its spread across borders. MVD is a highly contagious disease that causes hemorrhagic fever, similar to Ebola virus. There are no approved treatments or vaccines.

Following the fall of Bashir al-Assad in Syria in December, over 103,000 people fled to Lebanon for safety, including at least 16,000 people who fled violence in Syria's coastal provinces, Tartous and Latakia, in early in March.  Most are experiencing protection risks and in need of humanitarian assistance.

Since late December, over 23,000 people have been displaced to Gorom-Gorom commune in the Sahel region of northern Burkina Faso due to increasing insecurity. Many of those displaced are also facing high levels of malnutrition and food insecurity.

The conflict in Sudan between Sudan's military (SAF) and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF) has continued and intensified in parts of the country, particularly North Darfur, Khartoum, and Al Jazirah states. This has resulted in massive food insecurity, with over 630,000 people facing catastrophic or IPC5 levels of food insecurity, and more than 8 million people in emergency or IPC4 levels. At least 50,000 people have been displaced by fighting as well, although the true figure is likely higher.

Since the beginning of 2025, at least 69,000 people have fled from violence in eastern DRC to neighboring Burundi. Most have arrived to Rugombo, in north-west Burundi, near the border with the DRC. Note that the situation in the DRC is being classified separately.

Since the ceasefire between Israel and Hamas collapsed in March, Israel has expanded its offensive in Gaza, displacing at least 124,000 people in the past few weeks alone. Furthermore, Israeli airstrikes and attacks have killed over 4,600 people since late December. Meanwhile, needs remain high with, nearly 350,000 people facing Catastrophe (IPC 5) levels of food insecurity, and 876,000 facing emergency (IPC 4).

A fire broke out in Bri Mashamari IDP camp in Konduga, Borno state in northeast Nigeria on 23 March. 75% of the camp was burnt completely, leaving 12,600 people homeless. Water storage facilities, food, livestock and an IRC child protection safe space were all destroyed as well.

There has been a rapid rise in cases and deaths in a meningitis outbreak affecting four local government areas of Kebbi state in northwest Nigeria. An outbreak was first declared in late January and, since then, 653 cases and 56 deaths have been reported.
Since end of December 2024 the number of Sudanese nationals who have fled their country to Chad has risen by around 74,000, to reach a total of nearly 1.2 million people.

An earthquake was felt across Myanmar at around 1pm local time on Friday March 28th. Initial reports suggested it had a magnitude of 7.7 but the Thai government subsequently issued a higher assessment of 8.2. A number of smaller after shocks have also been reported. Tremors from the original quake were felt as far away as Bangkok, in neighboring Thailand (1,000km away). Reports are still coming in but there appears to be widespread destruction in the national capital, Naypyidaw, and the country's second city, Mandalay. Of particular concern, the epicenter of the earthquake was in Sagaing, an area that has seen significant conflict over the past few years, where there were already high levels of need, displacement and destruction of infrastructure, and where humanitarian access is extremely constrained.

Over 140 people have been killed and 730 injured but we expect these figures to rise. We will update this classification as we get more information about the impacts of the earthquake.

In early February, fighting between the South Sudanese military and an armed group called the White Army in Upper Nile province, broke out and has since escalated. After an attack on an army barrack in Upper Nile, government forces have launched attacks on civilian areas, displacing at least 63,000 people. The White Army is aligned with Vice President Machar, former rival of President Kiir, causing concern that the current violence will ignite a new civil war. We will monitor the situation carefully and update this classification as dictated by events.

A 7.7 magnitude earthquake was felt across Myanmar at around 1pm local time on Friday March 28th, followed by several aftershocks. Tremors from the original quake were felt as far away as Bangkok, in neighboring Thailand (1,000km away). There has been widespread destruction in the country's second city, Mandalay, as well as the national capital Naypyidaw. Of particular concern, the epicenter of the earthquake was in Sagaing, an area that has seen significant conflict over the past few years, where there were already high levels of need, displacement and destruction of infrastructure, and where humanitarian access is extremely constrained.

Over 1,000 people have already been reported as killed and over 2,000 injured but we expect these figures to continue rising rapidly as the situation on the ground becomes clearer. Major damage to hospitals and other infrastructure has also been reported. We will update this classification as we get more information about the impacts of the earthquake.

A 7.7 magnitude earthquake was felt across Myanmar at around 1pm local time on Friday March 28th, followed by several aftershocks. Tremors from the original quake were felt as far away as Bangkok, in neighboring Thailand (1,000km away). There has been widespread destruction in the country's second city, Mandalay, as well as the national capital Naypyidaw. Of particular concern, the epicenter of the earthquake was in Sagaing, an area that has seen significant conflict over the past few years, where there were already high levels of need, displacement and destruction of infrastructure, and where humanitarian access is extremely constrained. Over 3,350 people have already been reported as killed and over 4,800 injured but we expect these figures to continue rising rapidly as the situation on the ground becomes clearer. Furthermore, at least 15,000 homes have been destroyed, leaving over 60,000 without shelter. We will update this classification as we get more information about the impacts of the earthquake.

Since 21 January, Israeli forces have escalated operations in the West Bank, killing at least 101 people and displacing 40,000 Palestinian refugees from multiple camps including Jenin, Tulkarm, Nur Shams,  and El Far'a. The Israeli military says residents of the camp will not be permitted to return.

Since late January, flooding in the Lac region of western Chad has displaced over 32,000 people. Liwa and Daboua sub-prefectures are particularly affected, with thousands of homes, cattle, and cropland destroyed.

In North and South Kivu in the eastern DRC, over 1.5 million people are facing emergency (IPC4) levels of food insecurity. This is exacerbated by the ongoing conflict between Rwandan-backed armed group, M23, and the Congolese armed forces in these regions, which have left millions displaced. Note that the conflict has been classified separately.

Since mid-January, over 190,000 Afghans have been forcibly deported, or have returned under threat of being arrested and forcibly deported, from Iran and Pakistan back to Afghanistan. While forced deportations have been ongoing in both Iran and Pakistan, they have increased in the past month in particular. Please note that those being forcibly deportation or returning out of fear of deportation are considered as displaced in the emergency classification system as they are not returning voluntarily and are arriving to a situation with high humanitarian needs.

Since late January, over 132,000 people have been displaced in Ituri province in eastern DRC due to communal conflict involving the non-state armed group, CODECO, which has increasingly also come into conflict with Ugandan troops deployed to Ituri.  Furthermore, over 300,000 people are experiencing IPC4 levels of food insecurity in Ituri. Please note conflict and food insecurity in North and South Kivu have been classified separately.
Flash flooding through Somalia has affected nearly 30,000 people, with most of those being in Puntland. More flooding is likely as the Gu rainy season (April to June) continues.

Multiple fires have broken out in IDP camps throughout Borno state in northeast Nigeria. The fires have left at least 17,000 people homeless, and destroyed water storage facilities, food, livestock, as well as an IRC child protection safe space in one of the affected locations. Over half of those displaced by the fires were in Konduga local government area (LGA), while the others were in Monguno LGA.  Furthermore, over 150,000 people are facing IPC4 levels of food insecurity in Borno and Yobe states in NE Nigeria

Over 526,000 people are facing emergency, or IPC4, levels of food insecurity in states throughout northwest Nigeria, including Zamfara, Sokoto, and Katsina. Worsening food insecurity is driven by persistent insecurity in these regions, including assaults on farmers, kidnapping, and banditry.

In early February, fighting between the South Sudanese military and an armed group called the White Army in Upper Nile state broke out and has since escalated.  At least 130,000 people have been displaced in Upper Nile and Jonglei states, although clashes have spread to several other states as well. The White Army is aligned with Vice President Machar, former rival of President Kiir, causing concern that the current violence will ignite a new civil war. We will monitor the situation carefully and update this classification as necessary

Since early February, over one million Afghans have been forcibly deported, or have returned under threat of being arrested and forcibly deported, from Iran and Pakistan back to Afghanistan. While forced deportations have been ongoing in both Iran and Pakistan, they have increased in the past month in particular. Please note that those being forcibly deported or returning out of fear of deportation are considered as displaced in the emergency classification system as they are not returning voluntarily and are arriving to a situation with high humanitarian needs.

Sudan is facing massive food insecurity, with over 630,000 people experiencing catastrophic or IPC5 levels of food insecurity, and more than 8 million people in emergency or IPC4 levels. Nearly half of the population is facing IPC 3 or higher food insecurity. This is largely driven by the ongoing conflict between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF). Please note, the conflict is now being classified separately from food insecurity to better track new developments.

The para-military Rapid Support Forces (RSF) have taken control of Zamzam camp, outside of North Darfur capital, El Fasher. Since 10 April, the RSF has escalated attacks on Zamzam, and nearby Abu Shouk camp, displacing at least 400,000 people and killing over 450. This classification will continue to be updated as the situation evolves. Please note, food insecurity in Sudan has been classified separately (as SD360) from the conflict to better track new developments in the conflict.

There has been a rapid rise in meningitis cases and deaths in northwest Nigeria, with at least 2,170 cases reported and nearly 100 deaths since March. Cases have been reported in 23 states across Nigeria, but Kebbi, Sokoto, and Katsina in NW Nigeria have the highest caseloads.

Since the ceasefire between Israel and Hamas collapsed in March, Israel has expanded their offensive in Gaza and restricted all humanitarian aid, resulting in an increase in food insecurity. The entire population is facing crisis (IPC3) levels or higher of food insecurity, with at least 469,000 people experiencing catastrophic (IPC5) levels, and over 1.1 million in emergency (IPC4) levels of food insecurity. The IPC assessment warns that famine is likely given the renewed hostilities, displacement of people, and lack of humanitarian access. Please note, conflict is being classified separately from food insecurity to better track developments. Food insecurity will remain classified as PS334.

Since April, flooding throughout Somalia has affected 108,000 people, leaving over 12,000 displaced throughout Jubaland, Hirshabelle, South West, Puntland, Gulmudug and Banadir regions. More flooding is likely as the Gu rainy season (April to June) continues.

A 7.7 magnitude earthquake was felt across Myanmar at around 1pm local time on Friday March 28th, followed by several aftershocks. Impacts were felt throughout the country but are particularly severe in Sagaing, the epicenter of the earthquake and where there has been significant conflict and limited humanitarian access over the past few years. The earthquake killed at least 3,800 people and injured over 5,700. At least 44,000 homes have been totally destroyed, leaving over 200,000 people without shelter.

Since the collapse of the ceasefire between Israel and Hamas in March, Israel has renewed attacks on Gaza, resulting in at least 436,000 people displaced since 18 March. Furthermore, over 4,600 people have been killed by Israeli airstrikes and attacks since early February, with over 8,100 others injured. Please note, food insecurity caused by the restriction of humanitarian aid in Gaza is classified as PS334. Conflict is being classified separately to better track new developments.

Since mid-February, over 2,800 cases of mpox were reported throughout Sierra Leone. The outbreak has been ongoing since January, but the number of cases reported has spiked over the past few weeks. Cases were first reported in the Western Areas but have spread throughout the country. Just over half of all cases reported are in healthcare workers.

In North and South Kivu in the eastern DRC, over 1.5 million people are facing emergency (IPC4) levels of food insecurity.  Food insecurity was exacerbated by  ongoing conflict between Rwandan-backed armed group, M23, and the Congolese armed forces in these regions, which have left millions displaced. Note that changes in the conflict will be classified separately.

Over 815,000 people are facing emergency, or IPC4, levels of food insecurity in states throughout northwest Nigeria, including Zamfara, Sokoto, and Katsina. Worsening food insecurity is driven by persistent insecurity in these regions. While insecurity in NW Nigeria is largely comprised of attacks on farmers, kidnapping, and banditry, there are also reports sustained violent campaigns by organized armed groups with links to the Islamic State (IS).

In early February, fighting between the South Sudanese military and an armed group called the White Army in Upper Nile state broke out and has since escalated and spread. Since March, at least 208,000 people have been displaced, mostly in Upper Nile and Jonglei States. The White Army is aligned with Vice President Machar, former rival of President Kiir, causing concern that the current violence will ignite a new civil war. We will monitor the situation carefully and update this classification as dictated by events

Since March, over 1.2 million Afghans have been forcibly deported, or have returned under threat of being arrested and forcibly deported, from Iran and Pakistan back to Afghanistan. While forced deportations have been ongoing in both Iran and Pakistan, they have increased in the past month in particular. Please note that those being forcibly deported or returning out of fear of deportation are considered as displaced in the emergency classification system as they are not returning voluntarily and are arriving to a situation with high humanitarian needs.

The para-military Rapid Support Forces (RSF) have taken control of Zamzam camp, outside of North Darfur capital, El Fasher. Since 10 April, the RSF has escalated attacks on Zamzam, and nearby Abu Shouk camp, displacing at least 425,000 people and killing over 450. This classification will continue to be updated as the situation evolves. Please note, food insecurity in Sudan has been classified separately (as SD360) from the conflict to better track new developments in the conflict.

In early May, an armed group, JNIM, in Burkina Faso launched a series of coordinated attacks aimed at population centers in the Sahel province, largely targeting the northern town of Djibo. Djibo has been blockaded since 2022, meaning civilians could not leave the city for safety.  Needs in Djibo are extremely high given the lack of humanitarian access and ongoing violence.

Violence between farming and herding communities in Benue, central Nigeria, has led to over 300 deaths and displaced around 12,000 people since late March. Conflict of this kind flares up sporadically in central Nigeria but it is a growing risk as climate change and environmental degradation pushes herders further south.

Since the ceasefire between Israel and Hamas collapsed in March, Israel has expanded their offensive in Gaza and restricted all humanitarian aid, resulting in an increase in food insecurity. The entire population is facing crisis (IPC3) levels or higher of food insecurity, with at least 469,000 people experiencing catastrophic (IPC5) levels, and over 1.1 million in emergency (IPC4) levels of food insecurity. The IPC assessment warns that famine is likely given the renewed hostilities, displacement of people, and lack of humanitarian access. Please note, conflict is being classified separately from food insecurity to better track developments. Food insecurity will remain classified as PS334.

Since the collapse of the ceasefire between Israel and Hamas on 18 March 2025, Israel has renewed attacks on Gaza, resulting in at least 680,000 people displaced. Furthermore, over 1,600 people have been killed and more than 5,000 injured over the same time period - bringing the total killed since October 2023 to over 52,000. Please note, food insecurity caused by the restriction of humanitarian aid in Gaza is classified as PS334. Conflict is being classified separately to better track new developments

Nearly 481,000 people are experiencing emergency (IPC4) levels of food insecurity in the Central African Republic. Food insecurity is driven by conflict, poor agricultural production, and other shocks, with displaced populations being particularly impacted.

Since early July, at least three tropical waves have made landfall in Venezuela, causing intense flooding throughout the western part of the country. Flooding has affected over 266,000 people, destroyed infrastructure in urban areas, and heightened humanitarian needs. Needs are particularly high in Apure state, where humanitarian access is severely limited.  New data is still coming in about the impacts of the flooding and so we will monitor the situation and update this classification if necessary.

South Sudan is grappling with multiple crises. Fighting between the national military and an armed group that began in Upper Nile state has spread, displacing at least 190,000 people in the past three months, mainly in Upper Nile, Jonglei, and Warrap states. The conflict and resulting displacement have exacerbated a cholera outbreak, with over 30,000 new cases reported in the past three months, and deepened food insecurity. At least 83,000 people face catastrophic (IPC Phase 5) food insecurity, while 1.4 million are in emergency (IPC Phase 4) conditions, primarily in areas also affected by conflict and cholera.

Since mid-April, the para-military Rapid Support Forces (RSF) have escalated attacks on North Darfur capital, Al Fasher, and have taken control of nearby Zamzam IDP camp.  These attacks have displaced over 523,000 people and killed at least 1,175 others. Most of those displaced have gone to Tawila, a nearby town in North Darfur. Furthermore, North Darfur is seeing an increase in cholera cases, particularly in Tawila, due to overcrowded conditions for people who have been displaced. The cholera outbreak is expected to worsen as the rainy season begins. Data and access remain limited in North Darfur and this classification will be updated when possible.

Since 12 July, clashes between Druze and Bedouin armed groups in Syria’s As-Suwayda governorate escalated into broader violence after the Syrian army intervened, resulting in over 300 deaths and the displacement of at least 6,500 people. In response, Israel launched airstrikes on Syria in both As-Suwayda and Damascus, further intensifying the conflict; the situation remains fluid and classifications will be updated accordingly.

Since 12 July, clashes between Druze and Bedouin armed groups in Syria’s As-Suwayda governorate escalated into broader violence after the Syrian army intervened, resulting in over 300 deaths and the displacement of at nearly 80,000 people. In response, Israel launched airstrikes on Syria in both As-Suwayda and Damascus, further intensifying the conflict; the situation remains fluid and classifications will be updated accordingly.

Since April, over 2 million Afghans have been forcibly deported, or have returned under threat of being arrested and forcibly deported, from Iran and Pakistan back to Afghanistan. While forced deportations have been ongoing in both Iran and Pakistan, they have increased in the past month in particular. Please note that those being forcibly deported or returning out of fear of deportation are considered as displaced in the emergency classification system as they are not returning voluntarily and are arriving to a situation with high humanitarian needs.

From May through July, armed groups in Burkina Faso launched a series of coordinated attacks aimed at population centers in the Sahel province. At least 6,100 people have been displaced by such attacks throughout the Sahel, with many moving towards Dori, in Seno province of the Sahel.  Attacks have also targeted the town of Djibo which has been blockaded since 2022, meaning civilians could not leave the city for safety.  Needs throughout the Sahel region are extremely high given the lack of humanitarian access and ongoing violence.

Since May, over 224,000 people have been displaced in Ituri province of eastern DRC due to armed attacks, mostly by the non-state armed group CODECO. Most displacements occurred in Djugu territory, as well as Irumu and Mahagi.

Since July 23 and following a weeks of tensions, armed clashes on the Thailand-Cambodia border have resulted in at least 138,000 people displaced, 19 deaths, and 130 injuries in Thailand. This prompted the government to declare martial law in eight border districts across Chanthaburi and Trat provinces.

Over 1.5 million people are experiencing emergency (IPC4) levels of food insecurity across government-controlled areas of Yemen. Yemen's food insecurity crisis is driven by economic shocks and natural hazards that have interrupted agricultural production, alongside extreme cuts to humanitarian aid. Food insecurity is expected to worsen in September. Please note, food insecurity in northern Yemen will be classified separately.

Since June, nearly 90,000 people have been displaced in Somalia’s Hiraan and Gedo regions due to armed clashes. In Hiraan, fighting between federal forces and Al-Shabaab displaced 62,000, while clashes between federal and Jubaland forces in Gedo displaced nearly 28,000 others

Israel's expanded offensive in Gaza and restrictions on humanitarian aid have led to a severe increase in food insecurity. In late July, the IPC announced famine is unfolding in Gaza, with least 469,000 people facing catastrophic (IPC5) levels of food insecurity and 1.1 facing emergency (IPC4) levels.  Please note, conflict is being classified separately from food insecurity to better track developments. Food insecurity will continue to be classified under PS334.

Since 13 July, at least 187 suspected cholera cases have been reported in Ouaddai province of eastern Chad, with at least 10 deaths. Most cases were reported from Dougi camp, which houses refugees from neighboring Sudan. The upcoming rainy season and further arrival of refugees is likely to exacerbate the outbreak

At least 50,000 people have arrived to Ethiopia's Gambella region after fleeing violence in South Sudan's Upper Nile state.  Furthermore, at least 560 cases of cholera have been reported in the past 3 months in Gambella, including in camp settings.

Between 20 and 28 July, armed attacks in Cabo Delgado province of northern Mozambique displaced over 46,000 people. The majority of those displaced were from Chiúre district in Cabo Delgado, and nearly half of all people displaced were children.

Since late May, over 500 cases of cholera and 20 deaths have been reported throughout four districts in Côte d'Ivoire. Yopougon East and Port-Bouët-Vridi, districts around the capital city, Abidjan, are reporting the highest case loads, followed by Sassandra and Jacqueville.

In late July, armed clashes on the border of Thailand and Cambodia displaced nearly 154,000 people in Cambodia. A ceasefire between Thailand and Cambodia has been reached, but tensions are still high. Please note, impacts in Thailand are classified separately under TH458.

Flash floods from heavy monsoon rains that started on Thursday have affected tens of thousands of people in Pakistan's northwest Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province. Flooding has killed nearly 400 people, and hundreds of others remain missing, while damage to roads has hampered search and rescue operations. Further rains are expected.

Nearly 481,000 people are experiencing emergency (IPC4) levels of food insecurity in the Central African Republic. Food insecurity is driven by conflict, poor agricultural production, and other shocks, and displaced populations are particularly impacted.

Since early July, at least three tropical waves have made landfall in Venezuela, causing intense flooding throughout the western part of the country. Flooding has affected over 307,000 people, destroyed infrastructure in urban areas, and heightened humanitarian needs. Needs are particularly high in Apure state, where humanitarian access is severely limited.  New data is still coming in about the impacts of the flooding and so we will monitor the situation and update this classification if necessary.

Over 3.7 million people are experiencing emergency (IPC4) levels of food insecurity across Ansar Allah (AA) controlled areas in Northern Yemen. Yemen's food insecurity crisis is driven by conflict and natural hazards that have interrupted agricultural production, alongside extreme cuts to humanitarian aid. Please note, this is classified as White indicating the IRC cannot respond due to the Foreign Terrorist Organization designation (FTO) of AA by the US government which prevents the IRC from operating in this part of Yemen. Food insecurity in government-controlled areas has been classified separately as YE456.

In mid-July, clashes between Druze and Bedouin armed groups in Syria’s As-Suwayda governorate escalated into broader violence after the Syrian army intervened, resulting in over 1,100 deaths and the displacement of over 190,000 people. In response, Israel launched airstrikes on Syria in both As-Suwayda and Damascus, further intensifying the conflict. Although violence has largely subsided since a ceasefire was agreed to in July, tensions remain high.
Efforts to contain a diphtheria outbreak in Somalia have been undermined by vaccine shortages and aid cuts. Over 1,700 cases have been recorded in the last three months, leading to 58 deaths.

Since late August, armed clashes in Ghana have displaced about 9,000 people to Bounkani region in the northeast of Cote d'Ivoire, which neighbors Ghana. Internal displacement in Ghana will be classified separately.

On 31 August 2025, a 6.0+ magnitude earthquake struck Southeastern Afghanistan, near the Pakistan border. At least 800 people have been killed and 2,000 injured across Nangarhar, Kunar, Laghman, and Nuristan. Over 12,000 people are directly affected. Landslides and blocked roads are hampering access to remote areas

On 31 August 2025, a 6.0+ magnitude earthquake struck Southeastern Afghanistan, near the Pakistan border. At least 1,400 people have been killed, 3,000 people injured, and 8,000 houses destroyed across Nangarhar, Kunar, Laghman, and Nuristan . Landslides and blocked roads are hampering access to remote areas.

Since 13 July, at least 1,200 suspected cholera cases have been reported in Ouaddai and Sila provinces in eastern Chad, with 86 deaths. The outbreak is already spreading rapidly and the upcoming rainy season and further arrival of refugees is likely to further exacerbate the outbreak.

The health ministry of the DRC has declared an Ebola outbreak in Bulape health zone of Kaisa province in central DRC. Since 20 August, at least 28 cases with 15 deaths have been recorded,  including two deaths of healthcare workers.

Flash floods from heavy monsoon rains that began on Thursday have devastated Pakistan’s northwest Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province. At least 829 people have been killed and hundreds remain missing, while damage to roads has severely hampered search and rescue operations. Overall, more than 1.5 million people have been affected, with further rains expected in the coming days

On 31 August 2025, a massive landslide struck western Sudan’s Marra Mountains following weeks of heavy rainfall. The disaster completely destroyed the village of Tarasin, killing at least 370 people, with estimates upwards of 1,000 as many are still trapped under debris or were swept away by floodwaters.

Over 1.6 million people are experiencing emergency (IPC4) levels of food insecurity across government controlled areas of Yemen. Yemen's food insecurity crisis is driven by economic shocks and natural hazards that have interuppted agricultural production, alongside extreme cuts to humanitarian aid. Furthermore, heavy rains in late August have affected over 165,000 people in southern Yemen.  Please note, food insecurity in northern Yemen is classified separately as YE459.

Since mid-June, over 370,000 people have been displaced in Ituri province of eastern DRC due to armed attacks, mostly by the non-state armed group CODECO. Most displacements occurred in Djugu territory, as well as Irumu and Mahagi.

Israel's expanded offensive in Gaza and restrictions on humanitarian aid have led to a severe increase in food insecurity. In late July, the IPC announced famine is unfolding in Gaza, with over 640,000 people facing catastrophic (IPC5) levels of food insecurity and 1.1 facing emergency (IPC4) levels.  Please note, conflict is being classified separately from food insecurity to better track developments. Food insecurity will continue to be classified under PS334.

Since mid-June, at least 640 cholera cases have been reported in Bujumbura province of Burundi, with a spike in cases occuring early September. Most cases are in Cobitoke and Bujumbura Nord districts. Ongoing rains and floods, limited health system capacity, and movement of refugees and returnees through Burundi are expected to exacerbate the outbreak.

The security situation in the NW and SW regions of Cameroon is tense, with armed groups imposing a lock-down, restricting movement for 4.5 million people. Amid the lockdown, however, displacement due to armed clashes continues, and at least 4,000 people were displaced in Menchum Valley in NW region in July.

Since 13 July, at least 2,614 cholera cases have been reported in Ouaddai, Guera, and Sila provinces in eastern Chad, with 148 deaths. The outbreak is already spreading rapidly, and the rainy season and further arrival of refugees is likely to further exacerbate the outbreak.

Over the past 10 days, three earthquakes struck the Philippines affecting at least 2.3 million people. The first occurred on 30 September, in the Cebu province and was followed by hundreds of aftershocks. On 10 October, two back-to-back earthquakes hit Davao Oriental province. At least 20,000 people have been displaced and 85 killed due to the series of earthquakes.

Heavy rains have caused flooding in the Francisco Morazán department of Honduras. Over 31,000 people have been affected, 15 killed, and hundreds displaced. Additionally, flooding has completely isolated at least 66 communities.

Heavy rainfall caused severe flooding and landslides across eastern Mexico, affecting at least 370,000 people. Vercruz, Puebla, Hidalgo, San Luis Potosí, and Querétaro states were most affected. More flooding is expected in the coming days.

Heavy rainfall and rising water levels of the River Nile have caused flooding throughout six states in South Sudan. Over 287,000 people have been displaced and 886,000 affected, mostly in Jonglei and Unity states. Further flooding is likely.
Efforts to contain a diphtheria outbreak in Somalia have been undermined by vaccine shortages and aid cuts. Over 1,600 cases have been recorded in the last three months, leading to 57 deaths.

On 20 August, the health ministry of the DRC declared an Ebola outbreak in Bulape health zone of Kasai province in central DRC. Since then, at least 64 cases have been reported and 45 deaths. Women and girls account for over half of all cases and fatalities.

Non-state armed group activity has constrained food systems in Haiti, where more than 1 million people within Port au Prince, and the Artibonite, Ouest, and Centre departments face Emergency (IPC 4) levels of food insecurity. Further deterioration in levels of food insecurity is expected in coming months.

Between late July and 13 October 2025, non-State armed group attacks in Cabo Delgado triggered the displacement of over 167,000 people. A significant proportion of the newly are children and violence has also spilled over into the nearby Nampula province.

Amid extreme violence in El Fasher by the Rapid Support Forces (RSF), more than 100,000 civilians have been displaced to nearby areas in the past 90 days. The city has been besieged for nearly two years causing catastrophic levels of food insecurity among the affected population. Reports of the RSF gaining control of El Fasher (as well as reports of mass casualties, the loss of income earners in many families, and sexual violence) suggest that the humanitarian situation will worsen in the coming days.

The Rapid Support Forces (RSF) have launched a major offensive in North Kordofan state in recent weeks, resulting in the displacement of more than 40,000 people and violent attacks against the civilian population. The situation is expected to deteriorate in the coming weeks and months as the RSF redeploys fighters from North Darfur to North Kordofan.

Since early August, at least 1749 cholera cases have been reported in Bujumbura province of Burundi, with a spike in cases occuring through September and October. Most cases are in Cobitoke and Bujumbura Nord districts. Ongoing rains and floods, limited health system capacity, and movement of refugees and returnees through Burundi are expected to exacerbate the outbreak.

The security situation in the NW and SW regions of Cameroon is tense. Armed groups imposed a lock-down from Sep 8 to Oct 14, restricting movement for 4.5 million people. Even after the end of the lockdown, displacement due to armed clashes continues, and at least 4,500 people were displaced in Munyenge and Dumbo.

Hurricane Melissa, a category 5 storm, passed through Haiti on October 30, unleashing strong winds, rain, and flooding. More than 15,000 people in Haiti are displaced and 30 have been declared dead with 20 missing. The flooding from the storm may worsen the existing cholera outbreak, and has damaged crops, livestock, roads, and other infrastructure essential to people's livelihoods.

Sudan is facing massive food insecurity, with over 200,000 people experiencing catastrophic or IPC5 levels of food insecurity, and nearly 5 million people in emergency or IPC4 levels. Nearly half of the population is facing IPC 3 or higher food insecurity. This is largely driven by the ongoing conflict between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF). Please note, the conflict is now being classified separately from food insecurity to better track new developments.

In October, approximately 161,000 people were displaced from their homes in Kandahar, Afghanistan (primarily from the border city of Spin Boldak). The displacement was caused by Pakistani airstrikes and clashes between Afghan border forces and Pakistani troops.

In North Kivu, South Kivu, and Ituri in the eastern DRC, nearly 2.5 million people are facing emergency (IPC4) levels of food insecurity. Food insecurity was exacerbated by ongoing conflict between Rwandan-backed armed group, M23, and the Congolese armed forces in these regions, which have left millions displaced. Note that changes in the conflict will be classified separately.

South Sudan is facing high levels of food insecurity, with 27,856 people experiencing catastrophic or IPC5 levels of food insecurity, and more than 1.2 million people in emergency or IPC4 levels. Nearly half of the population is facing IPC 3 or higher food insecurity. This is driven largely by localized conflict, civil insecurity, and widespread flooding that has impacted agricultural lands and food production.

On November 4, Typhoon Kalmaegi passed over the Philippines with sustained winds of up to 130km/h. On November 9, Super Typhoon Fung-Wong impacted the Philippines as well, causing flooding and mass displacement. Nearly 20,000 houses were completely destroyed by the combined impacts of both storms.

Somalia is facing high levels of food insecurity, with 921,168 people experiencing IPC 4 (Emergency) levels of food insecurity. Approximately 15 percent of the population (4.3 million people) is experiencing IPC 3 or higher levels of food insecurity. This is driven largely by conflict and flooding in the south of Somalia as well as drought conditions that have affected crop yields in the north.

An outbreak of Marburg virus has been confirmed in the towns of Jinka and Hawassa in southern Ethiopia. Thus far nine cases have been reported in the outbreak, and six of the nine cases have died. The virus, which has clinical similarities to Ebola, has a fatality rate of around 50% and no vaccine or cure.

Heavy rainfall caused severe flooding and landslides across eastern Mexico, affecting at least 380,000 people and completely destroying 18,302 homes. Veracruz, Puebla, Hidalgo, San Luis Potosí, and Querétaro states were most affected.

The Rapid Support Forces (RSF) have launched a major offensive in the Kordofan states in recent weeks, resulting in the displacement of more than 200,000 people and violent attacks against the civilian population. The situation is expected to deteriorate in the coming weeks and months as the RSF redeploys fighters away from Darfur.

Heavy rainfall and rising water levels of the River Nile have caused flooding throughout eight states in South Sudan. Over 280,000 people have been displaced and over 1 million peple  affected, mostly in Jonglei and Unity states. Further flooding is likely.

Amid extreme violence in El Fasher by the Rapid Support Forces (RSF), over 100,000 civilians have been displaced to nearby areas since late October. The city has been besieged for nearly two years causing catastrophic levels of food insecurity among the affected population. Many civilians remain trapped in El Fasher with no access to aid or missing, and those who have managed to escape to safety have reported mass killings and sexual violence.

Heavy rains on the island of Sumatra in Indonesia caused massive flooding leading to widespread infrastructure and housing damage. Over 3500 houses were destroyed and the floods killed 744 people, with 551 people still missing.

Cyclone Ditwah impacted Sri Lanka on November 28, causing widespread flooding and devastating landslides. The cyclone and its impacts destroyed essential infrastructure and over 15,000 homes, killed 465, and 366 people remain missing. These numbers are expected to rise in the coming days.

Non-state armed group activity has constrained food systems in Haiti, where more than 1.8 million people (1 million people within Port au Prince, and the Artibonite, Ouest, and Centre departments) face Emergency (IPC 4) levels of food insecurity. Further deterioration in levels of food insecurity is expected in coming months.

In November 2025, a strong monsoon brought exceptionally heavy rainfall across southern Thailand triggering catastrophic flooding. Provinces particularly affected include Pattani, Yala, Narathiwat, and Songkhla. At least 4 million people were affected by the flooding, and over 200 killed.

Fighting between the Rwandan-backed armed group M23 and the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) has escalated in South Kivu, displacing over 400,000 people since September. Fighting has been heaviest in Uvira, Walungu, Fizi, and Mwenga territories, within South Kivu, in the eastern DRC.

Since early December, an escalation in conflict in South Kivu in the eastern DRC has displaced at least 42,000 people neighboringto Burundi. Please note, impacts of the conflict in the DRC are classified separately as CD490.

Fighting between the Rwandan-backed armed group M23 and the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) has escalated in South Kivu, displacing over 550,000 people since September. Fighting has been heaviest in Uvira, Walungu, Fizi, and Mwenga territories, within South Kivu, in the eastern DRC.

In early December, violence from both non-stated armed groups and the Nigerien armed forces displaced over 8,000 people in the Tillaberi region of Niger. Most have fled to communities near the border of Mali, which also face insecurity.

Sudan is facing massive food insecurity, with over 200,000 people experiencing catastrophic or IPC5 levels of food insecurity, and nearly 5 million people in emergency or IPC4 levels. Nearly half of the population is facing IPC 3 or higher food insecurity. This is driven by the ongoing conflict between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF). Please note, the conflict is now being classified separately from food insecurity to better track new developments.

South Sudan is facing high levels of food insecurity, with 27,856 people experiencing catastrophic or IPC5 levels of food insecurity, and more than 1.2 million people in emergency or IPC4 levels. Nearly half of the population is facing IPC 3 or higher food insecurity. This is driven largely by localized conflict, civil insecurity, and widespread flooding that has impacted agricultural lands and food production.

ver 4.6 million people are experiencing emergency, or IPC4, levels of food insecurity across Afghanistan. Food insecurity is driven by drought, which caused poor agricultural production and livestock losses, as well funding cuts to food assistance, and is compounded by natural hazards that further destroyed food stocks.

Somalia is facing high levels of food insecurity, with 921,168 people experiencing IPC 4 (Emergency) levels of food insecurity. Approximately 15 percent of the population (4.3 million people) is experiencing IPC 3 or higher levels of food insecurity. This is driven largely by conflict and flooding in the south of Somalia as well as drought conditions that have affected crop yields in the north.

Since late November, clashes by armed groups have displaced at least 15,000 in Gnagna province of Est region in Burkina Faso. People displaced are in need of food, protection, shelter, and WASH services.

Since December, clashes between government forces and the Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF) in Aleppo have displaced at least 140,000 people and killed 23 people. Most have been displaced to Afrin district, north of Aleppo.

The Rapid Support Forces (RSF) have launched a major offensive in the Kordofan states in recent weeks, resulting in the displacement of nearly 900,000 people and violent attacks against the civilian population. Furthermore, the RSF has besieged cities such as Dilling in South Kordofan, totally cutting off the population from humanitarian assistance.

Non-state armed group activity has constrained food systems in Haiti, where more than 1.8 million people (1 million people within Port au Prince, and the Artibonite, Ouest, and Centre departments) face Emergency (IPC 4) levels of food insecurity. Further deterioration is expected in the coming months

Iranian forces have killed over 2,500 people in a violent crackdown on protests that began in late December over economic conditions. Protests have escalated to a nationwide anti-government movement in all 31 provinces.  This classification will be updated with any future developments.

Amid extreme violence in El Fasher by the Rapid Support Forces (RSF), over 100,000 civilians have been displaced to nearby, with estimates of 60,000 people killed. The city has been besieged for nearly two years causing catastrophic levels of food insecurity among the affected population. Many civilians remain trapped in El Fasher with no access to aid or missing, and those who have managed to escape to safety have reported mass killings and sexual violence. Please note, this classification has increased due to updates to the classification system as well as new data that has been released.

Since early December, at least 90,000 people have been displaced from the eastern DRC to neighboring Burundi due to an escalation in conflict around South Kivu province. Please note, impacts of the conflict in the DRC are classified separately as CD490.

Since late October, over 80,000 people have been displaced to the Tera commune of Tillaberi region in Niger due to non-state armed group activity. Communities were forced to either join the non-state armed groups, or leave their community, prompting mass displacement.

Fighting between the Rwandan-backed armed group M23 and the Congolese armed forces (FARDC) has escalated in South Kivu, displacing over 1 million people since October. Fighting has been heaviest in Uvira, Walungu, Fizi, and Mwenga territories, within South Kivu, in the eastern DRC

Renewed fighting between the South Sudanese military (SSPDF) and their main opposition group, the Sudan's Peoples Liberation Army in Opposition (SPLA-iO), has displaced over 230,000 people in South Sudan's Jonglei state. Further clashes are expected as the SSPDF has ordered civilians and NGOs to evacuate opposition held territory in Jonglei as they prepare to retake the area.

Sudan is facing massive food insecurity, with over 140,000 people experiencing catastrophic or IPC5 levels of food insecurity, and nearly 5 million people in emergency or IPC4 levels. Nearly half of the population is facing IPC 3 or higher food insecurity. This is largely driven by the ongoing conflict between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF). Please note, the conflict is now being classified separately from food insecurity to better track new developments.

About 4.7 million people are experiencing emergency, or IPC Phase 4, levels of food insecurity across Afghanistan. Food insecurity is compounded by a weak, contracting economy, declining remittances following the return of over 2.5 million people from Iran and Pakistan in 2025, and recent earthquakes that have further strained food stocks and livelihoods.

One case of Nippah virus infection (NiV) has been confirmed in the Rajshahi district of northwestern Bangladesh. NiV is a disease transmitted to humans through infected animals, such as bats, or person to person through direct contact with bodily fluids. It can cause fevers, vomiting, and brain swelling, that can lead to death. The case fatality ratio for Nippah virus in Bangladesh is 72% and there is no treatment or vaccine.

Drought conditions have intensified across Somalia leading to widespread water scarcity, crop failure, livestock losses, and displacements. More than 4.6 million people have been affected by the drought, with the central regions of Galgaduud and Mudug among the worst hit.

Armed attacks in Bagaré province in Nord region of Burkina Faso displaced at least 7,000 people since late January. Most of those displaced went to Bagaré town, and are in need of shelter, food, and livelihood support after their homes, livestock, and crops were destroyed in the attacks.

Torrential rains, strong winds, and hailstorms have triggered landslides across several provinces in Burundi. A total of 9,466 people have been affected, including 1,953 newly displaced people, with 12 fatalities and 35 injuries.

Attacks by armed bandits throughout Northwest Nigeria have displaced at least 20,000 people and killed at least 20. Most attacks occurred in Zamfara and Sokoto states in the NW and resulted in water, shelter, food, and protection needs.

4.6 million affected by drought and nearly half a million displaced, as worsening conditions drive a near doubling of people facing acute food insecurity to 6.5 million, while acute malnutrition rises for a second consecutive year, with over 1.8 million children affected.

The US-led conflict against Iran has expanded to include airstrikes in Lebanon. Following the strikes on Iran, Hezbollah launched a series of missiles at Israel. In response, Israel is carrying out airstrikes across Lebanon that have displaced at least 25,000 people and killed at least 30. The situation is changing rapidly, and this classification will be updated accordingly.

US-led airstrikes and conflict in Iran have forced hundreds of Iranians to seek safety in neighboring Türkiye. As the conflict continues, more arrivals to Türkiye are likely, and some might take more dangerous routes through the mountains if the border crossing is closed. Impacts in Iran and other countries will be classified separately.

US and Israeli airstrikes on Iran have killed over 1,000 people and displaced at least 100,000 so far. Most of those displaced are from Tehran and are heading to the north of the country. There are likely more people displaced than currently reported, but information is limited due to the internet blackout in Iran. This classification will be updated as soon as more data is available. Please note, related impacts in other countries will be classified separately.

The US-led conflict against Iran has expanded to include airstrikes in Lebanon. Following the strikes on Iran, Hezbollah launched a series of missiles at Israel. In response, Israel is carrying out airstrikes across Lebanon that have displaced at least 60,000 people and killed at least 40. The situation is changing rapidly, and this classification will be updated accordingly.

The US-led conflict against Iran has expanded to include airstrikes in Lebanon. Following the strikes on Iran, Hezbollah launched a series of missiles at Israel. In response, Israel is carrying out airstrikes across Lebanon that have displaced at least 80,000 people and killed at least 70. The situation is changing rapidly, and this classification will be updated accordingly.

Clashes on the border between Pakistan and Afghanistan, starting late February, have displaced at least 160,000 people throughout the border regions of Afghanistan. At least 40 civilians have been killed by airstrikes with over 100 injured.
